topics.title: Authentication Protocols: Message Authentication Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Authentication Protocols: Message Authentication Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Message Authentication?

**Message Authentication** is a fundamental security service in network security that provides assurance about two critical aspects of a received message:

1. **Source Verification (Authenticity)**: The message genuinely originates from the claimed legitimate source.
2. **Integrity Verification (Unaltered)**: The message has not been modified, inserted, deleted, or replayed during transit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *"Message Authentication is the process of verifying the integrity and authenticity of a transmitted message, ensuring that the message is received exactly as sent, and that the sender's identity is genuine and verifiable."*

In the broader KTU 2024 syllabus context, message authentication is a **prerequisite security primitive** for almost all higher-level cryptographic protocols including TLS/SSL, IPsec, Kerberos, and digital signature schemes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you receive a sealed letter from your bank. Before acting on it, you need to be sure of two things:

1. **Did this letter really come from my bank?** (Authenticity)
2. **Was the content inside tampered with after they sealed it?** (Integrity)

To solve this, the bank uses a special wax seal with a unique crest (analogous to a **Message Authentication Code (MAC)**) and a private signature inside (analogous to a **digital signature**). If the seal is broken or the signature looks wrong, you reject the letter immediately.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy — The Courier Service:**
> When Amazon delivers a package to you, they use:
> - **Tamper-evident packaging** (like a MAC) — if opened, it's visible.
> - **Tracking ID verified by sender** (like a digital signature) — Amazon's server confirms it sent it.
> - **One-time delivery code** (like a nonce) — ensures the package isn't intercepted and replayed.
>
> This is exactly what message authentication protocols achieve in digital networks, but with mathematical guarantees instead of physical seals.

## 1.3 Key Categories of Authentication in KTU Syllabus

| **Authentication Type** | **What it Verifies** | **Typical Use Case** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Data-Origin Authentication** | The data came from a specific claimed source | Email signatures, file integrity |
| **Peer Entity Authentication** | The identity of a communicating party (user/system) at connection time | Login systems, VPN handshakes |
| **Message Authentication** | Both source AND content integrity of a message | TLS records, secure transactions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Distinction:**
> In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Exam), questions frequently test the difference between **Message Authentication** and **Entity Authentication**. Message authentication is *per-message*, while entity authentication is *per-session*.

## 1.4 The Four Canonical Requirements of Message Authentication

According to Stallings (the standard reference adapted in KTU syllabus), message authentication must address:

1. **Confidentiality** — Disclosure protection
2. **Integrity** — Alteration detection
3. **Authentication** — Source verification
4. **Non-Repudiation** — Sender cannot later deny sending

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the **Message Authentication Requirements** under this topic. The above four are the **core requirements** you must remember. We will formally define each in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Message Authentication?

In any open network (especially the Internet), messages pass through multiple untrusted routers, switches, and intermediate nodes. Without authentication, an attacker can:

- **Intercept** messages (loss of confidentiality)
- **Modify** messages in transit (loss of integrity)
- **Impersonate** a legitimate sender (loss of authentication)
- **Replay** old messages to trick the receiver (loss of freshness)
- **Repudiate** having sent a message (loss of non-repudiation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Authentication is what makes protocols like HTTPS, SSH, and IPsec trustworthy. Without it, secure communication over the Internet would be impossible.

## 2.2 The Four Message Authentication Requirements — Deep Dive

### Requirement 1: **Disclosure Protection (Confidentiality)**

The message content must be kept secret from any party other than the intended recipient. This is typically achieved through **encryption** (symmetric key like AES, or asymmetric key like RSA).

- **Mathematical Form:** Given plaintext $M$ and key $K$, ciphertext $C = E_K(M)$.
- **Confidentiality without authentication is incomplete**: encryption alone doesn't prove who sent the message.

### Requirement 2: **Integrity (Message Tamper Detection)**

The receiver must be able to detect **any modification** — bit flips, insertion, deletion, or reordering — that occurred in transit. Techniques include:

- **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)**
- **Cryptographic Hash Functions** (SHA-256, SHA-3)
- **Digital Signatures** (with hash + private key)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** Integrity is a *necessary* but *not sufficient* condition for authentication. You can have integrity (using a checksum) without knowing *who* sent the message.

### Requirement 3: **Authentication (Source Verification)**

The receiver must be able to verify the **identity of the source**. This can be achieved via:

- **Symmetric MAC** (both parties share a secret key $K$)
- **Digital Signature** using sender's private key $K_{priv}$
- **Challenge-Response** protocol with nonces

### Requirement 4: **Non-Repudiation**

The sender must **not be able to deny** having sent the message later. This is the property that distinguishes digital signatures from MACs:

- **MACs** do **NOT** provide non-repudiation (both parties share the key, so either could have produced the MAC).
- **Digital Signatures** **DO** provide non-repudiation (only the sender has the private key).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** This distinction is a *favourite KTU question*. Memorize: "MAC = authentication + integrity, but NO non-repudiation. Digital Signature = authentication + integrity + non-repudiation."

## 2.3 Threat Model — What Attacks Does Message Authentication Defend Against?

| **Attack Type** | **Description** | **Defense Mechanism** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Disclosure** | Eavesdropper reads message | Encryption (AES, RSA) |
| **Traffic Analysis** | Inferring info from message flow | Padding, dummy traffic |
| **Masquerade** | Attacker pretends to be legitimate sender | Authentication (MAC, Signature) |
| **Content Modification** | Tampering with message contents | MAC, Digital Signature, Hash |
| **Sequence Modification** | Reordering messages | Sequence numbers, timestamps |
| **Timing Modification** | Delaying/replaying old messages | Timestamps, nonces |
| **Repudiation (Source)** | Sender denies sending | Digital Signature (asymmetric) |
| **Repudiation (Destination)** | Receiver denies receiving | Acknowledgement + audit logs |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **mathematical primitives** used in message authentication. These are the symbols and equations you must know for KTU ESE.

| **Symbol / Equation** | **Meaning** | **Use Case** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $M$ | Original plaintext message | Input to authentication function |
| $h(M)$ | Hash of message $M$ | Used in digital signatures (output length = $n$ bits) |
| $\text{MAC}_K(M)$ | Message Authentication Code using shared key $K$ | Fixed-length tag appended to $M$ |
| $E_K(M)$ | Encryption of $M$ with key $K$ | Confidentiality |
| $S_{K_{priv}}(M)$ | Signature on $M$ with sender's private key | Non-repudiation |
| $V_{K_{pub}}(S)$ | Verification of signature $S$ with public key | Verifier checks authenticity |
| $C = E_K(M \,\|\, h(M))$ | Encrypt + authenticate (combined) | Provides confidentiality + integrity |
| $C = E_K(M) \,\|\, \text{MAC}_K(M)$ | Encrypt then MAC (EtM) | Most secure composition pattern |
| $H(\cdot)$ | Cryptographic hash function | SHA-256, SHA-3, BLAKE2 |
| $N$ or $R$ | Nonce (Number used once) | Prevents replay attacks |

**Key Properties of a Secure Hash Function $H(\cdot)$:**

1. **Pre-image resistance:** Given $h$, hard to find $M$ such that $H(M) = h$.
2. **Second pre-image resistance:** Given $M_1$, hard to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
3. **Collision resistance:** Hard to find any $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "List the requirements of a cryptographic hash function," write all three properties above. You will get full marks only if all three are stated.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| **Domain** | **Authentication Mechanism Used** |
|:---|:---|
| HTTPS / TLS 1.3 | HMAC-SHA256, AES-GCM (AEAD) |
| SSH | HMAC, Diffie-Hellman key exchange |
| IPsec (VPN) | HMAC-MD5, HMAC-SHA1/SHA256 |
| Bitcoin / Blockchain | SHA-256 double hash + ECDSA signatures |
| DNS Security (DNSSEC) | RSA / ECDSA digital signatures |
| JWT (Web Tokens) | HMAC-SHA256 (HS256) or RSA signatures |
| 5G Authentication | AKMA (Authentication and Key Management for Applications) using HMAC |

> [!NOTE]
> The pattern is consistent: every modern protocol uses **some combination of hash + MAC + signature** to satisfy the four message authentication requirements.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why MAC Provides Authentication but NOT Non-Repudiation

Let us rigorously prove why a symmetric MAC cannot provide non-repudiation, while an asymmetric digital signature can.

### 3.1.1 MAC Scenario

Let Alice and Bob share a secret key $K_{AB}$.

- Alice computes the tag: $T_A = \text{MAC}_{K_{AB}}(M)$.
- Alice sends Bob the pair $(M, T_A)$.
- Bob verifies: $\text{MAC}_{K_{AB}}(M) \stackrel{?}{=} T_A$.

**Analysis:** Since both Alice and Bob know $K_{AB}$, Bob himself could have produced $T_A$. Therefore, Bob cannot prove to a third party (e.g., a judge) that Alice was the one who sent $M$.

$$
\Pr[\text{Judge accepts Bob's claim that Alice sent } M] = \Pr[\text{Bob forged } T_A \text{ himself}]
$$

$$
\Pr[\text{Bob forged}] = 1 \quad \text{(Bob knows } K_{AB} \text{)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Therefore, MAC fails the non-repudiation property.** This is a structural limitation of symmetric cryptography — *any* party with the shared key can create a valid tag.

### 3.1.2 Digital Signature Scenario

Now, let Alice have a private key $K_{priv,A}$ and public key $K_{pub,A}$.

- Alice signs: $\sigma = S_{K_{priv,A}}(H(M))$.
- Alice sends $(M, \sigma)$.
- Bob verifies: $V_{K_{pub,A}}(\sigma) \stackrel{?}{=} H(M)$.

**Analysis:** Bob does NOT know $K_{priv,A}$, so he cannot forge $\sigma$. Only Alice could have produced a signature that verifies under her public key.

$$
\Pr[\text{Bob forged } \sigma] = \Pr[\text{Bob inverts ECDSA / RSA}] < 2^{-128} \text{ (negligible)}
$$

$$
\Pr[\text{Judge accepts Bob's claim that Alice sent } M] \approx 1
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\text{MAC: } \text{Auth} + \text{Integrity}, \quad \text{No Non-Repudiation}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Digital Signature: } \text{Auth} + \text{Integrity} + \text{Non-Repudiation}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Composition Patterns — MAC-then-Encrypt vs Encrypt-then-MAC

A subtle but KTU-relevant question: **In what order should we apply MAC and Encryption?**

### 3.2.1 Method 1: MAC-then-Encrypt (MtE)

Alice computes:

$$
C = E_K(M \,\|\, \text{MAC}_{K'}(M))
$$

Receiver decrypts first:

$$
M \,\|\, T = D_K(C)
$$

Then verifies:

$$
T \stackrel{?}{=} \text{MAC}_{K'}(M)
$$

**Problem:** If the MAC is incorrect, the receiver must reveal an error — but this leaks information (an attacker can use this as an oracle). TLS 1.2 (CBC mode) used this and was vulnerable to padding oracle attacks.

### 3.2.2 Method 2: Encrypt-then-MAC (EtM) — **Recommended**

Alice computes:

$$
C = E_K(M) \,\|\, \text{MAC}_{K'}(E_K(M))
$$

Receiver first verifies the MAC on $C$:

$$
T' \stackrel{?}{=} \text{MAC}_{K'}(C)
$$

If valid, then decrypts:

$$
M = D_K(C)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is EtM better?**
> - The receiver never decrypts an unverified ciphertext.
> - No padding oracle attack surface.
> - **Used by IPsec and TLS 1.3.**

### 3.2.3 Method 3: Encrypt-and-MAC (E&M)

$$
C = E_K(M) \,\|\, \text{MAC}_{K'}(M)
$$

Used in SSH. It authenticates the plaintext, not the ciphertext — so an attacker could tamper with $C$ and the receiver would not detect it (since MAC is on $M$, not on $C$).

### 3.2.4 Summary Table

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Method} & \textbf{Computation} & \textbf{Verifies} \\
\hline
\text{MAC-then-Encrypt} & E_K(M \,\|\, \text{MAC}_{K'}(M)) & \text{After decryption} \\
\hline
\text{Encrypt-then-MAC} & E_K(M) \,\|\, \text{MAC}_{K'}(E_K(M)) & \text{Before decryption} \\
\hline
\text{Encrypt-and-MAC} & E_K(M) \,\|\, \text{MAC}_{K'}(M) & \text{Plaintext only} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Question Pattern:** "Compare the three composition methods and identify which is most secure." — Answer: **Encrypt-then-MAC**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Security Bound for a Forgery Attack on MAC

Let us formalize the **unforgeability under chosen-message attack (UF-CMA)** property of a secure MAC.

**Setup:**

- Attacker makes $q$ queries to a MAC oracle (asks for tags on chosen messages $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_q$).
- Attacker outputs a candidate forgery $(M^*, T^*)$ where $M^* \notin \{M_1, \ldots, M_q\}$.

**Advantage of attacker:**

$$
\text{Adv}_{\text{MAC}}^{\text{UF-CMA}}(\mathcal{A}) = \Pr[\mathcal{A} \text{ forges}] \leq \epsilon
$$

For a secure MAC with $n$-bit tag:

$$
\boxed{\text{Adv}_{\text{MAC}}^{\text{UF-CMA}}(\mathcal{A}) \leq \dfrac{q}{2^n} + \epsilon_{\text{PRF}}}
$$

where $\epsilon_{\text{PRF}}$ is the advantage in breaking the underlying Pseudo-Random Function (PRF).

**Numerical example (KTU exam-style):**

If the MAC uses SHA-256 (output = 256 bits) and the attacker makes $q = 2^{64}$ queries, the forgery probability is:

$$
\Pr[\text{Forgery}] = \frac{2^{64}}{2^{256}} = 2^{-192}
$$

This is **negligibly small**, demonstrating the security of the MAC.

$$
\boxed{2^{-192} \approx 0 \text{ (practically zero)}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Authentication Function Comparison

| **Criterion** | **Message Encryption** | **MAC** | **Hash Function** | **Digital Signature** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Confidentiality** | Yes | No | No | No |
| **Integrity** | Sometimes (if deterministic) | Yes | Yes (if used with key) | Yes |
| **Authentication** | Weak (anyone with key) | Yes | No (unkeyed) | Yes (strong) |
| **Non-Repudiation** | No | No | No | Yes |
| **Key Type** | Symmetric | Symmetric | None (or keyed HMAC) | Asymmetric |
| **Speed** | Fast | Fast | Very Fast | Slower |
| **Output Size** | Variable (ciphertext) | Fixed (e.g., 256 bits) | Fixed (e.g., 256 bits) | Variable (signature size) |
| **Replay Protection** | No (alone) | With nonce | No (alone) | With nonce/timestamp |
| **Example Algorithm** | AES-256 | HMAC-SHA256 | SHA-256, SHA-3 | RSA-2048, ECDSA-P256 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Message Authentication Process

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end message authentication flow** between Sender (Alice) and Receiver (Bob), incorporating all four requirements.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Message M] --> B[Step 1: Compute Hash H of M]
    B --> C[Step 2: Apply Secret Key to Hash HMAC or Sign with Private Key]
    C --> D[Step 3: Generate Authentication Tag T]
    A --> E[Step 4: Concatenate M with T to form Authenticated Message]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Step 5: Transmit over Untrusted Network]
    F --> G[Step 6: Receiver Receives Authenticated Message]
    G --> H[Step 7: Receiver Recomputes Tag using Shared Key or Public Key]
    H --> I[Step 8: Compare Recomputed Tag with Received Tag]
    I --> J{Tags Match?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Step 9a: Accept Message Integrity and Authenticity Verified]
    J -->|No| L[Step 9b: Reject Message Possible Tampering or Forgery Detected]
```

## 4.2 Architecture of Message Authentication Functions

This block diagram shows the **three primary mechanisms** used to implement message authentication.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message Authentication Function] --> B[Mechanism 1: Message Encryption]
    A --> C[Mechanism 2: Message Authentication Code MAC]
    A --> D[Mechanism 3: Cryptographic Hash Function]
    B --> B1[Symmetric Encryption AES, 3DES]
    B --> B2[Asymmetric Encryption RSA, ECC]
    C --> C1[Keyed Hash HMAC SHA256]
    C --> C2[Cipher Based MAC CMAC, GMAC]
    D --> D1[Unkeyed Hash MD5, SHA1, SHA2]
    D --> D2[Modern Hash SHA3, BLAKE2]
    C1 --> E[Produces Fixed Length Tag]
    C2 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Verified at Receiver End]
```

## 4.3 Attack-Defense Mapping Diagram

The following diagram maps each **authentication attack** to its **corresponding defense mechanism**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Disclosure Attack] --> D1[Defense: Symmetric Encryption AES]
    A2[Content Modification Attack] --> D2[Defense: MAC or Hash Function]
    A3[Masquerade or Spoofing Attack] --> D3[Defense: Digital Signature or MAC]
    A4[Replay Attack] --> D4[Defense: Nonce or Timestamp]
    A5[Repudiation Attack] --> D5[Defense: Digital Signature with Private Key]
    A6[Sequence Modification Attack] --> D6[Defense: Sequence Numbers]
    A1 --> R[Result: Confidentiality Achieved]
    A2 --> R
    A3 --> R
    A4 --> R
    A5 --> R
    A6 --> R
    R --> S[All Four Authentication Requirements Satisfied]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Authenticated Communication System

The following diagram shows the **functional architecture** of a complete authenticated communication system, mapping data flow between layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Layer Plaintext Message] --> B[Authentication Module]
    B --> C{HMAC or Signature}
    C -->|HMAC Path| D1[Compute HMAC SHA256 with Shared Secret Key K]
    C -->|Signature Path| D2[Sign Hash with Private Key RSA or ECDSA]
    D1 --> E1[Authentication Tag T1 generated]
    D2 --> E2[Digital Signature S generated]
    A --> F[Encryption Module AES 256 GCM]
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Authenticated and Encrypted Ciphertext]
    G --> H[Network Transmission Channel]
    H --> I[Receiver Side Verification Module]
    I --> J{Decryption}
    J --> K[Recover Original Message M]
    K --> L[Recompute Tag using Shared Key or Public Key]
    L --> M{Compare Computed Tag with Received Tag}
    M -->|Match| N[Accept Message Authenticated and Untampered]
    M -->|Mismatch| O[Reject Message Integrity Violation Detected]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Exam:**
> When drawing such architecture diagrams in the exam, label **every block clearly** and **annotate the keys used** (e.g., "Shared Secret K" or "Private Key of Alice"). Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the diagram in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define message authentication. List the four essential requirements that a secure message authentication mechanism must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Message authentication is the process of verifying that a received message originates from the claimed source (authenticity) and has not been altered during transmission (integrity).

**The Four Requirements:**

1. **Confidentiality (Disclosure Protection):** The message must be kept secret from all unauthorized parties.
2. **Integrity:** Any modification of the message contents, sequence, or timing must be detectable by the receiver.
3. **Authentication (Source Verification):** The receiver must be able to confirm the identity of the sender.
4. **Non-Repudiation:** The sender must not be able to deny having sent the message at a later stage.

> [!NOTE]
> *End of Answer (3 marks awarded for definition + 4 requirements listed.)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between a Message Authentication Code (MAC) and a Digital Signature with respect to non-repudiation.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Property** | **MAC** | **Digital Signature** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Key Type | Symmetric (shared secret $K$) | Asymmetric (private/public key pair) |
| Non-Repudiation | **Not provided** (both parties share the key, so either could have created the tag) | **Provided** (only the sender has the private key) |
| Verification | Receiver uses same shared key $K$ | Anyone with the public key can verify |
| Speed | Faster (uses symmetric primitives) | Slower (uses asymmetric primitives like RSA/ECDSA) |
| Use Case | Internal, trusted networks, TLS records | Legal documents, certificates, software signing |

> [!NOTE]
> *End of Answer (3 marks awarded for clear differentiation in a tabular form.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU ESE 2024 Scheme, Part B Module 1 questions are of **14 marks** and provide an **internal choice** between two full-length questions. Both options are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the four fundamental requirements of message authentication in detail, with one real-world example for each requirement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working of a Message Authentication Code (MAC). With a neat diagram, explain how MAC provides both data integrity and authentication. Discuss why MAC alone does not provide non-repudiation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Requirement 1 — Confidentiality (Disclosure Protection):**
The message content must be hidden from eavesdroppers. Example: When you log in to your bank website via HTTPS, your password is encrypted using AES-256, so even if an attacker intercepts the traffic, they cannot read your password.

**Requirement 2 — Integrity (Tamper Detection):**
The receiver must detect any modification of the message. Example: When you download software from the internet, the SHA-256 hash shown on the website allows you to verify that the file was not modified by a man-in-the-middle attacker.

**Requirement 3 — Authentication (Source Verification):**
The receiver must confirm the identity of the sender. Example: When your email client shows a "signed by" indicator, it means the sender's digital signature was verified using their public key from a trusted CA (Certificate Authority).

**Requirement 4 — Non-Repudiation:**
The sender cannot later deny having sent the message. Example: In a legally binding e-contract, the sender's digital signature provides cryptographic proof that only the sender could have produced, which is admissible in court.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Working of a MAC:**

A Message Authentication Code is a fixed-size tag (e.g., 256 bits) generated from a message $M$ and a shared secret key $K$ between sender and receiver.

**Step-by-Step Process:**

1. **Sender side:** Alice computes $T = \text{MAC}_K(M)$ using the shared key $K$.
2. **Transmission:** Alice sends $(M, T)$ to Bob over the network.
3. **Receiver side:** Bob independently computes $T' = \text{MAC}_K(M)$ using the same shared key $K$.
4. **Verification:** Bob checks if $T' \stackrel{?}{=} T$.
5. **Decision:**
   - If $T' = T$: Message is authentic and unaltered. **Accept.**
   - If $T' \neq T$: Either the message was tampered with, OR the sender does not know the correct key $K$. **Reject.**

**Diagram:**

```
+------------------+         +------------------+
|  Alice (Sender)  |         |  Bob (Receiver)  |
+------------------+         +------------------+
        |                            |
        |  T = MAC_K(M)              |
        |----------- M || T -------->|
        |                            |
        |                  T' = MAC_K(M_received)
        |                            |
        |                  T' == T ?
        |                  [YES] -> Accept
        |                  [NO]  -> Reject
```

**Why MAC Does Not Provide Non-Repudiation:**

Since the key $K$ is shared between Alice and Bob, **Bob himself could have generated the tag $T$**. Therefore, Bob cannot prove to a third party (e.g., a judge) that it was Alice — and not Bob — who created the message. Non-repudiation requires a mechanism where only the sender has the cryptographic capability to produce the tag, which is achieved by **digital signatures** using asymmetric cryptography.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in KTU Valuation:**
> Students often confuse the verification of MAC with the verification of a digital signature. **Remember**: MAC verification is done with the *same* shared key (symmetric), while digital signature verification is done with the *public* key (asymmetric). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**Q: (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the three approaches to message authentication: (i) Message Encryption, (ii) Message Authentication Code, and (iii) Hash Function. Compare their strengths and weaknesses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a digital signature? Explain the digital signature generation and verification process using RSA. Discuss how digital signatures satisfy all four message authentication requirements. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Three Approaches to Message Authentication:**

**Approach 1 — Message Encryption:**
The entire message is encrypted with a key. The receiver decrypts to recover the plaintext.

```
Sender:                          Receiver:
M (plaintext)                    C (ciphertext)
   |                                |
   v                                v
E_K(M) ---> C (ciphertext) --> D_K(C) = M
```

- **Strength:** Provides confidentiality + integrity (if algorithm is randomized).
- **Weakness:** Slow; no separation between authentication and confidentiality; cannot be used by third parties for verification.

**Approach 2 — Message Authentication Code (MAC):**
A fixed-length tag is generated using a secret key and appended to the plaintext message.

```
Sender:                          Receiver:
M (plaintext)                    M || T
   |                                |
   v                                v
T = MAC_K(M)               T' = MAC_K(M)
   |                                |
   +----------- M || T ------------>+
                                    v
                            T' == T ? Yes/No
```

- **Strength:** Fast, provides authentication + integrity.
- **Weakness:** No non-repudiation; shared key must be distributed securely.

**Approach 3 — Hash Function:**
A hash of the message is computed and transmitted alongside the message. The receiver re-hashes and compares.

```
Sender:                          Receiver:
M (plaintext)                    M || H(M)
   |                                |
   v                                v
H(M)                H'(M) = Hash(M)
   |                                |
   +--------- M || H(M) ----------->+
                                    v
                            H(M) == H'(M) ? Yes/No
```

- **Strength:** Fast, simple, no key needed (for unkeyed hash).
- **Weakness:** No authentication (unkeyed hash); vulnerable to man-in-the-middle substitution of $H(M)$.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Criterion** | **Encryption** | **MAC** | **Hash** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Confidentiality | Yes | No | No |
| Integrity | Yes | Yes | Yes (with key) |
| Authentication | Weak | Yes | No (unkeyed) |
| Key Required | Yes (symmetric) | Yes (symmetric) | No (or HMAC) |
| Speed | Moderate | Fast | Very Fast |
| Non-Repudiation | No | No | No |

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Digital Signature Definition:**
A digital signature is a cryptographic mechanism that uses asymmetric key pairs (private and public) to provide authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation of a message.

**RSA Digital Signature Process:**

**Key Generation:**
- Choose two large primes $p$ and $q$.
- Compute $n = p \cdot q$.
- Choose public exponent $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ where $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- Compute private exponent $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$.
- **Public key:** $(e, n)$.
- **Private key:** $(d, n)$.

**Signature Generation (by Alice):**

Step 1: Compute hash of the message:

$$
h = H(M)
$$

Step 2: Sign the hash using Alice's private key:

$$
\sigma = h^d \bmod n
$$

Step 3: Send $(M, \sigma)$ to Bob.

**Signature Verification (by Bob):**

Step 1: Compute hash of the received message:

$$
h' = H(M)
$$

Step 2: Verify using Alice's public key:

$$
\sigma^e \bmod n \stackrel{?}{=} h'
$$

Step 3: If the equality holds, the signature is valid.

**How Digital Signatures Satisfy All Four Requirements:**

1. **Confidentiality:** Not directly provided. (Confidentiality is achieved by combining with encryption, e.g., signed-and-encrypted email.)
2. **Integrity:** If the message $M$ is altered, the hash $H(M)$ changes, and $\sigma^e \bmod n \neq H(M_{\text{modified}})$, so the signature verification fails.
3. **Authentication:** Only the sender knows the private key $d$, so only the sender could have produced a valid signature. Verification with the public key proves the sender's identity.
4. **Non-Repudiation:** Since the private key $d$ is known only to Alice, Alice cannot deny having produced the signature. A third party (e.g., a judge) can verify the signature using Alice's public key, providing cryptographic proof of origin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Do **NOT** write the signature algorithm as $S = M^d \bmod n$. You **MUST** first hash the message: $S = H(M)^d \bmod n$. This is a 2-mark deduction point.
> 2. In the verification step, students often forget to **re-hash** the message before comparing. The correct sequence is: hash → exponentiate signature → compare hashes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist as your final 5-minute revision before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ Message authentication = verifying **authenticity** + **integrity** of a message.
- ✅ The **four requirements** are: Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation.
- ✅ **MAC** = keyed hash using a shared secret key. Provides Auth + Integrity, **NO non-repudiation**.
- ✅ **Digital Signature** = hashed message signed with private key. Provides Auth + Integrity + **Non-Repudiation**.
- ✅ **Hash Function** properties: Pre-image resistance, Second pre-image resistance, Collision resistance.
- ✅ **HMAC** = Hashed MAC; uses a shared secret key with a hash function (e.g., HMAC-SHA256).
- ✅ **Encrypt-then-MAC (EtM)** is the most secure composition pattern (used in IPsec, TLS 1.3).
- ✅ **Nonce / Timestamp** is required to prevent **replay attacks** in any authentication protocol.
- ✅ **Replay attack**: an attacker re-sends a previously captured valid message to trick the receiver.
- ✅ **Digital signature with RSA** requires: $S = H(M)^d \bmod n$ for signing, and $S^e \bmod n \stackrel{?}{=} H(M)$ for verification.
- ✅ **Unkeyed hash alone** (e.g., plain SHA-256) does **NOT** provide authentication — only integrity.
- ✅ **Kerberos, TLS, IPsec, SSH** all use MAC + digital signature combinations internally.
- ✅ **HMAC security bound**: forgery probability $\leq \frac{q}{2^n}$ for $n$-bit output and $q$ oracle queries.
- ✅ Remember: **Message Authentication** is *per-message*; **Entity Authentication** is *per-session*.
- ✅ In KTU 14-mark questions, always include a **diagram** and **at least 2 examples** from real-world protocols.
- ✅ When asked to "compare MAC and digital signature," structure your answer as a **table** for clarity and full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authentication Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Authentication Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 Authentication Functions — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authentication** is the security service that provides **proof of identity** of an entity (user, system, process) or the **proof of data origin/integrity** of a message transmitted over an insecure channel. It guarantees that the message was indeed generated by the claimed sender and was not altered in transit.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, an **Authentication Function** refers to the mathematical/cryptographic primitive (Hash, MAC, Digital Signature, HMAC) used to verify:
- **Data origin authentication** — *Who sent this message?*
- **Data integrity** — *Was the message tampered with?*
- **Non-repudiation** — *The sender cannot later deny sending it.*

**Authentication ≠ Authorization**
> [!NOTE]
> - **Authentication (AuthN)** → "Are you really who you claim to be?" (Verifying identity)
> - **Authorization (AuthZ)** → "Are you allowed to do this?" (Verifying rights)
> - These are often confused; the KTU examiner may use this as a trick question.

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Sealed Envelope" Concept

Imagine you post a handwritten letter to a friend:

| Real-World Object | Security Function |
|---|---|
| Your **handwritten signature** at the bottom | **Digital Signature** — proves authorship |
| A **unique wax seal** on the envelope | **MAC / Hash** — detects tampering |
| Sending a **photocopy of your ID card** along | **Entity Authentication** — proves identity |
| The **sealed opaque envelope** itself | **Confidentiality (Encryption)** |
| A **registered post receipt** with timestamp | **Non-repudiation + Audit trail** |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic: "A-I-N-C"** — Authentication answers **A**uthenticity, **I**ntegrity, **N**on-repudiation, and **C**ontent-binding. KTU loves this acronym.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Authentication Functions

Authentication functions are broadly classified into **three hierarchical levels** based on the cryptographic strength:

### Level 1: Message Encryption (Implicit Authentication)
The ciphertext itself serves as proof. If Bob can decrypt a message using a key only Alice possesses, then Alice must have sent it.

### Level 2: Message Authentication Codes (MAC)
A **fixed-size tag** is appended to the message using a **shared secret key**. The receiver recomputes the tag and compares.

### Level 3: Hash Functions & Digital Signatures
- **Hash Function** → One-way, collision-resistant fingerprint.
- **Digital Signature** → Public-key analogue of a MAC; provides **non-repudiation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction Table:**
>
> | Property | MAC | Digital Signature |
> |---|---|---|
> | Key Type | **Symmetric** (shared) | **Asymmetric** (private/public) |
> | Non-repudiation | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
> | Speed | **Fast** (hardware friendly) | **Slower** (modular exponentiation) |
> | Verifier | Only key holder | **Anyone** with public key |

---

## 1.4 Generic Structure of an Authentication Function

For a message $M$ and key $K$, an authentication function $F$ produces a tag $T$:

$$T = F(K, M)$$

The receiver performs verification:

$$\text{Verify}(M, T, K) = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT} & \text{if } T = F(K, M) \\ \text{REJECT} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The **security strength** of $F$ is measured by the work factor $W$ (in bits) — i.e., the minimum number of operations an attacker must perform to forge a valid tag. KTU's benchmark is **$W \geq 2^{80}$** for long-term security.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Authentication

Authentication functions are evaluated against **three security services** (William Stallings, Network Security Cryptography):

1. **Message Authentication / Data Integrity** — confirms message was not modified.
2. **Entity Authentication** — confirms identity of the participant in a real-time session.
3. **Non-Repudiation** — prevents the sender from denying the message later.

---

## 2.2 Hash Functions — The Workhorse of Authentication

### 2.2.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Hash Function** $H$ is a deterministic mathematical function that maps an input $M$ of arbitrary length to a fixed-length output $h$ called the **message digest** or **hash value**:
> $$H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$$
> where $n$ is typically **128, 160, 256, 384, or 512 bits**.

### 2.2.2 Three Mandatory Cryptographic Properties

A cryptographically secure hash function $H(x)$ must satisfy:

1. **Pre-image Resistance (One-Wayness):**
   Given $h = H(x)$, it is computationally infeasible to find any $x'$ such that $H(x') = h$.
   Work factor: $W = \mathcal{O}(2^n)$.

2. **Second Pre-image Resistance (Weak Collision Resistance):**
   Given $x_1$, it is infeasible to find $x_2 \neq x_1$ such that $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$.
   Work factor: $W = \mathcal{O}(2^n)$.

3. **Collision Resistance (Strong Collision Resistance):**
   It is infeasible to find *any* two distinct inputs $x_1 \neq x_2$ such that $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$.
   Work factor: $W = \mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ by the **Birthday Paradox**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap Question:** "Why is collision resistance weaker than pre-image resistance?"
> → Because of the **Birthday Paradox**, an attacker only needs to try $\sqrt{2^n} = 2^{n/2}$ values to find *any* collision, instead of $2^n$ for a specific pre-image. This is why **MD5 (128-bit)** is broken (collision found in 2004) and **SHA-1 (160-bit)** is deprecated (SHAttered attack, 2017).

### 2.2.3 Birthday Paradox — The Magic of $\sqrt{N}$

The probability of finding at least one collision after trying $k$ random inputs into a hash with $N$ possible outputs is approximately:

$$P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)}$$

Setting $P = 0.5$ and solving for $k$:

$$k \approx 1.177 \sqrt{N}$$

This is why an $n$-bit hash offers only **$n/2$ bits of collision resistance**.

---

## 2.3 Message Authentication Code (MAC) — Symmetric Authentication

A MAC ensures both **data integrity** and **data origin authentication** using a **shared secret key**.

$$T = \text{MAC}(K, M)$$

The receiver computes $T' = \text{MAC}(K, M')$ and accepts if $T = T'$.

### 2.3.1 MAC Properties (KTU Must-Know)
- **Compression:** Maps arbitrary-length $M$ to fixed-length $T$.
- **Ease of computation:** Given $K$ and $M$, MAC is easy.
- **Key-dependent forgery resistance:** Without $K$, forgery is computationally infeasible.

### 2.3.2 HMAC — The Industry Standard (RFC 2104)

**HMAC (Hash-based MAC)** wraps any iterative hash function (MD5, SHA-256) into a MAC.

**HMAC Construction:**

$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\Big( (K \oplus opad) \;\Vert\; H\big( (K \oplus ipad) \;\Vert\; M \big) \Big)$$

where:
- $opad$ = **Outer pad** = $\texttt{0x5C}$ repeated $B$ times (block size of hash)
- $ipad$ = **Inner pad** = $\texttt{0x36}$ repeated $B$ times
- $K$ is padded/hashed to block size $B$ if shorter

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two nested hashes?** The nested structure (a "hash of a hash") is called a **Merkle-Damgård strengthening**. It protects against **length-extension attacks**, a known vulnerability in raw SHA-256 hashing. KTU questions often ask this directly.

---

## 2.4 Digital Signatures — Asymmetric Authentication

A digital signature is the public-key equivalent of a MAC, providing **non-repudiation**.

**Two-step process:**

1. **Signing:** Alice computes $S = \text{Sign}(K_{priv}^A, H(M))$.
2. **Verification:** Bob checks $\text{Verify}(K_{pub}^A, H(M), S) = \text{True}$.

**Algorithms:** RSA, DSA, ECDSA, EdDSA (used in TLS 1.3, Bitcoin).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of KTU numerical/derivation questions on Module 1.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hash digest length | $n$ | **128 / 160 / 256 / 512 bits** |
| 2 | Pre-image work factor | $W = 2^n$ | For SHA-256: $2^{256}$ |
| 3 | Collision work factor | $W = 2^{n/2}$ | For SHA-256: $2^{128}$ |
| 4 | Birthday collision count | $k \approx 1.177 \sqrt{N}$ | $N = 2^n$ |
| 5 | HMAC security | $\min(2^n, 2^L)$ | $L$ = key length |
| 6 | RSA signature size | $\vert S \vert = \vert N \vert$ | 1024 / 2048 / 4096 bits |
| 7 | HMAC blocks | $B = 64$ (SHA-256) / $128$ (SHA-512) | Bytes |
| 8 | MD5 digest | $128$ bits | **Broken since 2004** |
| 9 | SHA-1 digest | $160$ bits | **Deprecated since 2017** |
| 10 | SHA-256 digest | $256$ bits | **Current standard** |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Authentication Function Used |
|---|---|
| **TLS 1.3 handshake** | ECDSA signatures + HKDF (HMAC-based KDF) |
| **Bitcoin / Blockchain** | SHA-256 double hash + ECDSA (secp256k1) |
| **JWT (JSON Web Tokens)** | HMAC-SHA256 (HS256) or RSA signatures |
| **Software updates (Windows/Linux)** | Code signing using RSA + SHA-256 |
| **SSH login** | EdDSA / RSA signatures |
| **Credit card chip (EMV)** | MAC on transaction data (CBC-MAC) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Birthday Paradox Collision Probability

**Goal:** Show that finding a hash collision requires only $\mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ trials.

### Step 1: Probability of No Collision After $k$ Trials

Total possible hash values: $N = 2^n$.
We pick $k$ random inputs $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k$.

The probability that **all** $k$ values are distinct:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{no collision}) &= 1 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2}{N}\right) \cdots \left(1 - \frac{k-1}{N}\right) \\
&= \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Apply Taylor Approximation

For small $x$, we have $1 - x \approx e^{-x}$. Hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{no collision}) &\approx \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} e^{-i/N} = e^{-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} i} \\
&= e^{-\frac{k(k-1)}{2N}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Probability of At Least One Collision

$$P(\text{collision}) = 1 - e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)}$$

### Step 4: Find $k$ for 50% Collision Probability

Set $P(\text{collision}) = 0.5$:

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)} \implies e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)} = 0.5$$

Taking natural log:

$$-\frac{k(k-1)}{2N} = \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2$$

$$k(k-1) = 2N \ln 2$$

For large $k$, $k(k-1) \approx k^2$:

$$k^2 \approx 2N \ln 2 \implies k \approx \sqrt{2 \ln 2} \cdot \sqrt{N} \approx 1.177\sqrt{N}$$

### Step 5: Specialize to Hash Functions

Substituting $N = 2^n$:

$$k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** An $n$-bit hash offers only $n/2$ bits of **collision security**. For SHA-256 ($n = 256$), an attacker needs $\approx 2^{128}$ trials — still computationally infeasible today. For MD5 ($n = 128$), only $2^{64}$ trials — feasible on commodity hardware (achieved in 2004 by Wang et al.).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: HMAC Security Strength

**Goal:** Justify the security claim of HMAC: $\text{security} = \min(2^n, 2^L)$, where $L$ is the key length in bits.

### Setup
$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\Big( (K \oplus opad) \;\Vert\; H\big( (K \oplus ipad) \;\Vert\; M \big) \Big)$$

### Step 1: Inner Hash Attack
The attacker can vary $M$ arbitrarily. The inner hash $H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M)$ is a standard hash with $n$-bit output.

- To find an inner collision: $2^{n/2}$ work.
- To invert inner hash: $2^n$ work.

### Step 2: Outer Hash Attack
The outer hash takes the inner hash value as input. To find a collision in the outer hash, the attacker needs a collision in the inner hash first.

- So work factor for outer collision: also $2^{n/2}$.

### Step 3: Key Recovery
If $L < n$, brute-forcing $K$ over the keyspace needs $2^L$ trials.
If $L \geq n$, then the limiting factor is the hash strength $2^n$ (or $2^{n/2}$ for collision).

### Step 4: Combine
$$\text{Work to forge HMAC} = \min(2^L, 2^{n/2} \text{ collisions}, 2^n \text{ pre-image}) \approx \min(2^L, 2^{n/2})$$

For **HMAC-SHA-256** with a 256-bit key:

$$\text{Security} = \min(2^{256}, 2^{128}) = 2^{128} \text{ bits}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Why use HMAC instead of plain $H(K \Vert M)$?"* The answer: **Length-extension attacks**. A naive $H(K \Vert M)$ allows an attacker, knowing $H(K \Vert M)$ and $\vert M \vert$, to compute $H(K \Vert M \Vert \text{pad})$ without knowing $K$. HMAC's nested construction defeats this.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Choosing Hash Length for an ATM Network

**Problem (KTU-style):** An ATM processes 1 million transactions per day. The bank wants collision probability of a SHA-based MAC to be less than $10^{-9}$ over 10 years. What minimum digest length $n$ is required?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total messages in 10 years.

$$N_{\text{msgs}} = 10^6 \times 365 \times 10 = 3.65 \times 10^{9} \approx 2^{31.76}$$

**Step 2:** Probability of at least one collision among $k$ messages (using birthday formula).

$$P \approx 1 - e^{-k(k-1)/(2 \cdot 2^n)}$$

**Step 3:** Set $P \leq 10^{-9}$.

$$10^{-9} \geq 1 - e^{-k^2/2^{n+1}} \implies e^{-k^2/2^{n+1}} \geq 1 - 10^{-9} \approx 1$$

Taking log:

$$-\frac{k^2}{2^{n+1}} \geq \ln(1 - 10^{-9}) \approx -10^{-9}$$

$$2^{n+1} \leq k^2 \times 10^{9}$$

$$2^{n+1} \leq (2^{31.76})^2 \times 10^{9} = 2^{63.52} \times 10^{9} \approx 2^{63.52} \times 2^{29.9} = 2^{93.42}$$

$$n + 1 \leq 93.42 \implies n \geq 92.4 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4:** Add a safety margin (NIST recommendation: $\geq 112$ bits for legacy, $128$ for new systems).

> [!TIP]
> **Answer: $n \geq 128$ bits.** Hence **SHA-256** is the correct choice, not SHA-1 (160-bit) or MD5 (128-bit, but broken by collision attacks). This exact type of problem is a KTU **14-mark favorite**.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: When to Use What

| Attack Scenario | Recommended Authentication Function | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Bulk data integrity (e.g., file download) | **SHA-256 hash** | Fast, public verification |
| Authenticated message between two parties with shared key | **HMAC-SHA-256** | Keyed, fast, secure |
| Software code distribution (Microsoft, Apple) | **RSA / ECDSA signature** | Public verifiability + non-repudiation |
| Real-time stream authentication (VoIP, video) | **Lightweight MAC (GMAC, Poly1305)** | Speed critical |
| Blockchain transactions | **ECDSA over secp256k1** | Compact signatures, non-repudiation |
| IoT sensor with $\leq 16$ KB RAM | **SPONGENT / PHOTON** (lightweight hash) | Low footprint |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Authentication Function Flow

This diagram shows the **complete end-to-end authentication workflow**, covering message origin authentication, integrity verification, and non-repudiation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SenderSide["SENDER SIDE - Alice"]
        A1["Message M"] --> A2["Hash Function H"]
        A2 --> A3["Digest h = H M"]
        A3 --> A4["Sign using Kpriv of Alice"]
        A4 --> A5["Signature S"]
        A1 --> A6["Append M and S"]
    end

    A6 --> B1["Network Channel Insecure"]

    subgraph ReceiverSide["RECEIVER SIDE - Bob"]
        B1 --> C1["Receive M and S"]
        C1 --> C2["Compute h1 = H M"]
        C2 --> C3["Verify S using Kpub of Alice"]
        C3 --> C4{"Signature Valid?"}
        C4 -->|Yes| C5["ACCEPT - Authenticated and Untampered"]
        C4 -->|No| C6["REJECT - Discard Message"]
    end

    style SenderSide fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style ReceiverSide fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style A2 fill:#b3e0ff
    style A4 fill:#b3e0ff
    style C2 fill:#ffd9b3
    style C3 fill:#ffd9b3
    style C5 fill:#d4f4d4
    style C6 fill:#f4d4d4
```

---

## 4.2 MAC vs Digital Signature — Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MACFlow["MAC - Symmetric Authentication"]
        M1["Message M"] --> M2["MAC Algorithm with Shared Secret K"]
        K1["Shared Secret K"] --> M2
        M2 --> M3["Tag T = MAC K M"]
        M3 --> M4["Send M + T over Channel"]
        M4 --> M5["Receiver Recomputes T prime = MAC K M"]
        M5 --> M6{"T == T prime?"}
        M6 -->|Yes| M7["ACCEPT"]
        M6 -->|No| M8["REJECT - Tampered or Forged"]
    end

    subgraph SigFlow["Digital Signature - Asymmetric Authentication"]
        S1["Message M"] --> S2["Hash to get h = H M"]
        S2 --> S3["Sign h with Kpriv"]
        S3 --> S4["Signature S"]
        S4 --> S5["Send M + S over Channel"]
        S5 --> S6["Receiver hashes M to h prime = H M"]
        S6 --> S7["Verify S with Kpub to recover h"]
        S7 --> S8{"h == h prime?"}
        S8 -->|Yes| S9["ACCEPT - Authenticated + Non Repudiable"]
        S8 -->|No| S10["REJECT"]
    end

    style MACFlow fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#1f5582
    style SigFlow fill:#f8e8f4,stroke:#821f55
    style M7 fill:#d4f4d4
    style M8 fill:#f4d4d4
    style S9 fill:#d4f4d4
    style S10 fill:#f4d4d4
```

---

## 4.3 HMAC Internal Construction (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K["Secret Key K"] --> KP["Pad or Hash K to Block Size B"]
    KP --> K1["K XOR ipad"]
    KP --> K2["K XOR opad"]

    M["Message M"] --> C1["Concatenate"]
    K1 --> C1
    C1 --> H1["Inner Hash H"]
    H1 --> C2["Concatenate"]
    K2 --> C2
    C2 --> H2["Outer Hash H"]
    H2 --> OUT["HMAC Output Tag"]

    style K fill:#fff2cc
    style M fill:#fff2cc
    style H1 fill:#b3d9ff
    style H2 fill:#b3d9ff
    style OUT fill:#d4f4d4
```

---

## 4.4 Authentication Hierarchy — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Authentication Functions))
    Level1
      Message Encryption
      Implicit Authentication
    Level2
      Message Authentication Code MAC
      Keyed Hash HMAC
      CBC MAC
      CMAC
    Level3
      Cryptographic Hash Functions
      MD5 Broken
      SHA 1 Deprecated
      SHA 2 Family SHA 224 256 384 512
      SHA 3 Keccak
      Digital Signatures
      RSA Signature
      DSA
      ECDSA
      EdDSA
    Services Provided
      Data Integrity
      Origin Authentication
      Non Repudiation
    Properties
      Pre image Resistance
      Second Pre image Resistance
      Collision Resistance
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech** evaluation pattern. Mark distribution follows the official **ESE Module template**:
> - Part A: 3 marks (short answer)
> - Part B: 14 marks (descriptive with internal choice; split into 7 + 7)

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Explain the three security services provided by message authentication.**

**Model Answer:**

Message authentication provides three core security services:

1. **Data Integrity:** Ensures that the message has not been altered, inserted, deleted, or replayed during transmission. The receiver detects any modification by comparing a recomputed hash/MAC against the received one.

2. **Data Origin Authentication (Message Authentication):** Confirms that the message was indeed originated from the claimed sender. Achieved through shared secrets (MAC) or digital signatures (asymmetric).

3. **Non-Repudiation:** Prevents the sender from denying that they sent the message. Only asymmetric (digital signature) schemes provide this, because the sender's private key is unique and non-shareable.

These three services together ensure that communication is **trustworthy and verifiable** in adversarial environments. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between a Message Authentication Code (MAC) and a Hash Function.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hash Function | MAC |
|---|---|---|
| **Key** | Keyless (no secret) | Keyed (uses shared secret) |
| **Output** | Hash value $H(M)$ | Tag $T = \text{MAC}(K, M)$ |
| **Authentication** | Only integrity check | Both integrity **and** origin authentication |
| **Non-Repudiation** | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Collision Resistance** | Required | Required (keyed variant) |
| **Example** | SHA-256, SHA-3 | HMAC-SHA-256, CMAC |

**Key insight:** A plain hash function only proves that the message has not been tampered with; it does **not** prove **who** sent it, because anyone can compute $H(M)$. A MAC binds the message to a specific key holder. *(3 marks)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the three security properties of cryptographic hash functions. Why is collision resistance considered weaker than pre-image resistance? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and security of HMAC. Why is HMAC preferred over a simple $H(K \Vert M)$ scheme? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a) Hash Function Properties

A cryptographic hash function $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ must satisfy:

**1. Pre-image Resistance (One-Wayness):**
Given $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find $x$ such that $H(x) = h$.
- **Work factor:** $W = \mathcal{O}(2^n)$
- **Real-world need:** Password storage — given the stored hash, attacker cannot recover the password.

**2. Second Pre-image Resistance:**
Given $x_1$, it is infeasible to find $x_2 \neq x_1$ with $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$.
- **Work factor:** $W = \mathcal{O}(2^n)$
- **Real-world need:** Prevent an attacker from substituting a fraudulent document with the same hash.

**3. Collision Resistance:**
It is infeasible to find **any** two distinct $x_1, x_2$ such that $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$.
- **Work factor:** $W = \mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$

**Why collision resistance is weaker — the Birthday Paradox:**

By the birthday paradox, when sampling $k$ random values from a set of size $N = 2^n$, the probability of collision exceeds 50% when $k \approx 1.177 \sqrt{N} = 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$.

In contrast, finding a **specific** pre-image requires trying $2^n$ values on average.

| Property | Work Factor | Example: SHA-256 |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-image | $2^{256}$ | Infeasible |
| Second pre-image | $2^{256}$ | Infeasible |
| Collision | $2^{128}$ | Infeasible (still safe) |
| MD5 Collision | $2^{64}$ | **Broken in 2004** |

Hence, collision resistance is *exponentially easier to break* (by a square-root factor) than pre-image resistance. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b) HMAC Construction and Security

**HMAC Definition (RFC 2104):**
$$\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big( (K \oplus opad) \;\Vert\; H\big( (K \oplus ipad) \;\Vert\; M \big) \Big)$$

where:
- $B$ = byte length of hash input block (e.g., $B = 64$ for SHA-256)
- $ipad$ = $0x36$ repeated $B$ times
- $opad$ = $0x5C$ repeated $B$ times
- $K$ is zero-padded to $B$ bytes (or pre-hashed if longer)

**Step-by-step operation:**

1. Pad/Hash $K$ to $B$ bytes.
2. Compute $S_i = K \oplus ipad$ and $S_o = K \oplus opad$.
3. Inner hash: $H_{\text{in}} = H(S_i \Vert M)$.
4. Outer hash: $\text{HMAC} = H(S_o \Vert H_{\text{in}})$.
5. Output fixed-length tag.

**Security Proof Sketch:**
HMAC's security reduces to the underlying hash function's strength. The proven work factor to forge is:

$$W_{\text{HMAC}} = \min(2^L, 2^{n/2})$$

where $L$ = key length, $n$ = hash output length. For HMAC-SHA-256 with $L = 256$: $W = 2^{128}$.

**Why HMAC and not $H(K \Vert M)$?**

A naive $H(K \Vert M)$ is vulnerable to **length-extension attacks**. In Merkle-Damgård hashes (SHA-256, MD5, SHA-1), given $H(K \Vert M)$ and $\vert M \vert$, an attacker can compute $H(K \Vert M \Vert \text{pad} \Vert \text{attacker-chosen-data})$ **without knowing $K$**.

The double-hash (outer + inner) structure of HMAC defeats this attack because the secret $K$ enters both the inner and outer hash in key-mixed form, breaking the length-extension chain.

> **Additional benefits of HMAC:**
> - Reuses existing hash implementations (no new primitive).
> - Provably secure under reasonable assumptions on $H$.
> - Constant-time implementations available (timing-attack resistant). *(7 marks)*

---

### **OR**

### **Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2023] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of a digital signature scheme. Compare it with MAC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An organization uses SHA-1 for digital signatures. They want to upgrade to a hash function such that the probability of a collision in $10^{12}$ messages is at most $10^{-12}$. Determine the minimum required hash length. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a) Digital Signature Scheme

**Digital Signature Operation:**

**Setup Phase:** Each user generates a key pair $(K_{pub}, K_{priv})$ where $K_{priv}$ is kept secret and $K_{pub}$ is published in a certificate (e.g., X.509).

**Signing (by Alice):**
1. Compute message digest: $h = H(M)$.
2. Sign digest: $S = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}^A}(h)$.
3. Transmit $(M, S)$ to Bob.

**Verification (by Bob):**
1. Compute digest of received message: $h' = H(M)$.
2. Recover original hash from signature: $h = \text{Verify}_{K_{pub}^A}(S)$.
3. Accept if and only if $h = h'$.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes):** The diagram illustrates the complete sender-side signing and receiver-side verification workflow.

**Comparison: Digital Signature vs MAC**

| Parameter | Digital Signature | MAC |
|---|---|---|
| **Key type** | Asymmetric (public + private) | Symmetric (shared secret) |
| **Non-repudiation** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Public verifiability** | ✅ Anyone with public key | ❌ Only key holder |
| **Speed** | Slow (modular arithmetic) | Fast (hash operations) |
| **Key management** | Complex (PKI required) | Simple (shared secret) |
| **Algorithm examples** | RSA, ECDSA, EdDSA | HMAC, CMAC, GMAC |
| **Primary use** | Legal contracts, software signing | Session authentication, TLS |

Digital signatures are **publicly verifiable** and provide **non-repudiation**, making them essential for legal documents, software distribution, and blockchain. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b) Hash Length Calculation

**Given:**
- Number of messages: $k = 10^{12}$
- Required collision probability: $P \leq 10^{-12}$

**Step 1:** Apply the birthday collision formula:

$$P \approx 1 - e^{-k(k-1)/(2 \cdot 2^n)}$$

**Step 2:** For very small $P$, $1 - e^{-x} \approx x$ when $x$ is small. So:

$$P \approx \frac{k^2}{2^{n+1}} \quad \text{(when } k \ll 2^{n/2}\text{)}$$

**Step 3:** Set $P = 10^{-12}$ and solve for $n$:

$$10^{-12} = \frac{(10^{12})^2}{2^{n+1}} = \frac{10^{24}}{2^{n+1}}$$

$$2^{n+1} = 10^{24} \times 10^{12} = 10^{36}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to base 2 using $10 \approx 2^{3.3219}$:

$$2^{n+1} = 10^{36} = 2^{36 \times 3.3219} = 2^{119.59}$$

$$n + 1 = 119.59 \implies n = 118.59$$

**Step 5:** Round up to next power-of-2 multiple for safety margin and standard hash sizes:

> **Minimum required hash length: $n = 160$ bits (SHA-1) or higher (recommended: SHA-256).**

**Verification with $n = 160$:**
$$P = \frac{(10^{12})^2}{2^{161}} = \frac{10^{24}}{2.92 \times 10^{48}} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-25} \ll 10^{-12} \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering recommendation:** Always use the next standard hash size (160 → 256 bits) for a comfortable security margin. The KTU examiner will accept $n = 160$ bits, but full marks require justification with safety margins. *(7 marks)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MAC with Digital Signatures** — MAC is *symmetric and lacks non-repudiation*. Many students write "MAC provides non-repudiation" — instant 2-mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Wrong work factor for collision resistance** — Writing $2^n$ instead of $2^{n/2}$ is a classic blunder. Always cite the **Birthday Paradox**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the security of HMAC formula** — $\min(2^L, 2^{n/2})$ is a high-frequency question. Writing $\min(2^L, 2^n)$ is wrong.
>
> 4. **Hash function weaknesses** — Stating "SHA-1 is secure" in 2024 is **factually incorrect**. SHA-1 has known collisions (SHAttered, 2017). Always mention the **deprecation year**.
>
> 5. **Not drawing the diagram** — In 7-mark questions, a diagram is **mandatory**. Skipping it costs 2–3 marks. Use clear labeled boxes (sender → hash → sign → send → verify).
>
> 6. **Mixing up $H(K \Vert M)$ and HMAC** — KTU specifically tests the **length-extension attack** rationale. Always mention the **double-hash (Merkle-Damgård strengthening)**.
>
> 7. **Wrong unit of $N$ in birthday paradox** — $N$ is the **number of possible hash outputs**, not the number of bits. Writing $N = n$ instead of $N = 2^n$ is a frequent error.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. It is your last-minute revision sheet.**

### ✅ Core Concepts to Remember
- **Authentication** = verify identity + integrity + non-repudiation
- **Three security services:** data integrity, origin authentication, non-repudiation
- **Authentication ≠ Authorization** (AuthN vs AuthZ)

### ✅ Hash Function Must-Knows
- **Three properties:** pre-image, second pre-image, collision resistance
- **Collision work factor:** $W = \mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ ← Birthday Paradox
- **MD5 (128-bit):** ❌ **Broken** (2004)
- **SHA-1 (160-bit):** ❌ **Deprecated** (2017)
- **SHA-2 (256-bit):** ✅ **Current standard**
- **SHA-3 (Keccak):** ✅ **NIST standard, 2015**

### ✅ MAC Must-Knows
- **MAC = symmetric** (shared secret), provides integrity + origin authentication
- **No non-repudiation** in MAC
- **HMAC = Hash-based MAC** (RFC 2104)
- **HMAC formula:** $H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M))$
- **HMAC security:** $\min(2^L, 2^{n/2})$
- **Why HMAC > $H(K \Vert M)$:** defeats length-extension attacks

### ✅ Digital Signature Must-Knows
- **Asymmetric**, provides integrity + origin authentication + **non-repudiation**
- **Sign with private key, verify with public key**
- **Algorithms:** RSA, DSA, ECDSA, EdDSA

### ✅ Key Formulas (Memorize!)
| Formula | Use |
|---|---|
| $P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-k^2/2N}$ | Birthday attack |
| $k \approx 1.177 \sqrt{2^n}$ | Collision trials for $n$-bit hash |
| $W_{\text{HMAC}} = \min(2^L, 2^{n/2})$ | HMAC security |
| $\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M))$ | HMAC construction |

### ✅ Common Real-World Deployments
- **TLS 1.3** → ECDSA + HMAC-SHA256
- **Bitcoin** → SHA-256 (double hash) + ECDSA
- **JWT** → HMAC-SHA256 (HS256) or RSA
- **Code signing** → RSA / ECDSA over SHA-256
- **SSH** → EdDSA / RSA signatures

> 🎓 **Final Tip:** Always justify *why* a particular algorithm is chosen by linking to the **threat model** and the **work factor required** — this is what KTU examiners reward with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Message Authentication Codes: Hash Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Message Authentication Codes: Hash Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Message Authentication Code (MAC) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** is a cryptographic technique that uses a **secret symmetric key** $K$ shared between the sender and the receiver to produce a fixed-size authentication tag (also called the MAC value, tag, or checksum) for a given message $M$. The MAC function is expressed as:
>
> $$\text{MAC}(K, M) = T$$
>
> where $T$ is a fixed-length authentication tag, $K$ is the shared secret key, and $M$ is the variable-length message. The MAC provides both **data integrity** (detection of modification) and **data origin authentication** (proof of sender's identity).

### 1.2 Cryptographic Hash Function — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Cryptographic Hash Function** $H(\cdot)$ is a deterministic mathematical function that accepts an input (message) of arbitrary length and produces a fixed-size output called a **hash value**, **message digest**, or **fingerprint**, typically of length $n$ bits. The formal signature is:
>
> $$H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$$
>
> The output is a deterministic, one-way mapping that is computationally infeasible to reverse.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **MAC** | A **wax seal stamped on a royal envelope** using a unique signet ring (the shared secret key). Only the king and the trusted messenger possess identical signets. If the seal is broken or replaced, tampering is detected. | Authentication + Integrity |
| **Hash Function** | A **human fingerprint** — every person (data) has a unique fingerprint (digest) of fixed length. You cannot reconstruct a person from their fingerprint alone. | Integrity only (keyless) |
| **HMAC** | A **bank's certified courier slip** that combines a secret bank stamp (key) with the fingerprint of the parcel (hash) to prove both the identity of the courier and the integrity of the contents. | Authentication + Integrity (keyed hash) |

### 1.4 Why MAC and Hash Functions Are Needed

- **Plain encryption** (e.g., AES-CBC) protects **confidentiality**, but does not always guarantee integrity (because of malleability in some modes).
- **Hash alone** (e.g., SHA-256) guarantees **integrity** but offers **no authentication** (anyone can compute a hash).
- **MAC** combines the best of both worlds: a secret key ensures only authorized parties can produce a valid tag, and the hash property ensures any tampering is detected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** A MAC provides authentication and integrity. A Hash provides only integrity. A Digital Signature provides authentication, integrity, and **non-repudiation**. These three distinctions are frequently asked in KTU Module 1 examinations.

### 1.5 The Three Fundamental Security Properties of Hash Functions

1. **Pre-image Resistance (One-Way Property):** Given a hash $h = H(M)$, it must be computationally infeasible to find any $M'$ such that $H(M') = h$.
2. **Second Pre-image Resistance (Weak Collision Resistance):** Given a specific message $M_1$, it must be computationally infeasible to find a different $M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
3. **Collision Resistance (Strong Collision Resistance):** It must be computationally infeasible to find *any two distinct* messages $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Requirements of a MAC Algorithm (RFC 2104 / NIST Standards)

A secure MAC algorithm must satisfy the following properties, which are the foundation of any **KTU Module 1 ESE question on MAC design**:

- **Ease of Computation:** Given $K$ and $M$, the MAC value $T$ must be computed efficiently.
- **Compression:** The MAC value $T$ has a fixed length (typically $64$, $128$, or $256$ bits) regardless of the message size $\vert M \vert$.
- **Computation-Resistance (Key Recovery Resistance):** Given one or more message-tag pairs $(M_i, T_i)$, it must be computationally infeasible to compute the secret key $K$.
- **Forgery-Resistance (Existential Unforgeability):** Without knowing $K$, it must be infeasible to produce a valid $(M, T)$ pair.
- **Tag Verification:** The receiver re-computes $\text{MAC}(K, M)$ and checks if the result equals the received $T$.

### 2.2 Types of MAC Constructions

| Construction Type | Mechanism | Key Property | Standard Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Keyed Hash MAC (HMAC)** | Wraps a cryptographic hash function (SHA-256, SHA-512) with two derived keys | Provably secure if underlying hash is strong | **HMAC-SHA-256**, HMAC-SHA-512 |
| **CMAC** | Uses a block cipher (AES) in CBC mode with carefully designed key handling | Standardized by NIST SP 800-38B | AES-CMAC |
| **GMAC** | Specialized for Galois field operations, used in GCM mode | High speed, parallelizable | AES-GMAC |
| **Poly1305** | One-time authenticator using polynomial evaluation | Extremely fast, but key used only once | Poly1305-AES |
| **CBC-MAC** (legacy) | Block cipher in CBC mode, output of last block is the tag | Insecure for variable-length messages without finishing step | ISO/IEC 9797-1 |

### 2.3 HMAC — The Industry Standard Keyed Hash

**HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)** is the most widely deployed MAC algorithm, standardized in **RFC 2104**, **FIPS 198-1**, and **ISO/IEC 9797-2**. It uses an underlying iterative hash function $H$ (e.g., SHA-256) and a secret key $K$.

> [!NOTE]
> **HMAC Operational Concept (The "Why" and "How"):**
> - **Why two keys (inner/outer)?** To prevent length-extension attacks and to allow a formal security proof (Bellare-Canetti-Krawczyk 1996).
> - **How does it work?** It sandwiches the message between two hashed layers: $H(K \oplus \text{opad} \, \Vert \, H(K \oplus \text{ipad} \, \Vert \, M))$.

The formal HMAC construction:

$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\left(\left(K' \oplus opad\right) \;\Vert\; H\left(\left(K' \oplus ipad\right) \;\Vert\; M\right)\right)$$

where:
- $K'$ is the key $K$ padded to the hash block size $b$ (e.g., $b = 512$ bits for SHA-256). If $K$ is longer than $b$, it is first hashed: $K' = H(K) \, \Vert \, 0...0$.
- $\text{ipad} = 0x36$ repeated $b$ times (the **inner pad**).
- $\text{opad} = 0x5C$ repeated $b$ times (the **outer pad**).
- $\oplus$ denotes bitwise XOR.
- $\Vert$ denotes bitwise concatenation.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Description | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ | Hash function signature | $n$ = digest length in bits |
| 2 | $\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H((K' \oplus opad) \Vert H((K' \oplus ipad) \Vert M))$ | HMAC definition | $K'$ = derived key, $b$ = block size |
| 3 | $b = $ block size of hash (e.g., 512 bits for SHA-256) | Inner hash block size | bits |
| 4 | $L = $ digest output length (e.g., 256 bits for SHA-256) | Hash output size | bits |
| 5 | $P_{\text{collision}} \approx 1 - e^{-k^2 / 2^{n+1}}$ | Birthday attack collision probability | $k$ = messages, $n$ = digest bits |
| 6 | $N_{\text{collision}} \approx \sqrt{2^n} = 2^{n/2}$ | Number of trials to find collision | messages |
| 7 | $P_{\text{preimage}} \approx k / 2^n$ | Pre-image attack probability | brute force |
| 8 | Recommended minimum digest size: $n \geq 256$ bits | Modern security requirement | bits |
| 9 | $T_{\text{output}} = 64$ to $256$ bits (typical) | MAC tag length | bits |
| 10 | $\text{SHA-256} \rightarrow n = 256, b = 512$ | Common parameters | bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - **TLS 1.3** uses **HMAC-SHA-256** in its Handshake and Record protocols.
> - **JWT (JSON Web Tokens)** uses **HMAC-SHA-256** for token signing (`HS256`).
> - **IPsec** uses **HMAC-SHA-256** in ESP and AH headers.
> - **Bitcoin** uses **SHA-256** (double-hashed: `SHA256(SHA256(x))`) for mining and address generation.

### 2.5 Security Strength Comparison (KTU High-Yield)

| Hash Function | Digest Size | Collision Resistance | Pre-image Resistance | Status (2024) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MD5** | 128 bits | **BROKEN** (≈ $2^{21}$ operations) | Weakened | **Deprecated — Do Not Use** |
| **SHA-1** | 160 bits | **BROKEN** (SHAttered, 2017, ≈ $2^{63}$ operations) | Weakened | **Deprecated by NIST (2011)** |
| **SHA-256** | 256 bits | ≈ $2^{128}$ operations | ≈ $2^{256}$ operations | **Secure — Recommended** |
| **SHA-384** | 384 bits | ≈ $2^{192}$ operations | ≈ $2^{384}$ operations | **Secure — Recommended** |
| **SHA-512** | 512 bits | ≈ $2^{256}$ operations | ≈ $2^{512}$ operations | **Secure — Recommended** |
| **SHA-3 (Keccak)** | 224/256/384/512 | Same as SHA-2 family | Same as SHA-2 family | **Secure — Future Standard** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The HMAC Construction

We will derive the HMAC algorithm from first principles, step by step, following the RFC 2104 specification.

**Step 1 — Define the underlying hash function and parameters.**

Let $H$ be the chosen cryptographic hash function (e.g., SHA-256).
Let $b$ be the input block size of $H$ in bits (for SHA-256, $b = 512$).
Let $L$ be the output length of $H$ in bits (for SHA-256, $L = 256$).
Let $K$ be the secret key of arbitrary length.

**Step 2 — Key Normalization (Create $K'$).**

If the key $K$ is shorter than $b$ bits, append zeros to the right until its length equals $b$:

$$K' = K \,\Vert\, 0...0 \quad \text{until } \vert K' \vert = b \text{ bits}$$

If $K$ is longer than $b$ bits, first hash it and then pad to $b$ bits:

$$K' = H(K) \,\Vert\, 0...0 \quad \text{until } \vert K' \vert = b \text{ bits}$$

This ensures the derived key fits the hash block size.

**Step 3 — Construct the Inner and Outer Keys.**

The two fixed padding constants are:

$$\text{ipad} = (0x36)_b = \underbrace{0x36 \, 0x36 \, \ldots \, 0x36}_{b/8 \text{ bytes}}$$

$$\text{opad} = (0x5C)_b = \underbrace{0x5C \, 0x5C \, \ldots \, 0x5C}_{b/8 \text{bytes}}$$

XOR each with the normalized key:

$$K_{\text{inner}} = K' \oplus \text{ipad}$$

$$K_{\text{outer}} = K' \oplus \text{opad}$$

**Step 4 — Inner Hash Computation.**

Concatenate the inner key with the message, then apply the hash function:

$$H_{\text{inner}} = H\left(K_{\text{inner}} \,\Vert\, M\right)$$

**Step 5 — Outer Hash Computation.**

Concatenate the outer key with the inner hash result, then apply the hash function a second time:

$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\left(K_{\text{outer}} \,\Vert\, H_{\text{inner}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Final Compact Form.**

Combining Steps 4 and 5, the complete HMAC definition is:

$$\boxed{\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\left(\left(K' \oplus opad\right) \;\Vert\; H\left(\left(K' \oplus ipad\right) \;\Vert\; M\right)\right)}$$

**Why Two Hashes?** The double-hash structure provides a clean security reduction: if the underlying hash $H$ is a secure Merkle-Damgård hash, then HMAC is provably secure (a PRF) as long as the key entropy is sufficiently high. The inner hash binds the key to the message; the outer hash protects the entire construction against length-extension attacks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Birthday Paradox and Collision Attack Complexity

The **Birthday Paradox** theorem is critical for KTU exams because it explains why collision attacks on hash functions are much faster than brute-force pre-image attacks.

**Step 1 — State the problem.**

Given a hash function with $n$-bit output, how many random messages $k$ must be hashed before the probability of finding at least one collision exceeds $50\%$?

**Step 2 — Apply the classical birthday probability formula.**

The probability that all $k$ hashed values are distinct (no collision) is approximately:

$$P_{\text{no collision}} \approx e^{-k(k-1) / (2 \cdot 2^n)}$$

For large $k$, this simplifies to:

$$P_{\text{no collision}} \approx e^{-k^2 / 2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 3 — Set the collision probability to $0.5$ (50%).**

We require:

$$1 - e^{-k^2 / 2^{n+1}} = 0.5$$

$$e^{-k^2 / 2^{n+1}} = 0.5$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-k^2 / 2^{n+1} = \ln(0.5) = -0.693$$

$$k^2 = 0.693 \cdot 2^{n+1} = \ln(2) \cdot 2^{n+1} = 2^n \cdot \ln(2) \cdot 2 \approx 2^n$$

$$k \approx \sqrt{2^n} = 2^{n/2}$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{N_{\text{collision}} \approx 2^{n/2}}$$

For example:
- MD5 ($n = 128$): collision in ≈ $2^{64}$ operations (theoretically; in practice, broken faster).
- SHA-1 ($n = 160$): collision in ≈ $2^{80}$ operations.
- SHA-256 ($n = 256$): collision in ≈ $2^{128}$ operations (computationally infeasible).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: MAC vs. Hash vs. Digital Signature

| Security Property | Hash Only | MAC (HMAC) | Digital Signature (RSA/ECDSA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Integrity** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Authentication** | No | Yes (shared key) | Yes (public key) |
| **Non-Repudiation** | No | No | Yes |
| **Key Type** | None | Symmetric (shared secret) | Asymmetric (private/public pair) |
| **Speed** | Very Fast | Fast | Slower (especially RSA) |
| **Verifiable by** | Anyone with the message | Only the holder of $K$ | Anyone with the public key |
| **KTU Example** | SHA-256 checksum | HMAC-SHA-256 in TLS | RSA-PKCS#1 v1.5 in PGP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: MAC Generation and Verification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Sender Side] --> SK[Shared Secret Key K]
    SK --> MACgen[MAC Generation Algorithm]
    M[Message M] --> MACgen
    MACgen --> T[MAC Tag T]
    T --> TX[Transmitted: M and T]
    TX --> RV[Receiver Side]
    SK2[Shared Secret Key K] --> MACverify[MAC Verification Algorithm]
    M2[Received Message M] --> MACverify
    T2[Received Tag T] --> MACverify
    MACverify --> CMP{Compare T computed vs T received}
    CMP -->|Match| ACC[Message Accepted: Authentic and Untampered]
    CMP -->|No Match| REJ[Message Rejected: Tampered or Forged]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: HMAC Internal Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    K[Secret Key K] --> KP[Key Normalization to b bits K prime]
    M[Message M] --> IC[Inner Concatenation]
    KP --> XOR1[XOR with ipad]
    XOR1 --> IC
    IC --> IH[Inner Hash H]
    IH --> OC[Outer Concatenation]
    KP --> XOR2[XOR with opad]
    XOR2 --> OC
    OC --> OH[Outer Hash H]
    OH --> OUT[HMAC Tag T]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Hash Function Security Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cryptographic Hash Function H] --> P[Property 1: Pre-image Resistance]
    A --> S[Property 2: Second Pre-image Resistance]
    A --> C[Property 3: Collision Resistance]
    P --> P1[Given h find M is Hard]
    S --> S1[Given M1 find M2 such that H M1 equals H M2 is Hard]
    C --> C1[Find any M1 not equal M2 with H M1 equals H M2 is Hard]
    C --> C2[Birthday Attack: requires approximately 2 to the power n by 2 trials]
    P --> P3[Brute force: requires approximately 2 to the power n trials]
    S --> S3[Brute force: requires approximately 2 to the power n trials]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Hash Function Iterative Compression (Merkle-Damgård Construction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M0[Message Block M1] --> CF1[Compression Function f]
    IV[Initial Vector IV] --> CF1
    CF1 --> H1[Intermediate Hash H1]
    M1[Message Block M2] --> CF2[Compression Function f]
    H1 --> CF2
    CF2 --> H2[Intermediate Hash H2]
    M2[Message Block Mk] --> CFk[Compression Function f]
    Hk1[Intermediate Hash Hk minus 1] --> CFk
    CFk --> HK[Final Hash Value H of M]
    ML[Message Length Padding] --> CFk
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The Merkle-Damgård construction shows how a fixed-size compression function is iteratively applied to variable-length messages. Each block is processed with the previous hash output, producing a chain that culminates in the final digest. This structure is the basis of MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-2. SHA-3 uses a different **sponge construction** based on Keccak.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
