The protocol uses a **trusted third party**, the **Key Distribution Center (KDC)**, to help two parties A and B establish a fresh session key $K_{AB}$. | Step | Message | Purpose | |------|---------|---------| | 1 | $A \to \text{KDC} : ID_A \Vert ID_B \Vert N_1$ | A requests a session key with B, includes nonce $N_1$ | | 2 | $\text{KDC} \to A : E_{K_A}[K_{AB} \Vert ID_B \Vert N_1] \Vert E_{K_B}[K_{AB} \Vert ID_A]$ | KDC returns session key encrypted with A's master key + ticket encrypted with B's key | | 3 | $A \to B : E_{K_B}[K_{AB} \Vert ID_A]$ | A forwards the ticket to B | | 4 | $B \to A : E_{K_B}[K_{AB} \Vert ID_A]$ *(original ticket)* | B decrypts, recovers $K_{AB}$ and $ID_A$ | | 5 | $A \to B : E_{K_{AB}}[N_2]$ | A proves knowledge of $K_{AB}$ by encrypting a fresh nonce | | 6 | $B \to A : E_{K_{AB}}[f(N_2)]$ | B returns a function of $N_2$ proving knowledge of $K_{AB}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter for Kerberos?** > Kerberos V4 is essentially Needham-Schroeder **plus timestamping** to defeat replay attacks, **plus an extra tier (TGS)** so users only type their password once per session. ### 2.2 Kerberos V4 — Operational Steps (The Full Dialogue) Let the following notation hold: - $C$ = Client - $AS$ = Authentication Server - $TGS$ = Ticket Granting Server - $V$ = Application Server (Verifier) - $ID_C, ID_{TGS}, ID_V$ = Identifiers - $K_C, K_{TGS}, K_V$ = Long-term symmetric keys - $K_{C,TGS}, K_{C,V}$ = Short-term session keys - $TS$ = Timestamp; $Lifetime$ = Validity period - $N_1, N_2, N_3$ = Nonces #### Dialogue 1 — Obtaining the Ticket-Granting Ticket (TGT) $$ \text{(1)} \quad C \to AS : \quad ID_C \Vert ID_{TGS} \Vert TS_1 $$ $$ \text{(2)} \quad AS \to C : \quad E_{K_C}[K_{C,TGS} \Vert ID_{TGS} \Vert TS_2 \Vert \text{Lifetime}_2 \Vert \text{Ticket}_{TGS}] $$ where $$ \text{Ticket}_{TGS} = E_{K_{TGS}}[K_{C,TGS} \Vert ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert ID_{TGS} \Vert TS_2 \Vert \text{Lifetime}_2] $$ #### Dialogue 2 — Obtaining the Service Ticket The client now constructs an **Authenticator** $A_{C,TGS}$ to prove freshness: $$ A_{C,TGS} = E_{K_{C,TGS}}[ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert TS_3] $$ $$ \text{(3)} \quad C \to TGS : \quad ID_V \Vert \text{Ticket}_{TGS} \Vert A_{C,TGS} $$ $$ \text{(4)} \quad TGS \to C : \quad E_{K_{C,TGS}}[K_{C,V} \Vert ID_V \Vert TS_4 \Vert \text{Ticket}_V] $$ where $$ \text{Ticket}_V = E_{K_V}[K_{C,V} \Vert ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert ID_V \Vert TS_4 \Vert \text{Lifetime}_4] $$ #### Dialogue 3 — Client Authenticates to the Application Server $$ A_{C,V} = E_{K_{C,V}}[ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert TS_5] $$ $$ \text{(5)} \quad C \to V : \quad \text{Ticket}_V \Vert A_{C,V} $$ $$ \text{(6)} \quad V \to C : \quad E_{K_{C,V}}[TS_5 + 1] $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step (6) is the mutual authentication component.** The server increments the client's timestamp by 1, encrypts with the session key, and returns it — proving the server decrypted the ticket and possesses $K_{C,V}$. This converts the strictly one-way authentication into **mutual authentication**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula / Notation Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Used In | |--------|---------|---------| | $E_K[X]$ | Encryption of plaintext $X$ with key $K$ | Every Kerberos message | | $K_C$ | Client's secret key derived from password hash | AS ↔ C communication | | $K_{TGS}, K_V$ | Long-term keys of TGS and Server | Inside tickets | | $K_{C,TGS}$ | Session key between C and TGS | TGS dialogue | | $K_{C,V}$ | Session key between C and V | Service dialogue | | $TS_i$ | Current timestamp from clock $i$ | Replay protection | | $\text{Lifetime}_i$ | Validity window of a ticket | Replay protection | | $AD_C$ | Client's network address (IP) | Binds ticket to a host | | $N_i$ | Nonce (random number) | Freshness guarantee | | $\text{Ticket}_{TGS}$ | $E_{K_{TGS}}[K_{C,TGS} \Vert ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert ID_{TGS} \Vert TS_2 \Vert \text{Lifetime}_2]$ | Bearer credential for TGS | | $\text{Ticket}_V$ | $E_{K_V}[K_{C,V} \Vert ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert ID_V \Vert TS_4 \Vert \text{Lifetime}_4]$ | Bearer credential for V | | $A_{C,TGS}$ | $E_{K_{C,TGS}}[ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert TS_3]$ | Client's freshness proof | | $A_{C,V}$ | $E_{K_{C,V}}[ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert TS_5]$ | Client's freshness proof | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Distinction (Common Exam Trap):** > - A **Ticket** is **reusable** within its lifetime. It is encrypted with the *server's* key, so the client cannot read or modify it. > - An **Authenticator** is **single-use** per session. It is encrypted with the *session key* and provides freshness. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |--------|-------------| | **Enterprise IT (Active Directory)** | Microsoft's modern AD uses Kerberos **V5** as the default authentication protocol in Windows domains. | | **Hadoop / Big Data** | HDFS, HBase, Kafka secure clusters via Kerberos. | | **SSH with GSSAPI** | SSH can use Kerberos for single sign-on across UNIX hosts. | | **Network File Systems** | NFSv4 can use Kerberos for user authentication. | | **Cloud / SaaS** | Many IaaS platforms (AWS, Azure AD) integrate Kerberos via federation. | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Kerberos V4 Authentication Dialogue Below is the **exhaustive logical walk-through** of how each message in the Kerberos V4 dialogue is constructed, transmitted, received, and verified. This corresponds to the standard KTU 14-mark problem. --- #### PHASE 1 — The Login Exchange (Dialogue 1) **Step 1: Client → Authentication Server** $$ C \to AS : \quad ID_C \Vert ID_{TGS} \Vert TS_1 $$ **Logic:** - The user launches a service (e.g., `kinit` on Linux) and types their password. - The client sends its **identity** $ID_C$ and the identity of the ticket-granting service $ID_{TGS}$ it wants to use. - $TS_1$ is a current timestamp used to detect delay/duplication by AS. > The message is sent in **cleartext** (no encryption) because $ID_C$ is not a secret — only the password-derived key $K_C$ is. **Step 2: AS → Client** The AS looks up $K_C$ (derived from the password) and constructs: $$ E_{K_C}[K_{C,TGS} \Vert ID_{TGS} \Vert TS_2 \Vert \text{Lifetime}_2 \Vert \text{Ticket}_{TGS}] $$ $$ \text{Ticket}_{TGS} = E_{K_{TGS}}[K_{C,TGS} \Vert ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert ID_{TGS} \Vert TS_2 \Vert \text{Lifetime}_2] $$ **Logic of each field:** $$ \begin{aligned} K_{C,TGS} &:= \text{new random session key generated by AS} \\ ID_{TGS} &:= \text{included so client knows which TGS this key is for} \\ TS_2 &:= \text{AS's current timestamp (time the ticket was issued)} \\ \text{Lifetime}_2 &:= \text{typical value: 8 hours} \\ \text{Ticket}_{TGS} &:= \text{sealed under } K_{TGS}; \text{ client cannot read or forge it} \\ AD_C &:= \text{client's IP address — binds ticket to that network} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Client-side verification:** - Client computes $K_C = \text{password hash}$. - Decrypts the outer envelope: $D_{K_C}[E_{K_C}[\ldots]] = K_{C,TGS} \Vert ID_{TGS} \Vert TS_2 \Vert \text{Lifetime}_2 \Vert \text{Ticket}_{TGS}$. - Stores $K_{C,TGS}$ and $\text{Ticket}_{TGS}$ in its **credential cache** (`/tmp/krb5cc_1000` on Linux). - The user has now logged in; the password is **discarded from memory**. --- #### PHASE 2 — The Service Authentication Exchange (Dialogue 2) **Step 3: Client → TGS** The client wants to access server V (e.g., the `krbtgt/LDAP@REALM`). First, the client builds an **Authenticator**: $$ A_{C,TGS} = E_{K_{C,TGS}}[ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert TS_3] $$ Then sends: $$ C \to TGS : \quad ID_V \Vert \text{Ticket}_{TGS} \Vert A_{C,TGS} $$ **Logic of authenticator vs ticket:** | Aspect | Authenticator | Ticket | |--------|---------------|--------| | Encryption key | $K_{C,TGS}$ (client knows) | $K_{TGS}$ (client does NOT know) | | Purpose | Proves client has $K_{C,TGS}$ right now | Establishes shared session key | | Lifetime | Single use | Entire validity window | | Contains $TS_3$? | Yes (freshness) | Yes (issuance) | | Contains $AD_C$? | Yes (replay) | Yes (binding) | **Step 4: TGS → Client** TGS decrypts $\text{Ticket}_{TGS}$ with $K_{TGS}$ → recovers $K_{C,TGS}$. It then decrypts the authenticator with $K_{C,TGS}$ → confirms $ID_C$ and $AD_C$ match. TGS creates a **new session key** $K_{C,V}$ and a **service ticket**: $$ \text{Ticket}_V = E_{K_V}[K_{C,V} \Vert ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert ID_V \Vert TS_4 \Vert \text{Lifetime}_4] $$ $$ TGS \to C : \quad E_{K_{C,TGS}}[K_{C,V} \Vert ID_V \Vert TS_4 \Vert \text{Ticket}_V] $$ The outer envelope is encrypted with $K_{C,TGS}$ so **only the legitimate client** can recover $K_{C,V}$ and $\text{Ticket}_V$. --- #### PHASE 3 — The Service Access Exchange (Dialogue 3) **Step 5: Client → Application Server V** Build a new authenticator using $K_{C,V}$: $$ A_{C,V} = E_{K_{C,V}}[ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert TS_5] $$ $$ C \to V : \quad \text{Ticket}_V \Vert A_{C,V} $$ **Step 6: V → Client — Mutual Authentication** V decrypts $\text{Ticket}_V$ with $K_V$ → recovers $K_{C,V}$. Decrypts $A_{C,V}$ with $K_{C,V}$ → confirms $ID_C$ and $AD_C$ match. To prove its own identity: $$ V \to C : \quad E_{K_{C,V}}[TS_5 + 1] $$ **Logic of the $TS_5 + 1$:** - Only an entity that knows $K_{C,V}$ could have recovered $TS_5$ and incremented it. - The increment (rather than echo) prevents a reflection attack where an attacker could simply replay the timestamp back. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Kerberos V4 Security Properties | Threat | How V4 Defends | |--------|----------------| | **Eavesdropping** | Tickets encrypted with server's long-term key; authenticators encrypted with session key | | **Replay of ticket** | Bound to $AD_C$ (network address) | | **Replay of authenticator** | Single-use, contains $TS_5$ inside its validity window | | **Password theft at AS** | $K_C$ is a hash; password is not transmitted | | **Forged ticket** | Attacker doesn't know $K_{TGS}$ or $K_V$ | | **Replay of old TGT** | Time-stamped, restricted to 8-hour lifetime | | **Clock skew** | Tolerated within a few minutes; out-of-window authenticators rejected | ### 3.3 V4 Weaknesses (Why V5 Exists) | Weakness | Description | |----------|-------------| | **TGT double-encryption** | The TGT is encrypted twice: once by $K_C$ and once by $K_{TGS}$. Wasteful. | | **PCBC weak encryption mode** | V4 used Propagating CBC, which has a known vulnerability. | | **No Internet scalability** | V4 supports only DES, no address-type field (IPv6 fails). | | **Session key reuse** | $K_{C,TGS}$ used for many TGS requests — attacker can mount offline dictionary attack. | | **No delegation** | V4 cannot forward credentials for middle-tier services. | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Kerberos V4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph USER["User Workstation"] direction TB C1["Client Process C"] CC["Kerberos Credential Cache (TGT + Session Keys)"] end subgraph REALM["Kerberos Realm (Trusted KDC Zone)"] direction TB DB[("Principal Database
(Password Hashes, IDs)")] AS["Authentication Server AS
Key: K_C (per user)"] TGS["Ticket Granting Server TGS
Key: K_TGS"] end subgraph APPSRV["Application Server Zone"] V["Service Server V
(e.g. Mail, File, Print)
Key: K_V"] end C1 -- "1. ID_C || ID_TGS || TS1" --> AS AS -- "2. E_Kc[Kc,tgs || Ticket_TGS]" --> C1 C1 -. "stores in cache" .-> CC C1 -- "3. Ticket_TGS || Auth_C,TGS || ID_V" --> TGS TGS -- "4. E_Kc,tgs[Kc,v || Ticket_V]" --> C1 C1 -- "5. Ticket_V || Auth_C,V" --> V V -- "6. E_Kc,v[TS5+1]" --> C1 AS -. "looks up K_C" .-> DB TGS -. "verifies Ticket_TGS" .-> DB V -. "verifies Ticket_V" .-> DB style AS fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style TGS fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style V fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C1 fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000 style DB fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Kerberos V4 — Ticket & Authenticator Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TKT["Ticket_TGS (encrypted with K_TGS)"] T1["K_C,TGS"] T2["ID_C"] T3["AD_C"] T4["ID_TGS"] T5["TS_2"] T6["Lifetime_2"] T1 --- T2 --- T3 --- T4 --- T5 --- T6 end subgraph AUT["Authenticator (encrypted with K_C,TGS)"] A1["ID_C"] A2["AD_C"] A3["TS_3"] A1 --- A2 --- A3 end TKT -. "issued by AS" .-> CACHE[(Client Cache)] AUT -. "built by Client per request" .-> CACHE CACHE -- "Step 3 send" --> TGS_NODE{{TGS}} TGS_NODE -- "decrypts TKT with K_TGS" --> K1[Recover K_C,TGS] TGS_NODE -- "decrypts AUT with K_C,TGS" --> K2[Verify ID_C + AD_C + TS_3] style TKT fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style AUT fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style CACHE fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a style TGS_NODE fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Kerberos V4 — Needham-Schroeder Predecessor ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant A as Alice (Client) participant KDC as Key Distribution Center participant B as Bob (Server) A->>KDC: ID_A || ID_B || N1 KDC-->>A: E_Ka[K_AB || ID_B || N1] || E_Kb[K_AB || ID_A] A->>B: E_Kb[K_AB || ID_A] B->>A: E_Kab[N2] A->>B: E_Kab[f(N2)] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]** **Differentiate clearly between a Kerberos Ticket and an Authenticator. Why is the Authenticator needed if the Ticket is already encrypted?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Aspect | Ticket | Authenticator | > |--------|--------|---------------| > | **Encryption Key** | Server's long-term key ($K_{TGS}$ or $K_V$) | Session key ($K_{C,TGS}$ or $K_{C,V}$) | > | **Client can read it?** | No (server's key unknown) | Yes (owns session key) | > | **Reusability** | Reusable within lifetime | Single-use | > | **Purpose** | Safely deliver session key to server | Prove client currently possesses session key | > > The ticket alone is **not sufficient** for authentication because it can be stolen and replayed by an attacker during its lifetime. The authenticator binds the *current* presentation to the client by including a **fresh timestamp** $TS_i$ that the server cross-checks against its clock. **(1 mark for table, 1 mark for ticket limitation, 1 mark for authenticator's role.)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Jul 2024 Style]** **What is a Kerberos Realm? Why is the concept of "realm" introduced in Version 4?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > A **Kerberos Realm** is an administrative domain consisting of one or more **Authentication Servers, Ticket Granting Servers**, and a database of principals (users and services) that share a common secret-key infrastructure. By convention, realm names are uppercase (e.g., `KTU.EDU`). > > The realm concept is introduced in V4 to **(1)** provide a clear **naming hierarchy** (user@REALM), **(2)** enable **scope of authority** for the KDC — tickets are valid only within the realm that issued them, and **(3)** allow **inter-realm authentication** by chaining cross-realm TGTs. Without realms, Kerberos would be a single global KDC — not scalable. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for scope, 1 mark for inter-realm scalability.)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Kerberos V4 Full Dialogue** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Kerberos Version 4 authentication dialogue** between a client C, Authentication Server (AS), Ticket Granting Server (TGS), and an application server V. Clearly show all the messages exchanged, indicating the encryption key used in each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **structure and purpose** of the **Ticket-Granting Ticket (TGT)** and the **Authenticator** in Kerberos V4. Show how mutual authentication is achieved in Dialogue 3. **(7 marks)** > **Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:** > > Refer to Section 3 above (Dialogue 1, 2, 3). Marks distribution: > - **2 marks** — Drawing the block diagram with C, AS, TGS, V and showing six messages > - **2 marks** — Listing the exact message format for each step > - **2 marks** — Specifying the encryption key for each message > - **1 mark** — Identifying the TGT and Service Ticket structure > **Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:** > > **TGT structure** (1.5 marks): > $$\text{Ticket}_{TGS} = E_{K_{TGS}}[K_{C,TGS} \Vert ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert ID_{TGS} \Vert TS_2 \Vert \text{Lifetime}_2]$$ > - Field-by-field explanation: $K_{C,TGS}$ (session key), $ID_C$ (client identity), $AD_C$ (network address binding), $ID_{TGS}$ (intended TGS), $TS_2$ (issue time), $\text{Lifetime}_2$ (8 hours typical). > > **Authenticator structure** (1.5 marks): > $$A_{C,TGS} = E_{K_{C,TGS}}[ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert TS_3]$$ > - Built freshly per request using the session key. Provides a "live" credential tied to the current time $TS_3$. > > **Purpose — Ticket vs Authenticator** (2 marks): > - Ticket: portable credential, can be cached and reused. > - Authenticator: prevents replay; must match ticket's $ID_C$ and $AD_C$. > > **Mutual Authentication in Dialogue 3** (2 marks): > - V receives $\text{Ticket}_V$ and $A_{C,V}$. V decrypts ticket with $K_V$, recovers $K_{C,V}$, then decrypts $A_{C,V}$. V returns $E_{K_{C,V}}[TS_5 + 1]$. Only a server that truly decrypted the ticket could know $K_{C,V}$ and respond. The "**+1**" prevents reflection attacks (a man-in-the-middle cannot simply echo the timestamp). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Needham-Schroeder & Limitations of V4** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** **(a)** Describe the **Needham-Schroeder Symmetric Key Protocol** with all six steps. Discuss the **replay attack** it is vulnerable to and how Kerberos V4 addresses it. **(7 marks)** **(b)** List and explain **four weaknesses of Kerberos Version 4** that motivated the design of Version 5. **(7 marks — each weakness: 1.5 marks; plus 1 mark for coherent summary)** > **Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:** > > **Needham-Schroeder Protocol** (3.5 marks — full six-step listing from Section 2.1, with encryption keys specified). > > **Replay Vulnerability** (2 marks): > - The original Needham-Schroeder protocol does **not** use timestamps. An attacker who captures message 5 ($A \to B: E_{K_{AB}}[N_2]$) and message 6 ($B \to A: E_{K_{AB}}[f(N_2)]$) can replay them later. The classic **Denning-Sacco attack** (1981) showed that if an old session key $K_{AB}$ is ever compromised, all past and future sessions using tickets from that key are compromised. > > **How Kerberos V4 addresses it** (1.5 marks): > - Kerberos V4 introduces **timestamps** $TS_i$ inside authenticators, and tickets carry **lifetime** fields. Servers maintain a **clock-skew window** (typically 5 minutes). Replays outside this window are rejected. Additionally, tickets are **bound to the client's IP address** $AD_C$. > **Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:** > > 1. **Double-encrypted TGT** (1.5 marks) — The TGT in step 2 is encrypted twice (outer $K_C$, inner $K_{TGS}$). Redundant and inefficient. V5 encrypts only once. > 2. **PCBC encryption mode** (1.5 marks) — V4 used Propagating Cipher Block Chaining, which has a known integrity weakness. V5 mandates standard CBC with separate integrity. > 3. **No address-type flexibility** (1.5 marks) — V4 hard-codes IPv4 addresses in $AD_C$. V5 introduces a typed-address field supporting IPv6, ATM, etc. > 4. **No delegation / proxy** (1.5 marks) — V4 cannot issue tickets for a *third party* (e.g., a print server that needs to access a file server on the user's behalf). V5 introduces **proxy tickets** and **forwardable tickets**. > 5. **Summary** (1 mark) — These limitations made V4 unsuitable for Internet-scale and modern protocol stacks, leading to the IETF standardisation of V5 (RFC 1510, later RFC 4120). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the "Ticket is opaque to client" rule** — A common error is describing the client as if it can read the ticket contents. The ticket is encrypted with the **server's** key, so the client **cannot** decrypt it; it only forwards it. > 2. **Confusing timestamps with nonces** — A *nonce* is a random number with no time meaning; a *timestamp* is a clock value used to enforce freshness. V4 uses **timestamps**, not nonces, in the authenticator. > 3. **Skipping the "mutual authentication" step (Step 6)** — Many answers stop at Step 5. Step 6 ($V \to C: E_{K_{C,V}}[TS_5+1]$) is what upgrades one-way authentication to mutual. Examiners will deduct **2 marks** for omitting it. > 4. **Missing the role of $AD_C$** — The client network address binds the ticket to a specific host. Without it, a stolen TGT could be replayed from a different machine. > 5. **Writing $E_{K_{AB}}$ in Kerberos messages** — Kerberos uses **multiple keys** ($K_C, K_{TGS}, K_V, K_{C,TGS}, K_{C,V}$). Writing $K_{AB}$ in Kerberos notation confuses session and long-term keys. Use the exact subscripts. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Page Rapid Revision — Kerberos V4** - ✅ **One-Way Authentication** = prover authenticates to verifier; verifier does not reciprocate. - ✅ **Needham-Schroeder (1978)** = first formal symmetric-key authentication protocol using a trusted KDC; uses nonces. - ✅ **Kerberos = Needham-Schroeder + Timestamps + Multi-tier TGS**. - ✅ **Three Logical Servers**: AS (password check), TGS (service-ticket issuer), V (actual resource). - ✅ **TGT** = Ticket-Granting Ticket, issued by AS, used to request service tickets without re-authenticating. - ✅ **Two Key Types**: *Long-term keys* ($K_C, K_{TGS}, K_V$); *Session keys* ($K_{C,TGS}, K_{C,V}$). - ✅ **Two Credential Types**: *Ticket* (reusable, sealed under server's key); *Authenticator* (single-use, sealed under session key, contains timestamp). - ✅ **Mutual Authentication** = Step 6 in V4 dialogue: $E_{K_{C,V}}[TS_5 + 1]$. - ✅ **Replay Defense** = Timestamps + Lifetime + Address binding $AD_C$. - ✅ **Standard port** for Kerberos = **88/UDP** and **88/TCP**. - ✅ **Default port of KDC** = **88**; **kpasswd** = **464**. - ✅ **Kerberos V5 standardisation** = RFC 1510 (1993) → **RFC 4120 (2005)**. - ✅ **V4 weaknesses to memorise** = double-encryption, PCBC, no address-type, no delegation, no proxy. - ✅ **Key Equation to Memorise**: $$ \boxed{\text{Ticket}_V = E_{K_V}[K_{C,V} \Vert ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert ID_V \Vert TS_4 \Vert \text{Lifetime}_4]} $$ $$ \boxed{A_{C,V} = E_{K_{C,V}}[ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert TS_5]} $$ - ✅ **Exam-Safe Mnemonic** — **"A-T-A"** = **A**uthenticator + **T**icket + **A**ddress-binding = full authentication.

topics.title: Kerberos Version 5, X.509 Authentication Service, Public Key Infrastructure (PKI), Trust Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kerberos Version 5, X.509 Authentication Service, Public Key Infrastructure (PKI), Trust Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Kerberos Version 5 (Kerberos V5)

**Formal Definition:**
Kerberos Version 5 is a widely deployed **trusted-third-party network authentication protocol** that uses a symmetric-key cryptography architecture based on **Needham-Schroeder protocol** to authenticate clients and servers in an insecure open network environment. It is defined in **RFC 4120** and is the standardized version that replaced the older Kerberos Version 4 (RFC 1510) with significant improvements in cryptographic flexibility, ticket structure, and cross-realm operability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Kerberos V5 is a 3-headed mythological dog guarding the gates of Hades. In network security, the "three heads" represent the **Client**, the **Authentication Server (AS)**, and the **Ticket Granting Server (TGS)** working together to verify identity.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Analogy:**

Imagine you are traveling internationally. You don't carry your original passport every time you board. Instead:
1. At the **check-in counter (AS)**, you show your passport and receive a **boarding pass for the airport itself (TGT)**.
2. At every **gate (TGS)**, you show your boarding pass, and the airline issues a **specific gate pass (Service Ticket)**.
3. When you finally board the **plane (Target Server)**, you present the gate pass — the flight attendant trusts it because it came from the trusted airport authority (KDC).

The boarding pass has your name, the issuer, validity time, and seat — this is exactly how a **Kerberos ticket** is structured. Without the trusted authority (airport authority), the system would fail. That's why Kerberos relies on the **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** — a logically centralized, physically distributed entity consisting of the **AS + TGS + Database**.

### 1.2 X.509 Authentication Service

**Formal Definition:**
X.509 is an **ITU-T standard** (Recommendation X.509, originally published in 1988 as part of the X.500 directory series) that defines the format of **public-key certificates** and a framework for **certificate-based authentication** in distributed systems. It is the foundational data structure that enables asymmetric cryptography to scale across the internet, forming the basis of the modern **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** An X.509 certificate is a digitally signed document that binds a **public key** to an **identity** (subject), enabling third parties to verify the binding by validating the digital signature of a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Passport Analogy:**

A passport is the perfect analogy for an X.509 certificate:
- **Your photo & name (Subject)** — bound to the document.
- **Government signature (CA's digital signature)** — proves authenticity.
- **Issuing country (Issuer DN)** — tells you who vouches for it.
- **Expiry date (Validity Period)** — bounds the trust window.
- **Machine-readable zone (Public Key + Extensions)** — the actual cryptographic material.

When you travel, a hotel in a foreign country trusts your passport not because they know you, but because they trust the government that issued it. This is **transitive trust**, the exact mechanism X.509 enables.

### 1.3 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)

**Formal Definition:**
PKI is the **comprehensive set of policies, procedures, hardware, software, and people** required to create, manage, distribute, use, store, and revoke **digital certificates** based on asymmetric cryptography. It provides the framework for **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation** in open networks, standardized through frameworks like **IETF PKIX (RFC 5280)**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Notary Public:**

A notary public doesn't create the law; they **certify** that the person who signed a document is who they claim to be. PKI works the same way — the CA is the "notary" of the digital world. Browsers (clients) don't know every website owner personally; they trust a chain of notaries (CA hierarchy) that say "yes, this key belongs to google.com."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory PKI Components (KPI High-Yield):** RA, CA, Certificate Repository, Certificate Revocation System, Key Recovery Server, PKI Clients, and PKI Management Protocols.

### 1.4 Trust Models

**Formal Definition:**
A **Trust Model** is the formalized architecture that defines the **rules, relationships, and policies** by which entities (users, organizations, services) establish and propagate trust in a PKI ecosystem. The four canonical models are: **Hierarchical, Cross-Certification (Mesh), Hybrid, and Web of Trust**.

**Conceptual Analogy — Diplomatic Relations:**

- **Hierarchical Trust** → A single King/Emperor at the top (Root CA), with dukes below (Sub-CAs). All trust flows down from the King.
- **Mesh/Cross-Certification** → Independent kingdoms that sign treaties with each other (peer-to-peer CAs).
- **Hybrid** → A combination of monarchy and treaties (most modern deployments).
- **Web of Trust** → A citizens' guild where anyone can vouch for anyone, common in PGP (Pretty Good Privacy).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Cutting Note:** All four systems (Kerberos V5, X.509, PKI, Trust Models) are NOT independent — they form a **layered trust architecture**: *Kerberos* handles **network login authentication*, *X.509 certificates* are the **credential format*, *PKI* is the **ecosystem that issues/manages* them, and *Trust Models* are the **governance policies* that decide who trusts whom.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Kerberos Version 5 — Operational Breakdown

Kerberos V5 is built around **three logical servers** and uses a **two-ticket** mechanism to minimize the use of the master secret key.

#### 2.1.1 Architectural Components

| Component | Symbol | Role |
|-----------|--------|------|
| **Client / User** | C | The principal requesting service |
| **Authentication Server** | AS | Verifies user identity; issues TGT |
| **Ticket Granting Server** | TGS | Issues service-granting tickets |
| **Target Server / Service** | V | The application the user wants to access |
| **Key Distribution Center** | KDC | Logical server = AS + TGS + DB |

#### 2.1.2 Kerberos V5 — Step-by-Step Operation

**Phase 1: Initial Authentication (AS Exchange)**

The client proves identity to the AS **once per login session**.

**Step 1: Client → AS** *(plaintext, no encryption)*
$$C \rightarrow AS: \text{ ID}_C \, \| \, \text{ID}_{tgs} \, \| \, \text{TS}_1$$

- `ID_C` = user identity
- `ID_tgs` = identity of the TGS
- `TS_1` = timestamp (replay protection)

**Step 2: AS → Client** *(two-part encrypted reply)*

$$AS \rightarrow C: \{ K_{C,TGS} \, \| \, \text{ID}_{tgs} \, \| \, \text{TS}_2 \, \| \, \text{Lifetime}_2 \, \| \, \text{Ticket}_{tgs} \}_{K_C}$$

Where the **TGT** is:

$$\text{Ticket}_{tgs} = \{ K_{C,TGS} \, \| \, \text{ID}_C \, \| \, AD_C \, \| \, \text{ID}_{tgs} \, \| \, \text{TS}_2 \, \| \, \text{Lifetime}_2 \}_{K_{tgs}}$$

- $K_C$ = client's secret key (derived from password)
- $K_{C,TGS}$ = **session key** between C and TGS
- $K_{tgs}$ = TGS's secret key (known to KDC only)
- $AD_C$ = network address of C
- The client **decrypts** the outer part using $K_C$ to extract $K_{C,TGS}$. It **cannot** decrypt the TGT (encrypted with $K_{tgs}$).

**Phase 2: Service Ticket Request (TGS Exchange)**

**Step 3: Client → TGS** *(authenticator + TGT)*

$$C \rightarrow TGS: \text{ID}_V \, \| \, \text{Ticket}_{tgs} \, \| \, \text{Authenticator}_C$$

Where:

$$\text{Authenticator}_C = \{ \text{ID}_C \, \| \, AD_C \, \| \, TS_3 \}_{K_{C,TGS}}$$

**Step 4: TGS → Client** *(service-granting ticket)*

$$TGS \rightarrow C: \{ K_{C,V} \, \| \, \text{ID}_V \, \| \, \text{TS}_4 \, \| \, \text{Ticket}_V \}_{K_{C,TGS}}$$

Where:

$$\text{Ticket}_V = \{ K_{C,V} \, \| \, \text{ID}_C \, \| \, AD_C \, \| \, \text{ID}_V \, \| \, \text{TS}_4 \, \| \, \text{Lifetime}_4 \}_{K_V}$$

**Phase 3: Client/Server Authentication (AP Exchange)**

**Step 5: Client → Server V**

$$C \rightarrow V: \text{Ticket}_V \, \| \, \text{Authenticator}'_C$$

**Step 6: Server → Client** *(mutual authentication — optional in v4, standard in v5)*

$$V \rightarrow C: \{ TS_5 + 1 \}_{K_{C,V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $TS_5 + 1$?** This proves to the client that the server indeed possesses $K_{C,V}$ — meaning the ticket was real, not a replay. This mutual authentication is one of the key v4→v5 upgrades.

#### 2.1.3 Key Improvements: Kerberos V4 vs V5

| Feature | Kerberos V4 | Kerberos V5 |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| **Encryption System** | DES only (56-bit) | **Pluggable (AES, 3DES, RC4, etc.)** |
| **Address in Ticket** | Fixed (must match) | Optional, multiple addresses allowed |
| **Ticket Lifetime Encoding** | 8-bit (5-min units, max 21 hrs) | Arbitrary ASCII string |
| **Authentication Forwarding** | Not supported | **Supported (proxy tickets, forwarded TGTs)** |
| **Inter-Realm Authentication** | Complicated | **Simplified via ticket transitivity** |
| **Replay Protection** | Limited (fixed 5-min clock skew) | **Authenticator sequence numbers + replay cache** |
| **Pre-Authentication** | Not required | **Mandatory (prevents offline dictionary attacks)** |
| **Ticket Format** | Binary, fixed | **ASN.1 encoded (flexible)** |
| **Mutual Authentication** | Optional | **Standard in every exchange** |

### 2.2 X.509 — Certificate Format and Authentication

#### 2.2.1 X.509 Certificate Structure (X.509v3)

X.509v3 certificates follow a strict **ASN.1 DER (Abstract Syntax Notation One — Distinguished Encoding Rules)** structure:

**Certificate ::= SIGNED { SEQUENCE {**

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Version** | v1, v2, or **v3 (current standard, RFC 5280)** |
| **Serial Number** | Unique integer assigned by CA |
| **Signature Algorithm ID** | e.g., `sha256WithRSAEncryption` |
| **Issuer Name (DN)** | X.500 Distinguished Name of issuing CA |
| **Validity Period** | Not Before + Not After timestamps |
| **Subject Name (DN)** | Identity being certified |
| **Subject Public Key Info** | Algorithm OID + actual public key bits |
| **Issuer Unique ID** (v2/v3) | Optional |
| **Subject Unique ID** (v2/v3) | Optional |
| **Extensions** (v3 only) | Critical/Non-critical, e.g., Key Usage, SAN, CRL Distribution Points |

**} }**

Wrapped by the **Signature Algorithm Identifier** and the **CA's digital signature** over the entire DER-encoded body.

#### 2.2.2 X.509 Authentication Procedures (3 variants)

| Procedure | Direction | Mechanism | Use Case |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **One-Way Authentication** | A → B | 1 message: $\{ t_A, r_A, B, S_A, D_A[A] \}_{K_B}$ | Email, simple login |
| **Two-Way (Mutual) Authentication** | A ↔ B | Adds B's signed challenge response | TLS server auth |
| **Three-Way Authentication** | A ↔ B (3 messages) | Adds timestamp from B, prevents replay attacks | High-security sessions |

Where $t_A$ = timestamp, $r_A$ = nonce, $S_A$ = signature, $D_A[A]$ = data.

### 2.3 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) — The PKIX Model

The IETF **PKIX Working Group** standardized the PKI components in **RFC 5280** and related documents.

#### 2.3.1 PKIX Functional Architecture

| PKI Entity | Function |
|------------|----------|
| **End Entity (EE)** | User/device holding the certificate |
| **Certification Authority (CA)** | Signs and issues certificates |
| **Registration Authority (RA)** | Verifies identity of EE before CA signs |
| **CRL Issuer** | Publishes Certificate Revocation Lists |
| **Certificate Repository** | Database/Directory storing certs & CRLs (e.g., LDAP, OCSP responder) |
| **PKI Management Protocols** | CMP (Certificate Management Protocol), CMC, SCEP |
| **PKI Management Objects** | X.509 certs, CRLs, OCSP responses |

#### 2.3.2 Certificate Lifecycle

1. **Initialization** — Client generates key pair.
2. **Registration** — EE sends public key + identity proof to RA.
3. **Key Pair Generation** — Either by EE or CA.
4. **Certification** — CA signs and issues the certificate.
5. **Key Backup & Recovery** — For encrypted data recovery (escrow).
6. **Certificate Update** — Re-keying before expiry.
7. **Certificate Revocation** — Compromise, CA failure, etc.
8. **Expiration / Archival** — Long-term retention of past certs.

### 2.4 Trust Models

| Model | Topology | Trust Direction | Real-World Example |
|-------|----------|-----------------|--------------------|
| **Hierarchical** | Inverted Tree | Top-down from Root CA | U.S. DoD PKI, most enterprise PKIs |
| **Mesh / Cross-Certified** | Peer-to-peer graph | Bidirectional peer CAs | Federal PKI (FBCA bridges) |
| **Hybrid** | Combined (bridge CA + hierarchy) | Hierarchical + bridge-mediated | e-Governance, inter-bank PKI |
| **Web of Trust** | Decentralized, no root | User-to-user, PGP-style | PGP key signing parties, OpenPGP |

**Bridge CA Concept:** A Bridge CA is a special trust anchor that **does not issue certificates to end entities**. It cross-certifies with multiple hierarchical PKIs, enabling them to trust each other through a single root.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Constants

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|---------------|------------|---------------|
| $K_C$ | Client's master secret key | Derived from password via PBKDF2 |
| $K_{C,TGS}$ | Session key C↔TGS | 128-bit AES, 56-bit DES |
| $K_{C,V}$ | Session key C↔Server V | 128-bit AES, 56-bit DES |
| $K_{tgs}$, $K_V$ | Service secret keys | Symmetric, KDC-only |
| $AD_C$ | Client network address | IPv4/IPv6 |
| $TS_i$ | Timestamp | 5-min clock skew tolerance |
| $\text{Lifetime}_i$ | Ticket validity | Typical: 8–24 hours |
| $H(\cdot)$ | Hash function | SHA-256 minimum |
| $\text{Sig}_{CA}$ | CA digital signature | RSA-2048, ECDSA-P256 |
| $E_K[\cdot]$ | Encryption with key K | AES-CBC, AES-GCM |
| ASN.1 DER | X.509 encoding | Variable length |
| Certificate Serial | CA-issued integer | 64-bit, unique per CA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Application:** Every time you connect to a website via HTTPS, the entire X.509 → PKI → Trust Model chain executes in **under 100 milliseconds**: the browser validates the leaf certificate against an intermediate CA, which chains to a trusted root, and checks CRL/OCSP — this is what makes e-commerce viable at planetary scale.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Kerberos V5 — Mathematical Derivation of Session Key Generation

#### 3.1.1 Derivation: Why $K_{C,TGS}$ is Necessary

The security of Kerberos rests on **key separation** — no two sessions should reuse the same key. Let's derive why.

**Given:**
- $K_C$ = long-term secret of client (derived from password)
- $K_{tgs}$ = long-term secret of TGS
- Both are **persistent** and high-value targets.

**Goal:** Generate a short-lived session key $K_{C,TGS}$ for a single login session without ever transmitting the long-term keys.

**Step 1: Client computes password-based key**

The AS stores only the **hashed password**, not the password itself.

$$K_C = \text{PBKDF2}(\text{Password}_C, \text{salt}, n_{\text{iter}})$$

By **RFC 4120**, Kerberos V5 supports the **enc-encrypted** timestamp as pre-authentication, so:

$$\text{PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP} = E_{K_C}(TS_1)$$

**Step 2: Client decrypts AS response**

The client receives the AS_REPLY message:

$$M_{AS} = \{ K_{C,TGS} \, \| \, \text{ID}_{tgs} \, \| \, \text{TS}_2 \, \| \, \text{Lifetime}_2 \, \| \, \text{Ticket}_{tgs} \}_{K_C}$$

The client computes $K_C$ from the password, then decrypts:

$$\{ K_{C,TGS} \, \| \, \text{ID}_{tgs} \, \| \, \text{TS}_2 \, \| \, \text{Lifetime}_2 \, \| \, \text{Ticket}_{tgs} \} = D_{K_C}(M_{AS})$$

**Step 3: Client creates the Authenticator**

The client constructs an authenticator to prove possession of $K_{C,TGS}$:

$$\text{Authenticator}_C = \{ \text{ID}_C \, \| \, AD_C \, \| \, TS_3 \}_{K_{C,TGS}}$$

**Step 4: TGS validates the authenticator**

The TGS extracts $K_{C,TGS}$ from the TGT (using its own $K_{tgs}$):

$$K_{C,TGS} \leftarrow D_{K_{tgs}}(\text{Ticket}_{tgs})$$

It then decrypts the authenticator:

$$\{ \text{ID}_C \, \| \, AD_C \, \| \, TS_3 \} = D_{K_{C,TGS}}(\text{Authenticator}_C)$$

It verifies:
- $\text{ID}_C$ in TGT = $\text{ID}_C$ in authenticator
- $AD_C$ in TGT = $AD_C$ in authenticator
- $|TS_{\text{now}} - TS_3| \leq \Delta t_{\text{max}}$ (typically 5 minutes)
- Replay cache check: $TS_3$ not seen before.

**Conclusion:** Proof that the client holds $K_{C,TGS}$ proves the client was the intended recipient, since only the AS (or the TGS via $K_{tgs}$) can distribute $K_{C,TGS}$ to the legitimate client.

#### 3.1.2 Derivation: Mutual Authentication via $TS_5 + 1$

**Given:** The client sends $\text{Ticket}_V$ and $\text{Authenticator}'_C$ to server $V$.

**The server must prove to the client that it holds $K_{C,V}$.**

**Step 1:** $V$ extracts $K_{C,V}$ from $\text{Ticket}_V$ using its long-term key $K_V$:

$$K_{C,V} \leftarrow D_{K_V}(\text{Ticket}_V)$$

**Step 2:** $V$ decrypts the authenticator:

$$\{ \text{ID}_C \, \| \, AD_C \, \| \, TS_5 \} = D_{K_{C,V}}(\text{Authenticator}'_C)$$

**Step 3:** $V$ computes:

$$R_V = \{ TS_5 + 1 \}_{K_{C,V}}$$

**Step 4:** $V$ sends $R_V$ to the client.

**Step 5:** The client decrypts:

$$D_{K_{C,V}}(R_V) = TS_5 + 1$$

Since the original timestamp was $TS_5$, the client sees the value $TS_5 + 1$, confirming that $V$ has $K_{C,V}$.

**Mathematical justification of replay prevention:**

Suppose an attacker $E$ captures an old $(TS_5, R_V)$ pair from a previous session. The attacker replays it to $C$. However, the current session uses a **fresh $TS_5'$**. The check $D_{K_{C,V}}(R_E) = TS_5 + 1$ — if the attacker used a stale timestamp $TS_5^{\text{old}}$, the client would expect $TS_5' + 1$, not $TS_5^{\text{old}} + 1$. The mismatch is detected.

Also, the server's own replay cache stores the timestamp $TS_5$ for the duration $\text{Lifetime}_4$ to prevent the **same** authenticator from being reused against the **server** in replay attacks.

#### 3.1.3 Cross-Realm Authentication Derivation (Key Upgrade from v4)

In Kerberos V5, if a client in realm $A$ wants to access a server in realm $B$:

**Step 1:** Client $C$ requests a TGT for remote TGS $TGS_B$ from local TGS $TGS_A$.

**Step 2:** $TGS_A$ issues a **cross-realm TGT**:

$$\text{Ticket}_{TGS_B} = \{ K_{C,TGS_B} \, \| \, \text{ID}_C \, \| \, AD_C \, \| \, \text{ID}_{TGS_B} \, \| \, TS \, \| \, \text{Lifetime} \}_{K_{TGS_B}}$$

Where $K_{TGS_B}$ is a **shared inter-realm key** between realm $A$ and realm $B$ (a secret key shared only between the two TGSs).

**Step 3:** $C$ presents this to $TGS_B$, which decrypts it using the shared $K_{TGS_B}$.

**Step 4:** $TGS_B$ issues a service ticket for $V$ in realm $B$:

$$\text{Ticket}_V = \{ K_{C,V} \, \| \, \text{ID}_C \, \| \, AD_C \, \| \, \text{ID}_V \, \| \, TS' \, \| \, \text{Lifetime}' \}_{K_V}$$

**Key improvement over v4:** In V4, a separate shared key was needed for **every pair** of realms (n² keys for n realms). In V5, **transitive trust** is supported: Realm $A$ trusts $B$, $B$ trusts $C$, then $A$ can indirectly trust $C$ through a chain of cross-realm TGTs.

### 3.2 X.509 Certificate Verification — Mathematical Walkthrough

#### 3.2.1 Derivation: Certificate Path Validation (Chain Building)

**Given:** A user browser receives a leaf certificate $Cert_V$ from a web server, with a chain of intermediate certificates, and a list of trusted root CAs.

**Step 1: Subject-Issuer Chain Building**

The verifier iterates through the certificate chain:

$$\text{Issuer}(Cert_1) = \text{Subject}(Cert_2)$$
$$\text{Issuer}(Cert_2) = \text{Subject}(Cert_3)$$
$$\vdots$$
$$\text{Issuer}(Cert_{n-1}) = \text{Subject}(Cert_n)$$

where $Cert_n$ is signed by a **trusted root CA** in the local trust store.

**Step 2: Digital Signature Verification**

For each certificate $i$ in the chain, the verifier:

- Extracts the **signed portion** of $Cert_i$ (the TBSCertificate field).
- Computes the **hash**:

$$h_i = H(\text{TBSCertificate}_i)$$

- Decrypts the **signature** using the **public key** of the issuer of $Cert_i$ (i.e., the public key embedded in $Cert_{i+1}$):

$$h'_i = \text{Verify}_{PK_{i+1}}(\text{Sig}_{i})$$

- Checks: $h_i \stackrel{?}{=} h'_i$.

In RSA terms:

$$h_i \stackrel{?}{=} (\text{Sig}_i)^{e_i} \mod n_i$$

where $(e_i, n_i)$ is the RSA public key of the issuer.

**Step 3: Validity Period Check**

For each $Cert_i$:

$$T_{\text{notBefore},i} \leq T_{\text{now}} \leq T_{\text{notAfter},i}$$

**Step 4: Revocation Status Check**

Two methods:
- **CRL (Certificate Revocation List):** Download the issuer's CRL, check serial number.
- **OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol):** Query the OCSP responder, receive a signed status: `{ good, revoked, unknown }`.

**Step 5: Critical Extensions Check**

Any extension marked `critical` MUST be processed; if unrecognized, the certificate MUST be rejected.

**Verdict:** A certificate is **trusted** if and only if **all 5 steps** succeed.

#### 3.2.2 Derivation: Mutual Authentication via Three-Way X.509

**Three-Way Authentication** (prevents clock-skew dependence):

**Step 1: A → B**

$$A \rightarrow B: \text{Cert}_A, \; r_A, \; S_A(r_A, B, \text{Data}_A)$$

where $r_A$ = nonce from A, $S_A$ = A's signature.

**Step 2: B → A**

$$B \rightarrow A: \text{Cert}_B, \; r_B, \; S_B(r_A, r_B, A, \text{Data}_B)$$

Note: B includes A's nonce $r_A$ in its signature.

**Step 3: A → B**

$$A \rightarrow B: S_A(r_B, B)$$

A signs B's nonce $r_B$ plus $B$ to confirm receipt.

**Mathematical proof of mutual authentication:**

- A authenticates B because: B's signature includes $r_A$ (proving B received A's first message) AND $r_B$ (proving B generated a fresh value, indicating liveness).
- B authenticates A because: A's third message signs $r_B$ (proving A received B's second message) AND $r_B$ was generated by B (which A couldn't predict without prior knowledge of B's randomness).

This works **without** synchronized clocks — only nonces are used, making it superior to the two-way variant when clock skew is an issue.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Kerberos V4 | Kerberos V5 | X.509 v1/v2 | X.509 v3 | PKIX PKI | OpenPGP Web of Trust |
|---------|-------------|-------------|-------------|----------|----------|---------------------|
| **Crypto Basis** | Symmetric | Symmetric | Asymmetric | Asymmetric | Asymmetric | Asymmetric |
| **Central Authority Required** | Yes (KDC) | Yes (KDC) | Yes (CA) | Yes (CA) | Yes (CA) | No (peer-based) |
| **Certificate Structure** | Fixed binary | ASN.1 | Fixed | Extensible | X.509 + Extensions | PGP packets |
| **Replay Protection** | Time + Cache | Time + Cache + Nonce | Timestamp | Timestamp + Nonce | CRL + OCSP | Trust signatures + Key revocation |
| **Scalability** | Medium | High (cross-realm) | Low | High (hierarchy) | Very High | Low–Medium |
| **Pre-Auth** | No | **Yes (mandatory in practice)** | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A |
| **Standards Body** | MIT Project Athena | **IETF (RFC 4120)** | ITU-T X.509 | **IETF (RFC 5280)** | **IETF PKIX WG** | OpenPGP (RFC 4880) |
| **Default Algorithm** | DES | **AES-256 (modern)** | RSA | RSA / ECDSA | RSA-2048 / P-256 | RSA / DSA / Ed25519 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Kerberos V5 — Full Authentication Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client C
    participant AS as Authentication Server
    participant TGS as Ticket Granting Server
    participant V as Service Server V
    participant KDC as KDC Database

    Note over C,AS: PHASE 1: Authentication Service Exchange
    C->>AS: ID_C, ID_tgs, TS_1
    AS->>KDC: Lookup K_C
    KDC-->>AS: K_C retrieved
    AS->>C: {K_C,TGS, ID_tgs, TS_2, Lifetime_2, TGT}K_C

    Note over C,TGS: PHASE 2: Ticket Granting Service Exchange
    C->>TGS: ID_V, TGT, Authenticator_C
    TGS->>TGS: Decrypt TGT, verify Authenticator
    TGS-->>C: {K_C,V, ID_V, TS_4, Ticket_V}K_C,TGS

    Note over C,V: PHASE 3: Client/Server Authentication
    C->>V: Ticket_V, Authenticator_C
    V->>V: Decrypt Ticket_V, verify Authenticator
    V-->>C: {TS_5 + 1}K_C,V

    Note over C,V: MUTUAL AUTHENTICATION COMPLETE
```

### 4.2 X.509 Certificate Verification Chain (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Browser] --> B[Receive Leaf Cert from Server V]
    B --> C[Build Chain: Issuer matches Subject?]
    C --> D{Chain ends at Trusted Root?}
    D -- No --> E[Verification FAILED]
    D -- Yes --> F[Verify Each Signature]
    F --> G{All signatures valid?}
    G -- No --> E
    G -- Yes --> H[Check Validity Period]
    H --> I{Not expired?}
    I -- No --> E
    I -- Yes --> J[Check Revocation Status]
    J --> K{OCSP good or not revoked in CRL?}
    K -- No --> E
    K -- Yes --> L[Process Critical Extensions]
    L --> M{All critical extensions understood?}
    M -- No --> E
    M -- Yes --> N[TRUST ESTABLISHED - TLS Handshake Proceeds]
```

### 4.3 PKIX Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EE[End Entity - User or Device] -->|1. Registration Request| RA
    RA[Registration Authority] -->|2. Verified Identity Proof| CA
    CA[Certification Authority] -->|3. Signed X.509 Certificate| EE
    CA -->|4. Publish Certificate| CR[Certificate Repository LDAP]
    CA -->|5. Publish CRL| CRL[CRL Distribution Point]
    OCSP[OCSP Responder] -->|6. Real-time Status| EE
    EE -->|Verify| CR
    EE -->|Verify Status| OCSP
    EE -->|Verify Status| CRL
    KMS[Key Management Server] -->|Recovery| EE
    KM[PKI Management - CMP CMC SCEP] -->|Protocol| CA
    KM -->|Protocol| RA
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        H1 --> H3[Intermediate CA B]

        H2 --> H5[End Entity A2]

    end

        M1 <-->|Cross-Cert| M3
    end

    subgraph HB[HYBRID MODEL with BRIDGE]
        B1[Bridge CA] <-->|Cross-Cert| B2[Hierarchy 1 Root]
        B1 <-->|Cross-Cert| B3[Hierarchy 2 Root]

    end

    subgraph W[WEB OF TRUST]

```

### 4.5 Kerberos V5 Message Exchange Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MSG1["Message 1: C → AS (Plaintext)"]
        M1A[ID_C - User ID]
        M1B[ID_tgs - Requested TGS]
        M1C[TS_1 - Timestamp]
    end

    subgraph MSG2["Message 2: AS → C (Encrypted with K_C)"]
        M2A[K_C,TGS - Session Key]
        M2B[TS_2 - Timestamp]
        M2C[Lifetime_2]
        M2D[Ticket_tgs - TGT encrypted with K_tgs]
    end

    subgraph MSG3["Message 3: C → TGS (Mixed)"]
        M3A[ID_V - Service ID]
        M3B[Ticket_tgs - Forwarded as-is]
        M3C[Authenticator_C - Encrypted with K_C,TGS]
    end

    subgraph MSG4["Message 4: TGS → C (Encrypted with K_C,TGS)"]
        M4A[K_C,V - New Session Key]
        M4B[ID_V]
        M4C[TS_4]
        M4D[Ticket_V - Encrypted with K_V]
    end

    subgraph MSG5["Message 5: C → V (Mixed)"]
        M5A[Ticket_V]
        M5B[Authenticator_C - Encrypted with K_C,V]
    end

    subgraph MSG6["Message 6: V → C (Mutual Auth)"]
        M6A[TS_5 + 1 - Encrypted with K_C,V]
    end
```

### 4.6 PKI Certificate Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init: Key Pair Generation
    Init --> Registration: Submit PK + Identity Proof to RA
    Registration --> Certification: CA signs X.509
    Certification --> Active: Cert distributed and used
    Active --> Update: Re-key before expiry
    Active --> Revoked: Key compromise / CA failure
    Active --> Expired: Validity period over
    Update --> Certification
    Revoked --> [*]
    Expired --> Archived: Long-term storage
    Archived --> [*]
```
