| **Payload Confidentiality** | Not provided | Provided via DES, 3DES, AES, ChaCha20 |
| **Authentication Algorithm** | HMAC-MD5-96, HMAC-SHA-1-96, AES-XCBC-MAC-96, HMAC-SHA-256-128 | Same set as AH |
| **Encryption Algorithm** | None (no encryption at all) | DES, 3DES, AES-CBC, AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Insight:** A subtle but examinable distinction is that AH authenticates **invariant fields of the outer IP header** (e.g., source/destination IP, payload length, identification), while ESP does NOT. This is why AH is theoretically stronger in integrity but practically weaker in real-world deployment because it conflicts with NAT.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AH Header Structure — Bit-Level Field Layout

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 6
    block: ahBlock
        columns 6
        A["Next Header 8 bits"] B["Payload Length 8 bits"] C["Reserved 16 bits"] D["SPI 32 bits"] E["Sequence Number 32 bits"] F["ICV Variable Length"]
    end
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Figure 1: Authentication Header (AH) Field Layout (RFC 4302).** The fixed header occupies 12 bytes, followed by the variable-length ICV aligned to a 32-bit boundary.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of AH in an IP Packet

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Outbound IP Packet] --> saLookupA{SAD Lookup}
    saLookupA -->|Match Found| seqGenA[Generate Next Sequence Number]
    saLookupA -->|No Match| dropA[Drop Packet]
    seqGenA --> zeroMutA[Zero Mutable Header Fields]
    zeroMutA --> icvCompA[Compute ICV using HMAC]
    icvCompA --> ahInsertA[Insert AH into Packet]
    ahInsertA --> transmitA[Transmit to Network]
    transmitA --> recvB[Receiver Gets Packet]
    recvB --> saLookupB{SAD Lookup via SPI}
    saLookupB -->|Match Found| replayCheckB{Anti Replay Check}
    saLookupB -->|No Match| dropB[Drop Packet]
    replayCheckB -->|Within Window| zeroMutB[Zero Mutable Header Fields]
    replayCheckB -->|Replay Detected| dropReplay[Drop and Log]
    zeroMutB --> icvCompB[Recompute ICV]
    icvCompB --> icvCmpB{ICV Match}
    icvCmpB -->|Yes| acceptB[Accept and Deliver to Upper Layer]
    icvCmpB -->|No| dropTamper[Drop and Log Integrity Failure]
    style startA fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style transmitA fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style acceptB fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dropReplay fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dropTamper fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Figure 2: End-to-End AH Processing Flow.** Outbound processing (left branch) and inbound verification (right branch) are shown with explicit decision points for cryptographic integrity and anti-replay.

## 4.3 Transport Mode vs. Tunnel Mode — Packet Structure Comparison

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 5
    block: tm
        columns 5
        T1["Orig IP Hdr"] T2["AH"] T3["TCP/UDP"] T4["Data"] T5["..."]
    end
    block: tnm
        columns 5
        N1["New Outer IP Hdr"] N2["AH"] N3["Orig IP Hdr"] N4["TCP/UDP"] N5["Data"]
    end
    style T1 fill:#FFE082,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T2 fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T3 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T4 fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T5 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N1 fill:#FFE082,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N2 fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N3 fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N4 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N5 fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Figure 3: AH Transport Mode (top) vs. AH Tunnel Mode (bottom).** Notice that tunnel mode prepends an entirely new IP header, encapsulating the original packet in its entirety. This is the mode of choice for VPN gateways.

## 4.4 Anti-Replay Sliding Window Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    leftEdge[Left Edge S left] --> window[Anti Replay Window Size 64]
    window --> rightEdge[Right Edge S right]
    newArrival[New Packet S n] --> decision{S n Position Check}
    decision -->|Greater than S right| slideRight[Slide Window Rightward]
    decision -->|Within Window| bitmapCheck{Bitmap Slot Empty}
    decision -->|Less than S left| dropOld[Drop Too Old]
    bitmapCheck -->|Yes| markBit[Mark as Received and Accept]
    bitmapCheck -->|No| dropReplay[Drop Replay]
    slideRight --> markBit
    style leftEdge fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style rightEdge fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style window fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style markBit fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dropOld fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dropReplay fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Figure 4: Sliding Window Anti-Replay Logic.** The default window size of 64 ensures the receiver can tolerate minor packet reordering while still detecting replays of older packets.

## 4.5 AH vs. ESP — Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{Security Requirement} --> q1{Need Confidentiality Encryption}
    q1 -->|NO| q2{Need IP Header Authentication}
    q1 -->|YES| esp[Use ESP Protocol]
    q2 -->|YES| ah[Use AH Protocol]
    q2 -->|NO| q3{Need Both Confidentiality and Header Authentication}
    q3 -->|YES| ahEsp[Use AH plus ESP Combined]
    q3 -->|NO| espOnly[Use ESP with NULL Encryption]
    style ah fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style esp fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ahEsp fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style espOnly fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Figure 5: Protocol Selection Decision Tree.** This flowchart guides a network engineer in choosing between AH, ESP, or a combined AH+ESP configuration based on cryptographic requirements.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the three security services provided by the IPsec Authentication Header (AH). Why is confidentiality absent from AH?**

**Model Answer:**

The IPsec Authentication Header (RFC 4302) provides three principal security services:

1. **Data Integrity:** AH guarantees that the IP packet has not been altered — intentionally or accidentally — during transit. This is achieved through a cryptographic Message Authentication Code (MAC) stored in the ICV field.
2. **Data Origin Authentication:** AH cryptographically verifies that the packet was indeed transmitted by the claimed source. This is achieved because the ICV is computed using a shared secret key known only to the legitimate endpoints.
3. **Anti-Replay Protection:** AH protects against an attacker capturing and resubmitting old packets by enforcing a monotonically increasing 32-bit Sequence Number combined with a receiver-side sliding window.

**Confidentiality is deliberately absent** from AH because its design philosophy separates the concerns of integrity and secrecy. AH focuses exclusively on proving authenticity and untampered state. If confidentiality is also required, IPsec mandates the use of the Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) protocol, either alone or in combination with AH.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the SPI (Security Parameters Index) field in the AH header? What is the size of the SPI field?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Security Parameters Index (SPI)** is a **32-bit** arbitrary value carried in the AH header. Its role is to uniquely identify, in conjunction with the destination IP address and the IPsec protocol type (51 for AH), the **Security Association (SA)** that the receiver must use to process the incoming packet.

When an AH-protected packet arrives, the receiver uses the triplet **(Destination IP, Protocol = 51, SPI)** to perform a lookup in its **Security Association Database (SAD)**. The matched SA provides the cryptographic key, the authentication algorithm, the anti-replay window state, and other security parameters necessary for verification. Without the SPI, the receiver would have no way to determine which shared secret and algorithm to use for ICV recomputation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an internal choice (either Question A or Question B). Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. Examiners expect step-by-step derivations, labeled diagrams, and crisp tabular comparisons.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Authentication Header (AH) format** as defined in RFC 4302. Describe each field in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Transport Mode and Tunnel Mode** of operation for AH. Use diagrams to illustrate the differences. **(7 Marks)**

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — AH Header Format (7 Marks)

The IPsec Authentication Header, as defined in **RFC 4302**, has the following structure:

| Field | Length (bits) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Next Header | 8 | Identifies the upper-layer protocol (e.g., 6 for TCP, 17 for UDP, 4 for IP-in-IP) that follows the AH. |
| Payload Length | 8 | Specifies the length of the AH in 4-byte words, **minus 2** to exclude the first 8 bytes. For example, a value of 4 indicates a 24-byte AH. |
| Reserved | 16 | Reserved for future use. Must be zero on transmission and ignored on reception. |
| SPI | 32 | Security Parameters Index. Used in combination with destination IP and protocol number 51 to identify the SA. |
| Sequence Number | 32 | A monotonically increasing counter for anti-replay. Initialized to 1 when the SA is created. |
| ICV | Variable | The Integrity Check Value — a variable-length MAC whose size depends on the chosen authentication algorithm. |

**Diagram (refer to Figure 1, Section 4.1):**

The first 12 bytes are fixed; the ICV is variable and aligned to a 32-bit boundary. Common ICV lengths include 96 bits (HMAC-MD5-96, HMAC-SHA-1-96) and 128 bits (HMAC-SHA-256-128).

**Key points to remember:**
- The **Payload Length** value uses the encoding $(L_{\text{AH}}/4) - 2$, not $L_{\text{AH}}/4$.
- The **Reserved** field is part of the fixed header.
- The ICV is computed over the **entire protected payload** (mutable IP header fields zeroed + AH + upper-layer data).

#### Part (b) — Transport Mode vs. Tunnel Mode (7 Marks)

**Transport Mode AH** is used primarily for **host-to-host** communication. The original IP header remains intact, and the AH is inserted between the IP header and the upper-layer payload:

```
[Original IP Header] [AH] [TCP/UDP Header + Data]
```

**Advantages:** Lower overhead (no extra IP header), preserves the original IP addressing visible to internal routers.

**Disadvantages:** Limited coverage — does not protect the original IP header's mutable fields in transit; cannot be used when the gateway is the IPsec endpoint.

**Tunnel Mode AH** is used for **gateway-to-gateway** VPNs or **host-to-gateway** VPNs. A new outer IP header is prepended, and the entire original IP packet is encapsulated:

```
[New Outer IP Header] [AH] [Original IP Header] [TCP/UDP Header + Data]
```

**Advantages:** Complete encapsulation; the entire original packet is authenticated; ideal for traversing untrusted networks.

**Disadvantages:** Higher header overhead (additional 20 or 40 bytes for the new IP header); may cause MTU issues requiring fragmentation.

**Diagram (refer to Figure 3, Section 4.3).**

| Criterion | Transport Mode | Tunnel Mode |
|---|---|---|
| New IP Header | No | Yes |
| Overhead | Low | High |
| Use Case | Host-to-host | Gateway-to-gateway VPN |
| Coverage | AH + payload + invariant IP fields | Entire original packet |

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`
**(a)** Compare and contrast **AH and ESP** in detail. Highlight the services, coverage, and operational differences. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **anti-replay mechanism** used by AH. With an example, show how the sliding window algorithm detects replayed packets. **(7 Marks)**

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — AH vs. ESP Comparison (7 Marks)

| Property | AH | ESP |
|---|---|---|
| RFC | 4302 | 4303 |
| IP Protocol Number | 51 | 50 |
| Integrity | Yes (over IP header + AH + payload) | Yes (over AH + payload only, not outer IP header) |
| Authentication | Yes | Yes |
| Confidentiality | **No** | **Yes** (DES, 3DES, AES, ChaCha20) |
| Anti-Replay | Yes | Yes |
| Header Authentication | Yes (covers invariant IP header fields) | No (outer IP header is excluded) |
| NAT Compatibility | Poor | Better (with NAT-T and ESP-NULL) |
| Algorithm Examples | HMAC-SHA-256, AES-XCBC | AES-CBC + HMAC-SHA-256, AES-GCM |

**Key Inference:** AH provides *wider* integrity coverage because it authenticates invariant IP header fields, but it lacks confidentiality. ESP provides confidentiality but does not authenticate the outer IP header. In high-security deployments, AH and ESP can be nested to provide all four services simultaneously.

#### Part (b) — Anti-Replay Mechanism (7 Marks)

The anti-replay mechanism uses a **32-bit Sequence Number** in the AH header, combined with a **receiver-side sliding window** of default size **64**.

**Working Principle:**

1. The sender assigns a strictly increasing sequence number to each packet transmitted under a given SA. The first packet has sequence number 1.
2. The receiver maintains a sliding window with a right edge $S_{\text{right}}$ (the highest sequence number seen) and a left edge $S_{\text{left}} = S_{\text{right}} - 64 + 1$.
3. When a new packet arrives with sequence number $S_n$, the receiver checks:
   - If $S_n > S_{\text{right}}$: slide the window right, accept the packet, update $S_{\text{right}} = S_n$.
   - If $S_{\text{left}} \leq S_n \leq S_{\text{right}}$: check the bitmap. If the bit is unset, accept; if set, **reject as replay**.
   - If $S_n < S_{\text{left}}$: reject as too old.

**Numerical Example (refer to Section 3.2):**
With $W = 64$ and $S_{\text{right}} = 1000$, a packet with $S_n = 970$ lies within the window and is accepted (assuming the bit is unset). A packet with $S_n = 950$ also lies within the window. A packet with $S_n = 1030$ causes the window to slide to the right.

**Diagram (refer to Figure 4, Section 4.4).**

The sliding window balances two goals: **detecting replays** of old packets while **tolerating minor reordering** caused by network congestion or routing changes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Stating that AH provides encryption:** This is the single most common error. AH provides ONLY integrity and authentication, NEVER confidentiality. Loss: 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Wrong Payload Length formula:** Writing $L_{\text{AH}} / 4$ instead of $(L_{\text{AH}} - 8) / 4$. Loss: 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing the IP Protocol Number:** AH = 51, ESP = 50, ICMP = 1, TCP = 6, UDP = 17. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention mutable header fields:** The ICV computation zeroes out TTL, Header Checksum, and ToS/DSCP before hashing. Forgetting this loses 1-2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing SPI and Sequence Number:** SPI identifies the SA; Sequence Number prevents replay. Mixing them up loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Missing the role of the SAD:** The receiver uses SPI + destination IP + protocol 51 to look up the SA in the SAD. Failing to mention the SAD loses 1-2 marks.
> 7. **No diagram in Transport vs. Tunnel mode question:** KTU examiners expect at least a labeled block diagram. Loss: 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Authentication Header (AH)**

- AH is an **IPsec security protocol** standardized in **RFC 4302**, assigned IP protocol number **51**.
- AH provides **three services**: **Data Integrity, Data Origin Authentication, and Anti-Replay**. It does **NOT** provide **Confidentiality**.
- The AH header consists of **six fields**: **Next Header (8), Payload Length (8), Reserved (16), SPI (32), Sequence Number (32), and ICV (variable)**.
- The fixed header portion is **12 bytes**; the ICV is variable and aligned to 32 bits.
- The **Payload Length** field value is $(L_{\text{AH}} - 8) / 4$, where $L_{\text{AH}}$ is the total AH length in bytes.
- The **SPI** uniquely identifies the Security Association when combined with the destination IP and protocol 51.
- The **Sequence Number** is a 32-bit monotonically increasing counter used for anti-replay; the first packet of an SA has Sequence Number = 1.
- The **ICV** is a Message Authentication Code (MAC) computed using a shared secret key. Common algorithms include **HMAC-MD5-96, HMAC-SHA-1-96, HMAC-SHA-256-128**, and **AES-XCBC-MAC-96**.
- During ICV computation and verification, **mutable IP header fields** (TTL, Header Checksum, ToS/DSCP) are **zeroed** to ensure consistent hashing at both endpoints.
- The **anti-replay sliding window** has a default size of **64**. The constraint is $S_{\text{right}} - 64 + 1 \leq S_n \leq S_{\text{right}}$.
- **Transport Mode** preserves the original IP header; **Tunnel Mode** adds a new outer IP header. Both modes authenticate the protected regions.
- AH authenticates **invariant fields of the outer IP header**, while **ESP does not**. This is AH's unique cryptographic advantage.
- The forgery probability for AH is $2^{-L_{\text{ICV}}}$, which is cryptographically negligible.
- AH is suitable for **routing protocol security (OSPFv3, BGP)**, **defense networks**, and **compliance-driven environments** where confidentiality is unnecessary.
- The most common **KTU comparison question** is AH vs. ESP — memorize the comparative matrix in Section 3.5.
- AH is **NAT-incompatible** because NAT mutates the IP header fields that AH authenticates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)** is a member of the IPsec protocol suite (defined in **RFC 4303**, obsoleting **RFC 2406**) that provides **confidentiality, data origin authentication, integrity, and anti-replay protection** for IP packets. It operates at the **network layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model and is identified in the IP header by protocol number **50**.

ESP secures a payload (the original inner IP datagram or upper-layer segment) by **encapsulating** it — the payload is encrypted, and an ESP header and trailer are wrapped around it. ESP is one of the two primary IPsec security protocols, the other being **Authentication Header (AH, Protocol 51)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are sending a **highly confidential legal document** through a regular postal service that is known to tamper with mail:

1. You place the document inside a **tamper-proof, locked steel briefcase** (this represents **ESP encryption + integrity**).
2. You glue a **wax seal with your unique company stamp** on the briefcase so the receiver can verify it really came from you (this represents **ESP authentication**).
3. You attach a **special numbered luggage tag** to the briefcase so that even if someone tries to send the same briefcase twice, the receiver can detect the duplicate (this represents the **anti-replay sequence number**).
4. You write the **final destination address** (inner IP) on a sealed envelope **inside** the briefcase — and a new shipping address on the outside (this represents **tunnel mode encapsulation**).

A postal worker (attacker) intercepting the briefcase sees only a sealed metal box, cannot read the document, cannot modify it without breaking the seal, cannot impersonate you without your stamp, and cannot replay a previous shipment because the tag number is one-of-a-kind.

## 1.3 Key Services Provided by ESP

| Service | Provided? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | ✅ Yes | Via symmetric encryption (DES, 3DES, AES, ChaCha20) |
| **Data Integrity** | ✅ Yes | Via HMAC-MD5, HMAC-SHA-1, HMAC-SHA-256, AES-GCM |
| **Data Origin Authentication** | ✅ Yes | Same hash-based mechanism as integrity |
| **Anti-Replay Protection** | ✅ Yes | 32-bit monotonically increasing Sequence Number |
| **Traffic Flow Confidentiality** | ✅ Yes (tunnel mode only) | By encrypting the outer IP header fields |
| **Connectionless Integrity** | ✅ Yes | Stronger than AH — covers more of the packet |
| **Access Control** | ✅ Yes | Through Security Association (SA) selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike **AH (Authentication Header)**, ESP can provide **all four** core security services (confidentiality + integrity + authentication + anti-replay). The most important differentiator is that **ESP supports encryption**, which AH does not.

## 1.4 Protocol Number & Standard References

- **IANA Assigned Protocol Number:** **50**
- **Current RFC:** **RFC 4303** (December 2005, S. Kent, IPsec Working Group)
- **Old RFC (now obsolete):** RFC 2406
- **Mandatory algorithms in IPsec v3:** AES-CBC (RFC 3602), HMAC-SHA-1 (RFC 2404), AES-GCM (RFC 4106)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The combination of **ESP + AH** in a single packet is **NOT** recommended by IETF (RFC 4303 §3.1) because it provides minimal additional security at significant performance cost.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ESP Packet Format — The Complete Layout

The ESP packet is processed in the following order (top-to-bottom on the wire):

```
 +---------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+
 |               |               |               |               |
 |   IP Header   |  ESP Header   |   Payload     |   ESP Trailer  |  ESP Auth   |
 |  (Outer/Inner)| (SPI + Seq#)  |  Data         | (Pad+Pad Len  |  Data       |
 |               |               | (Encrypted)   |  +Next Hdr)   | (ICV)       |
 +---------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+
 |<------- Authenticated (in Tunnel Mode all, in Transport mode excludes outer IP) ------->|
 |<----- Encrypted (Payload + Trailer) ----->|
```

The fields appear in this **transmission sequence**:

| # | Field | Size (bits) | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Security Parameters Index (SPI)** | **32** | Arbitrary 32-bit value that, together with destination IP and protocol (50), identifies the Security Association (SA). |
| 2 | **Sequence Number** | **32** | Monotonically increasing counter to prevent replay attacks. Starts at 1, must NEVER roll over. |
| 3 | **Payload Data** | Variable | The actual data being protected (upper-layer segment, or full inner IP datagram in tunnel mode). **Encrypted**. |
| 4 | **Padding (0–255 bytes)** | Variable | Required for block ciphers (e.g., AES-CBC needs 16-byte alignment) and to hide true payload length. |
| 5 | **Pad Length** | **8** | Number of preceding pad bytes (0–255). |
| 6 | **Next Header** | **8** | Identifies the type of data carried in the Payload Data (e.g., 4 = IPv4, 6 = TCP, 17 = UDP, 41 = IPv6). |
| 7 | **Integrity Check Value (ICV)** | Variable | Truncated MAC computed over ESP Header + Ciphertext + ESP Trailer. Length depends on algorithm (e.g., 96 bits for AES-GCM, 12 bytes for HMAC-SHA-1-96). |

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Processing

### **Sender-Side Processing**
1. **SA Lookup:** Outbound packet triggers SPD/SADB lookup → identifies SA → derives SPI, key, algorithms, mode.
2. **Construct Header:** Generate **Sequence Number** (init or increment); build ESP Header `{SPI, Seq#}`.
3. **Construct Trailer:** Append padding to align to cipher block size; add **Pad Length** and **Next Header** fields.
4. **Encrypt:** Apply negotiated symmetric encryption (e.g., AES-CBC with 128/192/256-bit key) over the **Payload Data + Padding + Pad Length + Next Header** together. *(In AEAD modes like GCM, this step is fused with integrity.)*
5. **Compute ICV:** Apply HMAC (or GMAC for GCM) over the **ESP Header + Encrypted Payload + ESP Trailer** using the integrity key. Append the resulting **ICV**.
6. **Encapsulate (Tunnel mode only):** Prepend a new outer IP header; in transport mode, the original IP header is retained and only the Protocol field is changed to **50**.

### **Receiver-Side Processing**
1. **SA Lookup:** Outer IP + SPI locates the inbound SA in SADB.
2. **Replay Check:** Verify received Seq# is **within the anti-replay window** (default size = 32 or 64 packets). If duplicate or too-old → drop.
3. **Verify ICV:** Recompute MAC over ESP Header + Ciphertext + Trailer; **constant-time compare** with received ICV. If mismatch → drop.
4. **Decrypt:** Apply decryption algorithm to recover plaintext {Payload + Padding + PadLen + NextHdr}.
5. **Strip Padding & Trailer:** Remove pad bytes, verify Next Header, reassemble original payload.
6. **Deliver:** Forward the decapsulated packet up the stack or to the tunnel endpoint.

## 2.3 ESP Modes of Operation

### **A. Transport Mode**
- ESP header is inserted **between the original IP header and the upper-layer payload**.
- Used for **host-to-host** communication where both ends implement IPsec.
- **Original IP header is preserved** (with Protocol = 50).
- Saves bandwidth (no new IP header added).

```
[Orig IP Hdr (Protocol=50)] [ESP Hdr] [Encrypted Payload + ESP Trailer] [ICV]
```

### **B. Tunnel Mode**
- The **entire original IP datagram is encapsulated** inside ESP.
- A **new outer IP header** is prepended; its addresses are the tunnel endpoints (e.g., security gateways).
- Used for **gateway-to-gateway** VPNs and **host-to-gateway** remote access.
- Provides full traffic-flow confidentiality (hides internal IP topology).

```
[Outer IP Hdr (Protocol=50)] [ESP Hdr] [Encrypted (Orig IP Hdr + Payload) + ESP Trailer] [ICV]
```

## 2.4 ESP vs. AH — The Critical Comparison

| Feature | ESP (Protocol 50) | AH (Protocol 51) |
|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality (Encryption) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Integrity | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Authentication | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Anti-Replay | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Coverage of IP Header Fields | Excludes mutable fields | Excludes mutable fields (less coverage) |
| NAT Traversal | ✅ Possible (with NAT-T, UDP 4500) | ❌ Broken (AH protects IP addresses) |
| Tunnel Mode Available | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Transport Mode Available | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Bandwidth Overhead | Slightly higher (encryption) | Slightly lower |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Protocol Number (ESP) | $P_{ESP} = 50$ | IANA IP Protocol Number |
| SPI Size | $\vert SPI \vert = 32$ | bits |
| Sequence Number Size | $\vert Seq\# \vert = 32$ | bits |
| Pad Length Field Size | $\vert PadLen \vert = 8$ | bits |
| Next Header Field Size | $\vert NextHdr \vert = 8$ | bits |
| Anti-Replay Window Size | $W \in \{32, 64, 128, \dots, 2^{32}-1\}$ | packets (default 32) |
| AES Block Size | $B_{AES} = 128$ | bits (always 16 bytes) |
| Padding Required | $P = (B - (L \mod B)) \mod B$ | bytes, for $L$ = payload length |
| ICV Size (HMAC-SHA-1-96) | $\vert ICV \vert = 96$ | bits |
| ICV Size (AES-GCM) | $\vert ICV \vert = 128$ | bits (recommended) |
| Sequence Number Wrap Limit | Must NEVER wrap in same SA | Anti-replay strict requirement |
| Key Size (AES-CBC) | $k \in \{128, 192, 256\}$ | bits |
| Outer IP Header Increment (Tunnel) | Adds $20$ (IPv4) or $40$ (IPv6) | bytes overhead |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where does ESP actually run in production?** It is the **workhorse of nearly every commercial VPN**: Cisco IPsec, OpenVPN (in IPsec mode), Microsoft DirectAccess, AWS Site-to-Site VPN, strongSwan, and LibreSwan. The modern IKEv2 (RFC 7296) negotiates ESP child SAs and prefers **AES-256-GCM-128** for combined encryption + integrity (AEAD).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Padding for Block Cipher Alignment

ESP supports arbitrary payload sizes, but block ciphers (e.g., **AES-CBC**) only operate on **fixed-size blocks** of $B$ bits.

**Given:**
- Cipher block size: $B$ bits
- Length of Payload Data (in bits): $L$ bits
- Desired: number of padding bytes $P$ such that the total $(L + P \cdot 8)$ is a multiple of $B$.

**Step 1:** Compute how many bits short the payload is of the next block boundary.

$$ r = (B - (L \mod B)) \mod B $$

**Step 2:** If $r = 0$, the payload is already aligned; we still need **at least 1 byte of padding** (per RFC 4303) so the receiver can unambiguously determine the end of payload.

$$ P_{bytes} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } r = 0 \\ \dfrac{r}{8} & \text{if } r > 0 \end{cases} $$

**Worked Example:** Suppose Payload Data is **37 bytes** (296 bits) and cipher is **AES-CBC** with $B = 128$ bits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 37 \text{ bytes} = 296 \text{ bits} \\
r &= (128 - (296 \mod 128)) \mod 128 \\
  &= (128 - (296 - 2 \cdot 128)) \mod 128 \\
  &= (128 - 40) \mod 128 \\
  &= 88 \text{ bits} \\
P_{bytes} &= 88 / 8 = 11 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Total bytes fed to cipher = $37 + 11 = 48$ bytes $= 384$ bits $= 3 \times 128$ bits. ✅ Aligned.

## 3.2 Derivation: Encrypted Region vs. Authenticated Region

The cryptographic processing in ESP is **non-overlapping** in a subtle way:

- **Encryption input:** $\{ \text{Payload Data} \Vert \text{Padding} \Vert \text{Pad Length} \Vert \text{Next Header} \}$
- **Authentication input:** $\{ \text{ESP Header} \Vert \text{Encrypted Payload + Trailer} \}$

> This is the **"encrypt-then-MAC"** pattern (preferred over MAC-then-encrypt). It prevents padding oracle attacks and provides IND-CCA2 security for the encryption.

Let $C$ denote ciphertext and $T$ denote the trailer fields.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= E_k(\text{Payload} \Vert \text{Pad} \Vert \text{PadLen} \Vert \text{NextHdr}) \\
ICV &= \text{MAC}_{k_a}(\text{SPI} \Vert \text{Seq\#} \Vert C)
\end{aligned}
$$

**On the wire:**

$$
\text{Frame} = \text{OuterIP} \Vert \underbrace{\text{SPI} \Vert \text{Seq\#}}_{\text{ESP Header}} \Vert C \Vert ICV
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Threat → ESP Defense Mechanism

| Threat | ESP Defense | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Eavesdropping (Sniffing)** | Confidentiality | Symmetric encryption (AES-CBC, AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) |
| **Tampering / Bit-flipping** | Integrity | HMAC over encrypted payload + headers |
| **Masquerading / IP Spoofing** | Data-origin Authentication | HMAC computed with sender's secret key |
| **Replay Attack** | Anti-Replay | 32-bit Sequence Number + Sliding Window |
| **Traffic Analysis (Flow)** | Tunnel Mode | New outer IP hides internal addressing |
| **Padding Oracle on CBC** | Encrypt-then-MAC (EtM) | Authentication is checked **before** decryption |
| **Known-Plaintext Attack** | Strong modern ciphers | AES-128/256 with cryptographically random IVs |
| **Cut-and-Paste Attack** | Sequence Number & ICV | Each packet has unique Seq#; modifications break ICV |

## 3.4 AEAD Mode (AES-GCM) — Modern ESP Variant

Modern ESP deployments strongly prefer **Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD)** modes that combine confidentiality and integrity in a **single** primitive.

For **AES-GCM-128** with 16-byte ICV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Auth Tag (ICV)} &= \text{GCM-Auth}_{k, \text{IV}}(\text{AAD} \Vert C) \\
C &= \text{GCM-Enc}_{k, \text{IV}}(\text{Payload} \Vert \text{Pad} \Vert \text{PadLen} \Vert \text{NextHdr}) \\
\text{AAD} &= \text{SPI} \Vert \text{Seq\#} \Vert \text{ESP Trailer fields}
\end{aligned}
$$

**IV/Nonce format for AES-GCM (RFC 4106):** 8-byte salt (from SPI/key derivation) + 4-byte Sequence Number = **12-byte IV**. This guarantees nonce uniqueness **iff the Sequence Number never wraps**.

## 3.5 Anti-Replay Sliding Window — Formal Check

The receiver maintains a window of size $W$. For received Sequence Number $N_{recv}$:

- If $N_{recv} > N_{max}$ (right edge) → accept, slide window right, mark received.
- If $(N_{max} - W) < N_{recv} \leq N_{max}$ (inside window) → accept **iff not already received**, else drop.
- If $N_{recv} \leq (N_{max} - W)$ (left of window) → **drop** (too old, possible replay).
- If $N_{recv} = 0$ → drop (reserved; ESP Seq# begins at 1).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ESP Header + Trailer + ICV Field Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Outer IP Header 20 or 40 bytes Protocol equals 50] --> B[ESP Header 8 bytes]
    B --> C[Payload Data Variable Encrypted]
    C --> D[Padding 0 to 255 bytes Encrypted]
    D --> E[Pad Length 1 byte Encrypted]
    E --> F[Next Header 1 byte Encrypted]
    F --> G[ICV 12 to 16 bytes NOT Encrypted]
    
    subgraph ENC["ENCRYPTED REGION (covers payload pad padlen nexthdr)"]
        C
        D
        E
        F
    end
    
    subgraph AUTH["AUTHENTICATED REGION (covers ESP Hdr + Encrypted Region)"]
        B
        C
        D
        E
        F
    end
```

## 4.2 ESP Sender vs. Receiver Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SEND["SENDER SIDE"]
        S1[Outbound IP Packet] --> S2[SPD and SADB Lookup]
        S2 --> S3{Select ESP SA}
        S3 --> S4[Generate or Increment Sequence Number]
        S4 --> S5[Build ESP Header SPI plus SeqNum]
        S5 --> S6[Add Padding and ESP Trailer]
        S6 --> S7[Encrypt Payload plus Trailer using Encryption Key]
        S7 --> S8[Compute ICV using Integrity Key over ESP Hdr plus Ciphertext]
        S8 --> S9[Prepend Outer IP Header Tunnel Mode]
        S9 --> S10[Transmit Encrypted ESP Packet]
    end
    
    subgraph RECV["RECEIVER SIDE"]
        R1[Receive ESP Packet] --> R2[Inspect SPI and Outer IP]
        R2 --> R3[Lookup Inbound SA in SADB]
        R3 --> R4[Check Sequence Number against Anti Replay Window]
        R4 --> R5{Valid SeqNum?}
        R5 -->|No| R6[Drop Packet Log Replay Attempt]
        R5 -->|Yes| R7[Recompute ICV and Constant Time Compare]
        R7 --> R8{ICV Match?}
        R8 -->|No| R9[Drop Packet Log Integrity Violation]
        R8 -->|Yes| R10[Decrypt Payload plus Trailer using Decryption Key]
        R10 --> R11[Strip Padding Parse Next Header Field]
        R11 --> R12[Deliver Decapsulated Payload to Upper Layer]
    end
```

## 4.3 ESP Transport Mode vs. Tunnel Mode — Capsule Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TM["TRANSPORT MODE (Host to Host)"]
        T1[Original IP Header Protocol becomes 50] --> T2[ESP Header SPI plus SeqNum]
        T2 --> T3[Payload Encrypted]
        T3 --> T4[ESP Trailer Encrypted]
        T4 --> T5[ICV Authenticated]
    end
    
    subgraph TUM["TUNNEL MODE (Gateway to Gateway)"]
        U1[NEW Outer IP Header Endpoints are Security Gateways] --> U2[ESP Header SPI plus SeqNum]
        U2 --> U3[Original IP Header Encrypted]
        U3 --> U4[Payload Encrypted]
        U4 --> U5[ESP Trailer Encrypted]
        U5 --> U6[ICV Authenticated]
    end
```

## 4.4 ESP in the IPsec Architecture — Protocol Stack

```mermaid
graph TB
    L7[Application Layer HTTP SMTP DNS] --> L4[Transport Layer TCP UDP]
    L4 --> ESP[ESP Protocol 50 Provides Encryption Integrity Authentication]
    ESP --> AH2[Optional AH Protocol 51 Generally Avoided with ESP]
    AH2 --> IP[IP Layer Routing and Forwarding]
    IP --> L1[Link Layer Ethernet WiFi]
    
    K1[IKEv2 Protocol 500 and 4500 UDP] -.->|Negotiates SAs and Keys| ESP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading aid:** IKEv2 (UDP port 500/4500) negotiates the ESP child SAs but is a **separate protocol**; the actual ESP packet is identified by **IP Protocol 50**, not by a UDP/TCP port.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**List any six key differences between ESP and AH in IPsec.**

**Model Answer (6 crisp points, 3 marks):**

| # | ESP (Protocol 50) | AH (Protocol 51) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Provides **confidentiality** (encryption) | Does **NOT** provide confidentiality |
| 2 | Integrity covers ESP Header + Payload + Trailer | Integrity covers **more** of the IP header (excluding mutable fields) |
| 3 | Works with **NAT traversal** (NAT-T, UDP 4500) | **Breaks** with NAT (AH authenticates IP addresses) |
| 4 | Preferred for modern VPNs | Rarely used standalone today |
| 5 | Operates on **payload + trailer** for encryption | Operates on **entire packet minus mutable fields** for integrity only |
| 6 | ICV computed over ESP Hdr + Ciphertext | ICV computed over IP Hdr + AH + Payload |

*(Award 0.5 mark per correct point; any 6 → 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, S7 CSE]**
**Explain the purpose of the Sequence Number field in ESP.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Sequence Number** is a **32-bit monotonically increasing counter** carried in the ESP header. Its purpose is to provide **anti-replay protection**.

- It begins at **1** when an SA is established (a value of 0 is reserved).
- The sender **increments** it for every ESP packet sent on the SA.
- The receiver maintains a **sliding window** (default size 32) of recently received sequence numbers and uses the Sequence Number to **reject duplicate or old packets**.
- The counter **must never wrap around** within the lifetime of an SA; if it is about to wrap, a new SA must be negotiated.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for identifying it as anti-replay, 1 mark for sliding window concept, 1 mark for wrap-around rule.)*

---

## **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

> KTU 2024 Scheme follows the **Internal Choice pattern**: answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

### **QUESTION A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** Describe the **ESP protocol architecture**, including the format of the ESP packet, with a neat diagram showing all fields. Explain the function of each field. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Transport mode and Tunnel mode** of ESP with diagrams and explain a **real-world VPN scenario** where tunnel mode is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**ESP Packet Format (ASCII Diagram, 2 marks):**

```
+---------------------+------------+------------+------------+
|  Outer IP Header    |  ESP Header|  Payload   |  ESP Trailer|
|  (Protocol = 50)    | (SPI+Seq#) | (Encrypted)| (Pad+PadLen|
|                     |            |            |  +NextHdr) |
+---------------------+------------+------------+------------+ <-ICV--+
|<----- Authenticated (everything except outer IP) ----->|
|<-------- Encrypted (Payload + Trailer) ----------->|
```

**Field-by-field description (5 marks):**

1. **Security Parameters Index (SPI) – 32 bits:** A pseudo-random value selected by the receiver that, combined with destination IP and protocol (50), uniquely identifies the **Security Association (SA)** for this packet. The receiver uses it to look up the correct SA in its SADB.
2. **Sequence Number – 32 bits:** Monotonically increasing counter for **anti-replay**. Starts at 1, must never wrap. Used with a sliding window at the receiver.
3. **Payload Data – Variable:** The actual data being transported. In tunnel mode this is a complete inner IP datagram; in transport mode it is a transport-layer segment (TCP/UDP). **Encrypted** using the negotiated algorithm.
4. **Padding – 0 to 255 bytes:** Added for two reasons — (i) to align the plaintext to the cipher's block boundary (e.g., 16 bytes for AES), and (ii) to hide the true length of the payload. **Encrypted**.
5. **Pad Length – 8 bits:** Indicates the number of padding bytes (0–255) preceding this field. **Encrypted**.
6. **Next Header – 8 bits:** Identifies the **type of data** in the Payload Data field (e.g., 4 = IPv4, 6 = TCP, 17 = UDP, 41 = IPv6). **Encrypted**.
7. **Integrity Check Value (ICV) – Variable:** A **truncated MAC** computed over the ESP Header, Ciphertext, and ESP Trailer. Length depends on algorithm (e.g., 96 bits for HMAC-SHA-1-96, 128 bits for AES-GCM). **NOT encrypted** but **authenticated**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Transport Mode (3 marks):**
- Inserts ESP between the **original IP header** and the **upper-layer payload**.
- Original IP header is preserved; only its **Protocol field is changed to 50**.
- No new IP header is added.
- Used for **end-to-end** host-to-host security where both endpoints are IPsec-aware.

**Tunnel Mode (2 marks):**
- The **entire original IP datagram** becomes the ESP payload.
- A **brand-new outer IP header** is prepended with the **tunnel endpoints' addresses** (typically the public IPs of the security gateways).
- The original inner IP addresses are hidden inside the encrypted payload.
- Used for **gateway-to-gateway** VPNs and **host-to-gateway** remote-access VPNs.

**Real-world VPN Scenario (2 marks):**
A **multi-branch enterprise** uses tunnel-mode ESP to connect its **Mumbai headquarters** to its **Delhi branch office** over the public Internet. Both branches have a **Cisco ISR router** acting as an IPsec security gateway. ESP in tunnel mode:
- Encrypts all internal traffic (including the private 10.x.x.x addressing scheme).
- Authenticates every packet originating from either gateway.
- Prevents attackers on the public Internet from learning internal IP topology (traffic-flow confidentiality).
- Hides the actual source/destination hosts (employees' PCs) from external observers — only the gateway IPs are visible.

---

### **QUESTION B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**
**(a)** Explain the **security services** offered by ESP. Why is ESP considered more powerful than AH? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the **ESP processing at the sender and receiver** sides. Show how encryption and integrity are applied. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Security services offered by ESP (5 marks):**

1. **Confidentiality:** ESP encrypts the payload (and ESP trailer) using symmetric algorithms such as **AES-CBC, AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305, 3DES**. Prevents eavesdropping.
2. **Data Integrity:** ESP computes a MAC over the ESP Header, ciphertext, and ESP Trailer. Any modification during transit is detected at the receiver.
3. **Data-Origin Authentication:** Because only the sender possesses the secret integrity key, a valid ICV proves the packet originated from the claimed sender.
4. **Anti-Replay:** The 32-bit sequence number combined with a sliding window on the receiver prevents attackers from resending captured packets.
5. **Traffic-Flow Confidentiality (Tunnel mode):** The original IP header is encrypted and replaced with a new outer IP header, hiding source/destination patterns.
6. **Limited Connectionless Integrity:** The ICV provides per-packet integrity without requiring a stateful connection.
7. **Access Control:** Packet processing is permitted only if a valid SA exists in the SADB (implicit access control).

**Why ESP is more powerful than AH (2 marks):**
- AH provides only integrity, authentication, and anti-replay — **NO encryption**.
- ESP provides **all of AH's services PLUS confidentiality**, making it strictly more capable.
- AH also fails with NAT (because it authenticates source/destination IP addresses), whereas ESP works through NAT using NAT-Traversal (RFC 3948).
- For these reasons, ESP is the **default choice** in modern deployments; AH is rarely used.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Sender-side processing (3.5 marks):**
1. Look up SA in SADB using destination IP + SPI.
2. Increment Sequence Number (init at 1).
3. Build ESP Header `{SPI, Seq#}`.
4. Append Padding + Pad Length + Next Header to the payload.
5. **Encrypt** the entire trailer-plus-payload region using the **encryption key** $K_e$ and the negotiated algorithm (e.g., AES-CBC with random IV).
6. Compute **ICV** = `HMAC(K_a, SPI ‖ Seq# ‖ Ciphertext)`.
7. Prepend outer IP header (Tunnel mode).
8. Transmit packet.

**Receiver-side processing (3.5 marks):**
1. Inspect outer IP + SPI → look up inbound SA in SADB.
2. **Anti-replay check** on Sequence Number against the sliding window; drop if invalid.
3. Recompute ICV over `SPI ‖ Seq# ‖ Ciphertext`; **constant-time compare** with received ICV. Drop if mismatch.
4. **Decrypt** the ciphertext using $K_e$ to recover `{Payload, Pad, PadLen, NextHdr}`.
5. Strip padding, parse Next Header to determine upper-layer protocol.
6. Deliver the plaintext payload to the appropriate upper-layer entity.

**Encryption vs. Integrity (apply-once note):**
- Encryption covers **Payload + Padding + Pad Length + Next Header**.
- Integrity covers **ESP Header (SPI+Seq#) + Ciphertext** — i.e., encryption-first then MAC (**Encrypt-then-MAC** pattern).
- ICV is appended **after** the encrypted block; it is **authenticated but not encrypted**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in ESP questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting that ICV is authenticated, NOT encrypted** — many students say "ICV is encrypted." Wrong! It is *appended* but is plaintext on the wire.
> 2. **Confusing SPI and Sequence Number** — SPI identifies the **SA** (static for the lifetime of an SA); Sequence Number **changes per packet** (anti-replay).
> 3. **Saying ESP "provides all AH services plus encryption"** without mentioning the **NAT-traversal advantage** — examiners expect both reasons.
> 4. **Mixing up mode diagrams** — In **Transport mode** the original IP header is *retained*; in **Tunnel mode** a *new outer IP header* is added and the original is *inside* the encrypted payload.
> 5. **Forgetting the Next Header field** — students often omit the Next Header in the trailer diagram. It is mandatory.
> 6. **Wrong protocol number** — ESP = **50**, AH = **51**. Confusing these costs 1 full mark.
> 7. **Writing "ESP = RFC 2406"** — that is the obsolete RFC. The **current standard is RFC 4303** (cite this for full credit).

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** 📌

- ✅ **ESP = IP Protocol 50** (current RFC **4303**). AH = Protocol 51.
- ✅ ESP provides **confidentiality + integrity + authentication + anti-replay** — strictly more than AH.
- ✅ ESP packet = `{Outer IP} + {SPI(32b) + Seq#(32b)} + {Encrypted Payload} + {Pad + PadLen(8b) + NextHdr(8b)} + {ICV}`.
- ✅ **SPI** identifies the SA; **Sequence Number** provides anti-replay; **ICV** is authenticated-but-not-encrypted.
- ✅ **Transport mode** for host-to-host; **Tunnel mode** for gateway-to-gateway VPNs.
- ✅ **Encrypt-then-MAC** is the secure ordering; ICV is computed over ESP Header + Ciphertext.
- ✅ **Padding** aligns to cipher block (e.g., 16 B for AES) and obscures true payload length.
- ✅ Preferred modern algorithms: **AES-256-GCM-128** (AEAD), **ChaCha20-Poly1305**, **HMAC-SHA-256**.
- ✅ ESP works **with NAT-T** (RFC 3948, UDP 4500); AH does not.
- ✅ Sequence Number starts at **1**, must **never wrap** within an SA — if near wrap, establish a new SA.
- ✅ RFC 4303 §3.1 recommends **against combining ESP + AH** in the same packet.
- ✅ IKEv2 (UDP 500/4500) negotiates the ESP child SA; ESP itself is **not** a UDP protocol.
- ✅ The `Next Header` field in the trailer tells the receiver the **protocol of the decrypted payload** (4 = IPv4, 6 = TCP, 17 = UDP, 41 = IPv6).
- ✅ The ICV size is **algorithm-dependent**: 96 bits (HMAC-SHA-1-96) or 128 bits (AES-GCM).
- ✅ **Remember** to cite the **current RFC (4303)** in exam answers, not the obsolete 2406.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combining Security Associations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Combining Security Associations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Association (SA) — KTU 2024 Definition**
> A **Security Association (SA)** is a simplex (one-way) logical connection between a sender and a receiver that provides security services to the traffic carried on it. It is uniquely identified by a triple: **(Destination IP Address, Security Protocol Identifier, Security Parameter Index — SPI)**.

A single SA provides only one type of security service. In real networks, an organisation may simultaneously require **confidentiality (encryption)**, **integrity**, and **authentication**. Because one SA cannot deliver all three at once, **multiple SAs must be combined** to form a *Security Association Bundle*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why combine SAs?**
> ESP provides encryption *and* optional integrity/authentication. AH provides integrity/authentication but **no encryption**. To get **all three services** (confidentiality + integrity + authentication) between two endpoints, an ESP SA and an AH SA must be combined into a single bundle applied to the same IP packet.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Secure Diplomatic Pouch"**
> Think of a diplomatic pouch carrying a confidential letter. To send it safely:
> 1. **First**, the letter is sealed in a tamper-proof bag (this is like **AH** — proves the contents were not altered, the bag is genuine, but anyone holding the bag can *see* the letter exists).
> 2. **Second**, the sealed bag is placed inside an opaque, locked briefcase (this is like **ESP** — hides the contents from any observer, even though they can see the briefcase exists).
> 3. Finally, the locked briefcase is sent through a private diplomatic channel (this is the **tunnel / transport mode** that bundles both SAs together).
> Just as you can choose the order in which you wrap the letter, in IPsec the **order of applying AH and ESP** is a deliberate engineering decision that affects both security strength and performance.

## 1.3 Key Parameters to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **SPI (Security Parameter Index):** a 32-bit value assigned to the SA, carried in the AH/ESP header.
> - **Destination IP Address:** specifies the *receiver* of the SA (SAs are simplex).
> - **Protocol:** `AH = 51`, `ESP = 50` (IANA assigned protocol numbers).
> - **SAD (Security Association Database):** stores all active SAs at a host/gateway.
> - **SPD (Security Policy Database):** stores rules deciding which traffic gets which SAs.
> - **Seq No (Anti-replay):** 32-bit counter, mandatory in IPv4, *recommendation* in IPv6 per RFC 4302/4303.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Possible Security Service Combinations

When two IPsec peers (call them Host A and Host B, or A and an intermediate Security Gateway SG) need to communicate, the following combinations are defined by **RFC 4303 (ESP)** and **RFC 4302 (AH)**:

1. **Case 1 — End-to-End Security (single SA)**
   - One SA, either AH or ESP, between the two *end hosts* only.
   - All security services applied between the endpoints.
   - Most lightweight option.

2. **Case 2 — End-to-End + Gateway-to-Gateway (nested SAs)**
   - An **inner SA** between the two end hosts (provides end-to-end service).
   - An **outer SA** between two intermediate Security Gateways (provides the tunnel).
   - This is the classic *tunnel mode* topology used in VPNs.

3. **Case 3 — Combined End-to-End (transport adjacency)**
   - More than one SA between the same two end hosts.
   - One SA uses AH, the other uses ESP.
   - SAs are applied **at the same layer** (adjacent), not nested.

## 2.2 Why Ordering Matters (Auth-then-Encrypt vs Encrypt-then-Auth)

A subtle but **examination-critical** point in KTU 2024 questions is the order in which the two SAs are applied to the same packet:

| Order | Construction | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport Adjacency: ESP (inner) + AH (outer)** | Encrypt first, then authenticate the *encrypted* packet | Integrity covers the ciphertext, defeating ciphertext-tampering attacks | Recipients must authenticate *before* they can decrypt; replay protection of encrypted payload is harder |
| **Transport Adjacency: AH (inner) + ESP (outer)** | Authenticate first, then encrypt | Inner data authenticated in plaintext form | An attacker can modify the *ciphertext* (integrity only protects the unencrypted IP/upper-layer headers) — generally **discouraged** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** In current IPsec practice, **encrypt-then-authenticate** (ESP-then-AH) is recommended, but the historical literature used **authenticate-then-encrypt** (AH-then-ESP). State both in your answer and justify.

## 2.3 SA Bundle — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SA Bundle:** A set of two or more SAs that are applied (sequentially) to the same IP datagram such that the protection offered is the *union* of the protections offered by the individual SAs.

The SPD entries in modern IPsec implementations (RFC 4301) allow a *bundle* to be referenced by a single selector, and the order is enforced by a `SA_BUNDLE` list.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Notation / Value | KTU Memory Trick |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SA Identifier triple | {Dest-IP, Protocol, SPI} | "**D**estination, **P**rotocol, **S**PI = DPS" |
| 2 | IANA Protocol — AH | **51** | "5 (AH) + 1 = 51" |
| 3 | IANA Protocol — ESP | **50** | "5 (ESP) + 0 = 50" |
| 4 | SPI length | **32 bits** | "S-P-I = **S**ixty-**P**lus-**I**nteger" — same size as IP seq |
| 5 | SAs per packet | **1 ≤ n ≤ k** (a small integer, typically ≤ 4) | "Bundle" — at least 2 SAs |
| 6 | SA direction | **Simplex (one-way)** | Need 2 SAs for a bidirectional session |
| 7 | Seq Number length | **32 bits** | Same as SPI |
| 8 | AH coverage | IP header + payload (excl. mutable fields) | "AH sees the whole IP packet" |
| 9 | ESP coverage | Payload only (in transport); whole inner IP (in tunnel) | "ESP protects only what comes after its header" |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough of SA Combination Cases

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked derivation walks through the exact *packet-construction* logic for each of the three combining cases. The "derivation" here is the **header nesting algebra** — i.e., which header wraps which — which is a common KTU long-answer requirement.

## 3.1 Case 1 — End-to-End Single SA (Reference Baseline)

Let an original IP datagram be denoted as:

$$P = \{H_{IP}, H_{UL}, D\}$$

where $H_{IP}$ = original IP header, $H_{UL}$ = upper-layer header (TCP/UDP), $D$ = application data.

**Transport Mode ESP applied (Case 1a):**

$$P_{\text{out}} = \{H_{IP}^*, E_{SPI}, E_{Seq}, E_{IV}, E_{Payload}, E_{Pad}, E_{PadLen}, E_{NH}, E_{ICV}\}$$

**Tunnel Mode ESP applied (Case 1b):**

$$P_{\text{out}} = \{H_{IP}^{new}, E_{SPI}, E_{Seq}, E_{IV}, E_{H_{IP}}, E_{H_{UL}}, E_{D}, E_{Pad}, E_{PadLen}, E_{NH}, E_{ICV}\}$$

**Justification:** Tunnel mode adds a *new* outer IP header so the original $H_{IP}$ is also encrypted; transport mode leaves $H_{IP}$ in the clear but adds an ESP header/trailer in place.

## 3.2 Case 2 — End-to-End SA *Inside* a Gateway-to-Gateway SA (Iterated / Nested SAs)

This is the canonical **VPN topology**. The construction is **iterative** — an inner ESP SA is encapsulated by an outer ESP SA that runs between two security gateways.

Let the inner end-to-end SA be $SA_{\text{e2e}}$ and the outer gateway-to-gateway SA be $SA_{\text{g2g}}$. The construction proceeds as:

**Step 1 — Build the inner protected packet using $SA_{\text{e2e}}$ (tunnel mode):**

$$P_1 = \{H_{IP}^{e2e}, E_{\text{inner}}[H_{IP}, H_{UL}, D]\}$$

**Step 2 — Treat $P_1$ as the new "plaintext" payload and apply $SA_{\text{g2g}}$ (tunnel mode) on top:**

$$P_{\text{final}} = \{H_{IP}^{g2g}, E_{\text{outer}}[P_1]\}$$

Expanding $P_1$:

$$P_{\text{final}} = \{H_{IP}^{g2g}, E_{\text{outer}}[\,H_{IP}^{e2e}, E_{\text{inner}}[H_{IP}, H_{UL}, D]\,]\}$$

**Step 3 — Verification (de-cap by receiver):**

The remote gateway first strips the outer ESP tunnel (using $SA_{\text{g2g}}^{-1}$), recovers $P_1$, and forwards it through the untrusted network. The destination host then strips the inner ESP tunnel (using $SA_{\text{e2e}}^{-1}$), recovering the original $\{H_{IP}, H_{UL}, D\}$.

**Boundary condition:** A third (rarely used) SA may be added *outside* $SA_{\text{g2g}}$ if the packet must traverse another security domain; KTU only expects the 2-level case.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation for derivation:** In Case 2 the two SAs are **non-adjacent** — the inner SA's protected packet becomes the plaintext of the outer SA. This is what makes it an *iterated* (nested) bundle rather than a *transport-adjacent* bundle.

## 3.3 Case 3 — Transport Adjacency (Two SAs at the Same Layer)

Here both SAs terminate at the same two endpoints, but one uses AH and the other uses ESP. They are applied **adjacently** (not nested).

**Sub-case 3a — AH (outer) + ESP (inner) — authenticate-then-encrypt:**

$$P_{\text{final}} = \{H_{IP}, A_{Hdr}, A_{ICV}, E_{Hdr}, E_{IV}, E[\dots], E_{Trailer}, E_{ICV}\}$$

- First ESP encrypts the upper-layer data → produces a payload that includes its own trailer and ICV.
- Then AH authenticates the IP header, the AH header, and the *entire* ESP-protected payload (including the ESP ICV).

**Sub-case 3b — ESP (outer) + AH (inner) — encrypt-then-authenticate:**

$$P_{\text{final}} = \{H_{IP}, E_{Hdr}, E_{IV}, E[\,A_{Hdr}, A_{ICV}, H_{UL}, D\,], E_{Trailer}, E_{ICV}\}$$

- First AH authenticates the upper-layer data → produces an AH header + ICV.
- Then ESP encrypts everything *from AH header onwards*, including the AH ICV.
- On the receiving side, the receiver must **authenticate first, then decrypt** (encrypt-then-authenticate) so an attacker cannot probe decryption oracles.

**Why order matters (worked check):**

> [!NOTE]
> Suppose an attacker flips one bit in the ESP-encrypted AH ICV. With **encrypt-then-authenticate**, the receiver's AH will catch the bit flip *before* attempting to decrypt further. With **authenticate-then-encrypt**, the receiver's AH cannot even see that bit flip because the ICV was computed over plaintext, so the attack may be detected only after a decryption failure. This is the **CBC-MAC-then-Encrypt vs Encrypt-then-CBC-MAC** debate, and it directly maps onto AH-then-ESP vs ESP-then-AH.

## 3.4 Summary Table of Header Order

| Topology | # of SAs | Adjacency | Outermost → Innermost Header Order |
|---|---|---|---|
| Case 1 (single SA) | 1 | — | IP → AH *or* IP → ESP |
| Case 2 (nested VPN) | 2 | Nested (different endpoints) | IP<sub>g2g</sub> → ESP<sub>outer</sub> → IP<sub>e2e</sub> → ESP<sub>inner</sub> |
| Case 3a (AH+ESP, auth-then-encrypt) | 2 | Transport adjacent | IP → AH → ESP |
| Case 3b (ESP+AH, encrypt-then-auth) | 2 | Transport adjacent | IP → ESP( AH + upper-layer ) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — SA Bundle Construction Logic

```mermaid
    sa1{Select SA Bundle?}:::dec

    c2b[Outer SA: Gateway-to-Gateway ESP]:::outer

    c3b[Sub-case 3b: ESP then AH]:::adj2
    out1[One IP header plus one protocol]:::done
    out2[Two nested IP headers plus two ESP]:::done
    out3[One IP header plus AH plus ESP]:::done
    orig --> sa1
    sa1 -->|one SA only| c1 --> out1
    sa1 -->|inner plus outer| c2
    sa1 -->|two SAs same endpoints| c3

    c3 --> c3a --> out3
    c3 --> c3b --> out3
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How SAD/SPD Interact with a Bundle

```mermaid

    spd[Security Policy Database SPD]:::dec
    selselector{Selector match?}:::dec
    sasingle[Use single SA]:::c1
    sabundle[SA Bundle list SA1 SA2 ... SAn]:::c2
    sad[Security Association Database SAD]:::dec
    proc[Process: ESP first then AH or AH first then ESP]:::adj1
    out[Forward protected packet]:::done
    pkt --> spd --> selselector
    selselector -->|single match| sasingle
    selselector -->|bundle match| sabundle
    sasingle --> sad
    sabundle --> sad --> proc --> out
```
