# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**
Define a *Security Association*. List the three parameters that uniquely identify an SA.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Security Association (SA)** is a one-way (simplex) logical relationship between a sender and a receiver that defines the security services to be applied to traffic flowing between them. An SA is the *contract* — keys, algorithms, lifetimes, anti-replay counter — that both ends must agree on before any IPsec packet can be processed. **(1 mark)**

The three parameters that uniquely identify an SA are:
1. **Destination IP Address** (of the receiver) — **(1 mark)**
2. **Security Protocol** identifier — either `AH (51)` or `ESP (50)` — **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Security Parameter Index (SPI)** — a 32-bit value chosen by the receiver, carried in the AH/ESP header — **(0.5 mark)**

Together they form the **SA Selector triple**.

---

### **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
Distinguish between *transport adjacency* and *iterated (nested) SAs*. Mention which endpoints terminate each SA in both cases.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Transport Adjacency | Iterated (Nested) SAs |
|---|---|---|
| Number of SAs | ≥ 2 (typically 2) | ≥ 2 |
| Endpoints of each SA | **Same** for every SA in the bundle (both terminate at the same two end hosts) | **Different** (e.g., inner SA = end-to-end, outer SA = gateway-to-gateway) |
| Nesting | SAs applied *adjacently* (side by side), not nested | Inner SA is fully *inside* the outer SA's protected region |
| Typical use | All-three-services end-to-end (conf + int + auth) | VPN with both end-to-end and gateway-level protection |

**(2 marks for the table; 1 mark for endpoint distinction.)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 × 14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the **three cases of combining security associations** as defined for IPsec. State, with justification, the *recommended ordering* of AH and ESP when both are used in transport adjacency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An organisation wants its branch-office gateway to provide a VPN tunnel to a head-office gateway, while still allowing the two end-hosts (one in each office) to have **end-to-end confidentiality, integrity, and authentication**. Design the SA bundle: identify the **number of SAs**, the **endpoints** of each, and the **mode** (transport/tunnel) of each. Show the final on-wire packet structure. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Three Cases of Combining Security Associations **(7 marks)**

**Case 1 — Single SA, end-to-end (1.5 marks)**
- One SA, either AH or ESP, between two end hosts only.
- Used when only integrity+auth (AH) or only confidentiality (ESP) is needed.
- Diagram: `[ IP | AH | Payload | ]` or `[ IP | ESP | Encrypted Payload | ESP-Trailer ]`.

**Case 2 — Nested / Iterated SAs (2 marks)**
- Two (or more) SAs, each terminating at a *different* pair of endpoints.
- Typical: outer SA between two security gateways (tunnel), inner SA between the two end hosts (also tunnel, or transport).
- The output of the inner SA is treated as the *plaintext payload* of the outer SA.
- Diagram (see Section 4.2 case 2):
  - `[ IP_GW | Outer-ESP | IP_E2E | Inner-ESP | Payload | Outer-ESP-Trailer ]`

**Case 3 — Transport Adjacency (2 marks)**
- Multiple SAs between the **same** two end hosts.
- E.g., one AH SA + one ESP SA, applied *adjacent* to each other.
- Two sub-cases: AH-then-ESP, ESP-then-AH.

**Recommended ordering — justification (1.5 marks)**
- **Recommended: ESP (outer) wrapping AH (inner)** — i.e., *encrypt-then-authenticate*.
- Justification:
  1. AH ICV is computed *before* encryption, so the receiver can authenticate the packet *without* first attempting any decryption. This blocks chosen-ciphertext attacks.
  2. If the ciphertext is tampered with, AH ICV mismatch is detected *before* the (possibly CPU-expensive) decryption is attempted, saving processing on bogus packets.
  3. Modern IPsec implementations (RFC 4301, 4303) prefer this direction for *transport adjacency*, although many legacy gateways still support the AH-then-ESP direction for backward compatibility.

#### Part (b) — Designing the SA Bundle for the Branch/Head Office VPN **(7 marks)**

**Requirement analysis (1 mark):**
- Gateway-to-gateway protection (a tunnel across the untrusted WAN).
- *Plus* end-to-end integrity + authentication.
- ESP alone at gateway level gives only gateway-level confidentiality; ESP alone end-to-end gives only end-to-end confidentiality. To get **all three** end-to-end, we need an inner AH SA, plus ESP tunnels at both levels.

**Final design (3 marks):**

| SA # | Endpoints | Protocol | Mode | Provides |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SA1 (outer) | Branch-GW ↔ Head-GW | **ESP** | **Tunnel** | Confidentiality across the untrusted WAN, plus gateway-to-gateway integrity |
| SA2 (inner) | Branch-Host ↔ Head-Host | **ESP** | **Tunnel** (or transport, depending on NAT) | End-to-end confidentiality |
| SA3 (innermost) | Branch-Host ↔ Head-Host | **AH** | **Transport** (or tunnel) | End-to-end integrity + authentication *of the encrypted packet* |

So we need **3 SAs** — one outer ESP tunnel (gateway), one inner ESP SA (end-to-end), and one AH SA (end-to-end) adjacent to the inner ESP.

**On-wire packet structure (3 marks):**

Let
- $H_{IP}^{GW}$ = new outer IP header (with branch-GW as source and head-GW as destination)
- $H_{IP}^{HH}$ = host-end-to-end IP header
- $H_{UL}$ = TCP/UDP header, $D$ = application data

The packet construction (outermost first) is:

$$P = \{H_{IP}^{GW},\; \text{ESP}_1^{hdr},\; \text{ESP}_1^{iv},\; \text{Enc}_1[\,H_{IP}^{HH},\; \text{AH}^{hdr},\; \text{AH}^{icv},\; \text{ESP}_2^{hdr},\; \text{ESP}_2^{iv},\; \text{Enc}_2[\,H_{IP}^{HH}, H_{UL}, D],\; \text{ESP}_2^{trail}, \text{ESP}_2^{icv}\,],\; \text{ESP}_1^{trail}, \text{ESP}_1^{icv}\}$$

Explanation in plain words:
1. Innermost: end-host encrypts the application data with ESP₂ (confidentiality).
2. Then AH computes an ICV over $H_{IP}^{HH}$, the AH header, and the *entire* ESP₂ block (including ESP₂'s ICV) — this provides end-to-end authentication.
3. The branch gateway then adds its own outer ESP₁ header+trailer, fully encrypting the inner block (including the AH and inner ESP), and puts its own outer IP header in front with the gateway's IP addresses.

**Decapsulation (reverse order) at head office:**
1. Head-GW strips outer ESP₁ → forwards inner block to head-host.
2. Head-host verifies AH ICV (end-to-end authentication).
3. Head-host strips ESP₂ → recovers original $H_{IP}^{HH}, H_{UL}, D$.

**Trade-off / remark (out of 7, can be mentioned for bonus):**
- Three SAs per packet is the *maximum* practically useful; more than 4 SA nestings is rejected by most IPsec implementations because of header overhead.
- An alternative is to use only **two SAs** — outer ESP tunnel + inner ESP-with-integrity (ESP in GCM or GMAC mode that already provides both confidentiality and authentication) — and *drop AH entirely*. This is the modern preferred design.

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain why **one single SA cannot provide all required security services simultaneously**. Describe, with reference to RFC 4302 and RFC 4303, what services each protocol provides. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following scenario: Host A wants to send to Host B with both ESP and AH applied in *transport mode*. Draw the on-wire packet structure for **both** orderings (AH-then-ESP and ESP-then-AH), and explain which ordering is considered more secure and why. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Why one SA is not enough **(7 marks)**

**Per-protocol capabilities (3 marks):**

- **AH (Authentication Header, RFC 4302)** provides:
  1. **Data origin authentication** of the IP datagram.
  2. **Connectionless integrity** (replay protection via sequence numbers, but **not** confidentiality).
  3. Coverage of the **IP header** (most fields) and the upper-layer payload.

- **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload, RFC 4303)** provides:
  1. **Confidentiality** (encryption) of the payload.
  2. **Optional** integrity + authentication (when an ESP authentication algorithm is negotiated, e.g., HMAC-SHA-256, or an AEAD like AES-GCM is used).
  3. ESP in *tunnel mode* also protects the **inner IP header**.

**Why a single SA falls short (4 marks):**

1. **AH cannot encrypt.** Any application that needs confidentiality (financial, medical, defence) cannot rely on AH alone, because AH explicitly forbids encryption of the payload (RFC 4302, §3).
2. **ESP in confidentiality-only mode** (e.g., ESP-NULL, an algorithm that does no encryption) gives no integrity. So ESP without authentication is *not* a complete security service.
3. **ESP-with-integrity** (the modern default, e.g., AES-GCM, AES-CCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) does provide both confidentiality and integrity, and is the *recommended* single-SA solution today.
4. **However, the original IPsec architecture (RFC 2401, 1998) deliberately split AH and ESP** so that:
   - Hosts that *legally* cannot export encryption (e.g., pre-2000 US export regulations) could still authenticate traffic with AH only.
   - SAs could be *combined* in a bundle to obtain the strongest possible protection.
5. Therefore, in classic IPsec, to obtain **confidentiality + integrity + authentication** between the *same* two endpoints, you must combine **at least two SAs**: one ESP SA (for confidentiality) and one AH SA (for the extra integrity/authentication that covers the IP header itself — something ESP *cannot* cover in transport mode).

#### Part (b) — On-wire packet structures (7 marks)

Let $H_{IP}$ = original IP header, $H_{UL}$ = TCP/UDP header, $D$ = application data, $E[\cdot]$ = ESP encryption of the bracketed bytes, $A$ = AH authentication computed over the bracketed bytes.

**Sub-case 1 — AH (outer) then ESP (inner): authenticate-then-encrypt (3.5 marks)**

$$P_1 = \{\, H_{IP},\, A_{hdr},\, A_{ICV},\, E_{hdr},\, E_{IV},\, E[\,H_{UL}, D\,],\, E_{trail},\, E_{icv}\,\}$$

Where $A_{ICV}$ is computed over $\{H_{IP}, A_{hdr}, E_{hdr}, E_{IV}, E[\,H_{UL}, D\,], E_{trail}, E_{icv}\}$.

**Sub-case 2 — ESP (outer) then AH (inner): encrypt-then-authenticate (3.5 marks — but this is *physically impossible* in transport mode for end-to-end; see note below)**

The intended structure would be:

$$P_2 = \{\, H_{IP},\, E_{hdr},\, E_{IV},\, E[\,A_{hdr}, A_{ICV}, H_{UL}, D\,],\, E_{trail},\, E_{icv}\,\}$$

However, in strict transport mode the receiver has *no* way to verify $A_{ICV}$ without first decrypting the ESP payload — i.e., the receiver must decrypt before authenticating, which is exactly the **decrypt-then-authenticate** flaw.

**The correct encrypt-then-authenticate transport-adjacency layout (best KTU answer) is:**

$$P_2' = \{\, H_{IP},\, E_{hdr},\, E_{IV},\, E[\,H_{UL}, D\,],\, E_{trail},\, E_{icv},\, A_{hdr},\, A_{ICV} \,\}$$

Where $A_{ICV}$ is computed over $\{H_{IP}, E_{hdr}, E_{IV}, E[\,H_{UL}, D\,],\, E_{trail},\, E_{icv}\}$ — i.e., the AH is now *outer* to ESP. This is equivalent to the case-3a ordering but interpreted as **encrypt-then-auth** (the receiver verifies AH first, then decrypts ESP safely).

**Which is more secure? (Bonus 0.5 mark)**

**Encrypt-then-authenticate (P₂' — ESP inside, AH outside)** is more secure because:
- The receiver authenticates the *ciphertext* (and IP header) *before* attempting decryption, so any tampered packet is dropped without spending decryption CPU.
- It prevents chosen-ciphertext / padding-oracle attacks on the ESP encryption.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall**
> 1. **Do not confuse "transport adjacency" with "nested SAs".** Transport adjacency = same endpoints, multiple SAs *side by side*. Nested = different endpoints, SAs *one inside another*. Examiners explicitly deduct **2 marks** for mixing these up.
> 2. **Do not state that ESP "cannot provide integrity" in 2024.** ESP *with* an authentication algorithm (or AEAD) does provide integrity. State it correctly: classic ESP-*NULL* + HMAC, or AEAD ciphers.
> 3. **Order of header listing in the diagram is *outermost-left, innermost-right***. Many students reverse this. The IP header is always *leftmost* because it is the first header on the wire.
> 4. **IANA protocol numbers**: AH = **51**, ESP = **50**. Mixing them up loses a direct 0.5 mark.
> 5. **"SA is simplex"** is the single most-tested one-line fact in this module — write it explicitly.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Combining Security Associations"**
>
> ✅ **SA basics** — simplex, identified by {Destination IP, Protocol, SPI}; lives in the **SAD**; rules live in the **SPD**.
>
> ✅ **Three combination cases** —
>   - **Case 1:** single SA, end-to-end.
>   - **Case 2:** nested SAs (gateway-to-gateway *outside*, end-to-end *inside*).
>   - **Case 3:** transport adjacency (multiple SAs at the same layer, same endpoints).
>
> ✅ **Transport adjacency vs nested SAs** — same endpoints vs different endpoints. This is the **#1 distinguishing fact**.
>
> ✅ **Recommended order** — for both confidentiality and integrity end-to-end, **encrypt-then-authenticate** (ESP inside, AH outside) is preferred in modern IPsec.
>
> ✅ **ESP capabilities** — confidentiality (always), integrity+authentication (optional / AEAD).
>
> ✅ **AH capabilities** — integrity + authentication, **no encryption**; also covers mutable IP-header fields' *non-mutable* parts.
>
> ✅ **IANA protocol numbers** — AH = **51**, ESP = **50**; SPI = 32 bits; sequence number = 32 bits.
>
> ✅ **SA bundle** — a *set* of SAs applied in a defined order; the *union* of their protections.
>
> ✅ **Modes** — Transport (no new IP header) vs Tunnel (new outer IP header). Case 2 almost always uses **tunnel** mode.
>
> ✅ **Why not one mega-SA?** — historical/legal split between AH and ESP, plus the engineering desire to compose security services modularly.
>
> ✅ **Maximum practical nesting** — 3–4 SAs; more is rejected for header-overhead reasons.
>
> ✅ **Anti-replay** — handled by the 32-bit **sequence number** in every AH/ESP header; mandatory in IPv4, recommended in IPv6.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Management, Internet Key Exchange (IKE) Phases, Web Security Considerations, Secure Socket Layer and Transport Layer Security (SSL/TLS) Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Key Management, Internet Key Exchange (IKE) Phases, Web Security Considerations, Secure Socket Layer and Transport Layer Security (SSL/TLS) Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Key Management

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)
**Key Management** refers to the generation, distribution, storage, update, revocation, and destruction of cryptographic keys in a secure communication system. It is the overarching framework that ensures cryptographic keys are available to authorized entities when required, while remaining inaccessible to adversaries throughout their lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Key Management is the *largest* sub-module under Module 3 and frequently carries 14-mark questions in ESE. You must master symmetric key distribution, public-key distribution, and the **Diffie-Hellman (DH) Key Exchange** algorithm — both the mathematics and the man-in-the-middle vulnerability.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Locker Key Registry"
Think of a large office building with hundreds of employees. Each employee needs a key to their personal locker, but the building cannot hand out a master key to everyone. The building establishes a **Key Registry Office (KDC - Key Distribution Center)**: every employee authenticates at the office, and the office issues *session keys* for short-term use. Old keys are returned or destroyed; lost keys are revoked. The keys never leave the secure registry, but they are *shared* safely between parties who need to communicate. This is exactly how symmetric key management works in protocols like Kerberos.

---

## 1.2 Internet Key Exchange (IKE)

### Formal Definition
**Internet Key Exchange (IKE)** is a hybrid protocol that operates in the application layer of the IPsec suite. It uses the **ISAKMP (Internet Security Association and Key Management Protocol)** framework combined with the **Oakley** key-exchange protocol and the **SKEME** key-exchange technique to negotiate, authenticate, and establish shared symmetric keys for use by IPsec (ESP/AH).

> [!NOTE]
> **IKE is to IPsec what SSL Handshake is to HTTPS** — both negotiate security parameters and keys, but IKE works at the network layer for any IP traffic, whereas SSL/TLS works at the transport layer for application traffic.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Step Diplomatic Handshake"
Imagine two ambassadors (Alice and Bob) meeting in a foreign city. They cannot simply exchange secret documents at the airport (insecure public space). Instead, they follow a strict protocol:
1. **Phase 1 (Diplomatic Recognition):** They mutually verify each other's passports, agree on the *language of communication* (encryption algorithm), the *handshake style* (authentication method), and establish a *secure diplomatic channel* (IKE SA).
2. **Phase 2 (Document Exchange):** Using the secure channel, they exchange *session-specific* classified documents (IPsec SAs) with different parameters if needed.

IKE works the same way: Phase 1 creates a protected channel; Phase 2 uses that channel to negotiate many fast security associations.

---

## 1.3 Web Security Considerations

### Formal Definition
**Web Security** encompasses the policies, protocols, and mechanisms that protect data transmitted over the World Wide Web from threats such as eavesdropping, tampering, message forgery, identity spoofing, and denial-of-service. The *Considerations* include threats at the Web server, the Web browser, and the network in between, plus the cryptographic services (confidentiality, integrity, authentication, non-repudiation) required to mitigate them.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Postcard vs. Sealed Letter" Problem
A normal HTTP request is like a **postcard**: anyone in the postal chain (WiFi router, ISP, server admin) can read it. Web Security turns that postcard into a **sealed, wax-stamped, signed letter** that only the intended recipient can open, and the recipient can verify who sent it. SSL/TLS is the wax-seal technology that achieves this transformation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** Web Security is **not** just about encryption. KTU expects you to list **at least 4 threats** (e.g., integrity violation, confidentiality breach, authentication failure, denial of service) and the **specific SSL/TLS service** that mitigates each.

---

## 1.4 Secure Socket Layer (SSL) and Transport Layer Security (TLS)

### Formal Definition
**SSL (Secure Socket Layer)** is the original Netscape-developed protocol (1994–1996) providing communication security over the Internet. **TLS (Transport Layer Security)**, standardized by the IETF as **RFC 5246 (TLS 1.2)** and **RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3)**, is the cryptographic successor to SSL. Both protocols sit between the **application layer (HTTP, SMTP, FTP)** and the **transport layer (TCP)**, and they use a combination of **symmetric encryption, asymmetric encryption, message authentication codes, and digital certificates** to provide confidentiality, integrity, and authentication.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Trusted Courier Service"
When you place an order on Amazon, the website cannot send the product (your credit-card data) via regular mail. It hires a **Trusted Courier Service (SSL/TLS)**. The courier:
1. Shows an ID card signed by a known authority (digital certificate).
2. Seals the package so only the customer can open it (symmetric encryption with session key).
3. Hands the customer a receipt proving what was sent (MAC for integrity).
4. Agrees with the customer on a *new* secret code for next time (session key negotiation).

That is SSL/TLS in plain English.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Metrics for KTU Exam:**
> - **Default TLS port:** **443** (HTTP over TLS) and **993 / 995** for mail.
> - **SSL was deprecated in 2015 by IETF** in favor of TLS. Modern browsers no longer support SSL 2.0/3.0.
> - **TLS 1.3 (2018)** reduced the handshake to **1-RTT** (one round-trip time) and removed weak ciphers like RC4, MD5, SHA-1.
> - **Cryptographic suite convention:** `KeyExchange\_Authentication\_Cipher\_MAC` (e.g., `TLS\_ECDHE\_RSA\_WITH\_AES\_256\_GCM\_SHA384`).

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Key Management — The Complete Lifecycle

### 2.1.1 Key Categories (per NIST SP 800-57)
1. **Public Keys (Asymmetric)** — distributed openly; protected by digital certificates.
2. **Private Keys (Asymmetric)** — held secretly by owner; protected by hardware modules.
3. **Symmetric Keys (Secret)** — shared between two or more parties; lifetime is short.
4. **Master Keys** — long-term, used to derive short-term keys (key-encryption-key).
5. **Session Keys** — short-term, used to protect one communication session.

### 2.1.2 Key Distribution Methods

**A. Symmetric Key Distribution using a Key Distribution Center (KDC)**

The most common pattern in KTU exams. Sequence:
1. Alice → KDC : $Request\_A$ (Alice's ID, Bob's ID, Nonce $N_1$)
2. KDC → Alice : $E_{KA}(K_S \Vert ID_B \Vert N_1 \Vert E_{KB}(K_S \Vert ID_A))$
3. Alice → Bob : $E_{KB}(K_S \Vert ID_A)$
4. Bob → Alice : $E_{KS}(N_2)$
5. Alice → Bob : $E_{KS}(f(N_2))$

> Where $K_A$ = Alice's master key, $K_B$ = Bob's master key, $K_S$ = session key, $N_1, N_2$ = nonces.

**B. Public-Key Distribution (Three-Model Approach)**

| Model | Mechanism | Weakness |
|---|---|---|
| Public Announcement | Users broadcast their public key | Forgery by impostor |
| Publicly Available Directory | Trusted directory maintained by an authority | Directory tampering |
| Public-Key Authority | On-demand request/response to authority | Authority is bottleneck |

**C. Public-Key Certificates (X.509)**

A trusted **Certification Authority (CA)** binds an identity to a public key by signing a structure:
$$Certificate = CA\_Sign(Subject, PublicKey, Serial, Validity, Issuer, Algorithm)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The X.509 certificate format contains **Version, Serial Number, Signature Algorithm, Issuer, Validity Period, Subject, Subject's Public Key, and Signature**. Draw the structure if asked for 7 marks.

---

## 2.2 Diffie-Hellman (DH) Key Exchange

### 2.2.1 Theoretical Foundation
The DH protocol allows two parties to derive a **shared secret over an insecure channel** without prior shared information. Its security rests on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**: given $g, p, g^a \mod p$, computing $a$ is computationally infeasible for large primes.

### 2.2.2 Global Public Parameters
- A large prime $p$ (minimum **2048 bits** for security today).
- A primitive root (generator) $g$ where $1 < g < p$.

### 2.2.3 Protocol Steps
1. Alice chooses secret integer $a$, computes $Y_A = g^a \mod p$, sends $Y_A$ to Bob.
2. Bob chooses secret integer $b$, computes $Y_B = g^b \mod p$, sends $Y_B$ to Alice.
3. Alice computes $K = (Y_B)^a \mod p$.
4. Bob computes $K = (Y_A)^b \mod p$.

Both arrive at the same shared secret:
$$K = g^{ab} \mod p$$

### 2.2.4 Vulnerability: Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)
Without authentication, an attacker Mallory can:
- Replace $Y_A$ with $Y_M1 = g^{m1} \mod p$ to Alice
- Replace $Y_B$ with $Y_M2 = g^{m2} \mod p$ to Bob
- Establish two separate DH keys, fully decrypting and re-encrypting traffic.

**Mitigation:** Authenticate the DH exchange using **digital signatures** or run DH *inside* an authenticated tunnel (e.g., IKE Phase 1).

---

## 2.3 IKE — Detailed Phase Analysis

### 2.3.1 IKE Architecture Overview

IKE is a two-phase protocol built on the **ISAKMP** framework:

| Phase | Purpose | Output | Re-key Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1** | Mutual authentication, establish IKE SA | **IKE SA** (bidirectional) | Long-lived (days) |
| **Phase 2** | Negotiate IPsec SAs using IKE SA | **IPsec SA(s)** (unidirectional pairs) | Short-lived (hours) |

### 2.3.2 Phase 1 — Two Modes

**Main Mode (6 messages, identity protected):**
1. $I \to R : SA, g, p, N_I$  *(proposal + DH half-key + nonce)*
2. $R \to I : SA, g, p, N_R, Y_R$  *(chosen proposal + DH half-key + nonce)*
3. $I \to R : Y_I$  *(DH value of Alice)*
4. $R \to I : Y_R$  *(DH value of Bob — wait, actually position varies by implementation)*

*Corrected canonical flow (Stallings):*
- **Msg 1 & 2:** Algorithm negotiation (ISAKMP SA payload).
- **Msg 3 & 4:** DH key exchange (KE payload + nonces).
- **Msg 5 & 6:** Authentication (encrypted with derived key — identities are protected).

**Aggressive Mode (3 messages, identity exposed but faster):**
1. $I \to R : SA, g, p, N_I, Y_I, ID_I$
2. $R \to I : SA, g, p, N_R, Y_R, ID_R, [AUTH]$
3. $I \to R : [AUTH]$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two modes?** Main Mode is mandatory for compliance with strict security policies (identities hidden). Aggressive Mode is used in remote-access VPNs where speed matters and the IP address itself is identifying.

### 2.3.3 Phase 1 Authentication Methods
- **Pre-Shared Secret (PSK)** — symmetric key typed manually.
- **Digital Signatures (RSA / ECDSA)** — sign a hash of (Nonces + DH values).
- **Public-Key Encryption** — encrypt identities with peer's public key (rare).
- **Revised Public-Key Encryption** — only encrypt nonces (faster).

### 2.3.4 Phase 2 — Quick Mode (3 messages)
1. Negotiation of **IPsec SA** parameters (ESP/AH, transforms, lifetime).
2. Optional **PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy)** by performing a *new* DH exchange.
3. Exchange of nonces for key derivation.

After Quick Mode, both sides generate two pairs of keys (one for ESP, one for AH) using a **Pseudo-Random Function (PRF)** seeded with the IKE SA keying material and the Phase-2 nonces.

---

## 2.4 Web Security Considerations — Threat Model

### 2.4.1 Categories of Threats (RFC 4949 / Stallings)

| Threat | Web Server Side | Browser Side | Network Side |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Integrity Violation** | Unauthorized modification of data | Modifying browser cache | In-flight packet modification |
| **Confidentiality Breach** | Theft of DB | Stealing cookies/local storage | Eavesdropping on traffic |
| **Authentication Failure** | Impersonation of legitimate user | Spoofed websites | DNS poisoning |
| **Denial of Service** | Resource exhaustion | Browser crash (e.g., XML bombs) | SYN floods, BGP hijack |
| **Repudiation** | User denies transaction | — | — |

### 2.4.2 Cryptographic Services Required
1. **Confidentiality (C)** — symmetric encryption (AES).
2. **Integrity (I)** — MAC / HMAC / AEAD.
3. **Authentication (A)** — digital signatures, certificates.
4. **Non-Repudiation (NR)** — digital signatures with trusted timestamping.
5. **Access Control (AC)** — authorization tokens, RBAC.

SSL/TLS provides **C, I, A** by design. Non-repudiation is partially provided by certificates.

---

## 2.5 SSL/TLS Architecture

### 2.5.1 Protocol Stack

```
+-----------------------------------------+
|    HTTP / SMTP / FTP / App Protocols    |   ← Application Layer
+-----------------------------------------+
|  Handshake | ChangeCipherSpec | Alert   |   ← SSL/TLS Sub-Protocols
+-----------------------------------------+
|              SSL/TLS Record             |   ← Encapsulation Layer
+-----------------------------------------+
|                  TCP                    |   ← Transport Layer
+-----------------------------------------+
|                  IP                     |   ← Network Layer
+-----------------------------------------+
```

### 2.5.2 The Four SSL/TLS Sub-Protocols

| Protocol | Purpose | Key Message |
|---|---|---|
| **Handshake** | Negotiate version, cipher suite, authenticate, exchange keys | `ClientHello`, `ServerHello`, `Certificate`, `ClientKeyExchange`, `Finished` |
| **Change Cipher Spec (CCS)** | Signal that subsequent records will use the new agreed parameters | Single-byte message `1` |
| **Alert** | Convey warnings/fatal errors | `close_notify`, `handshake_failure`, `bad_certificate` |
| **Record** | Fragment, compress (optional in TLS), MAC, encrypt | `ContentType`, `Version`, `Length`, `Fragment` |

### 2.5.3 SSL/TLS Cryptographic Services Matrix

| Service | Mechanism | Algorithm Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Server Authentication | X.509 Certificate | RSA, ECDSA |
| Client Authentication (optional) | Client Certificate | RSA, ECDSA |
| Confidentiality (Bulk Encryption) | Symmetric Cipher | AES-128/256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 |
| Integrity / Authentication | MAC / AEAD | HMAC-SHA256, GCM Tag |
| Key Derivation | HKDF (TLS 1.3) / PRF | HMAC-based Extract-and-Expand |

### 2.5.4 TLS Handshake (TLS 1.2 — 2-RTT)

| Step | Direction | Message | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | C → S | `ClientHello` | Version, random, session ID, cipher suites, extensions |
| 2 | S → C | `ServerHello` | Chosen version, random, session ID, cipher suite |
| 3 | S → C | `Certificate` | Server's X.509 chain |
| 4 | S → C | `ServerKeyExchange` | DH parameters / ECDH params (signature required) |
| 5 | S → C | `ServerHelloDone` | Triggers client response |
| 6 | C → S | `ClientKeyExchange` | Pre-master secret / ECDH public value |
| 7 | C → S | `CertificateVerify` | (Optional) client signs handshake hash |
| 8 | C → S | `ChangeCipherSpec` | Switch to negotiated parameters |
| 9 | C → S | `Finished` | MAC of all prior handshake messages |
| 10 | S → C | `ChangeCipherSpec` | Switch |
| 11 | S → C | `Finished` | Verification + handshake done |

### 2.5.5 TLS 1.3 — The Modern Simplification
- **1-RTT handshake** (client sends key share in ClientHello; server responds in first flight).
- **0-RTT resumption** for repeat connections (with replay risk caveat).
- **Forward secrecy mandatory** (no RSA key transport).
- **All handshake after ServerHello is encrypted.**
- **Removed:** static RSA, CBC ciphers, MD5, SHA-1, 3DES, RC4.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DH Shared Secret | $K = g^{ab} \mod p$ | Both sides compute same $K$ |
| 2 | DH Public Value | $Y_A = g^a \mod p$ | Sent over insecure channel |
| 3 | Discrete Logarithm | $a = \log_g(Y_A \mod p)$ | Computationally infeasible for large $p$ |
| 4 | RSA Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | $p, q$ large primes |
| 5 | RSA Ciphertext | $C = M^e \mod n$ | Public-key operation |
| 6 | RSA Plaintext | $M = C^d \mod n$ | Private-key operation |
| 7 | Euler's Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Used to find $d \equiv e^{-1} \mod \phi(n)$ |
| 8 | Key Derivation (TLS 1.2) | $PRF(secret, label, seed)$ | Generates 6 key block values |
| 9 | Master Secret (TLS 1.2) | $MS = PRF(PreMasterSecret, "master secret", ClientRandom \Vert ServerRandom)$ | 48 bytes |
| 10 | Key Block | $KB = PRF(MS, "key expansion", CR \Vert SR)$ | Split into MAC keys, encryption keys, IVs |
| 11 | HMAC | $HMAC(K, m) = H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert m))$ | Standardized in RFC 2104 |
| 12 | Session ID length | typically **32 bytes** | TLS identifier |
| 13 | Minimum DH prime size | **2048 bits** (NIST 2024) | For 112-bit security |
| 14 | Recommended symmetric key | **AES-128 / AES-256** | RC4/SHA-1/3DES deprecated |
| 15 | TLS 1.3 minimum key | **AES-128** | All ciphers AEAD |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Symbol Warning:** In markdown tables, write $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of $\mid$ in formulas like $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ to avoid breaking the table pipe syntax.

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Banking** | TLS 1.2/1.3 protects online transactions; HSM stores private keys. |
| **Cloud (AWS/Azure)** | IKEv2 (RFC 7296) negotiates IPsec tunnels for VPC peering. |
| **Telemedicine** | SSL provides HIPAA-compliant data-in-transit confidentiality. |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Diffie-Hellman Works (Mathematical Proof)

We are given a public prime $p$ and a primitive root $g$. Alice chooses secret $a$, Bob chooses secret $b$.

**Step 1 — Alice computes her public value:**
$$Y_A = g^a \mod p$$

**Step 2 — Bob computes his public value:**
$$Y_B = g^b \mod p$$

**Step 3 — Alice receives $Y_B$ and computes:**
$$K_A = (Y_B)^a \mod p$$

Substitute $Y_B$:
$$K_A = (g^b)^a \mod p$$

Apply the exponent power rule $(x^m)^n = x^{m \cdot n}$:
$$K_A = g^{ba} \mod p$$

**Step 4 — Bob receives $Y_A$ and computes:**
$$K_B = (Y_A)^b \mod p$$

Substitute $Y_A$:
$$K_B = (g^a)^b \mod p$$

Apply the exponent power rule:
$$K_B = g^{ab} \mod p$$

**Step 5 — Compare $K_A$ and $K_B$:**
$$K_A = g^{ba} \mod p = g^{ab} \mod p = K_B$$

> **Conclusion:** Both parties arrive at the identical shared secret $K = g^{ab} \mod p$ without ever transmitting $a$ or $b$ over the channel.

**Step 6 — Attacker Observes Only:**
- $g$ (public)
- $p$ (public)
- $Y_A = g^a \mod p$ (public)
- $Y_B = g^b \mod p$ (public)

To compute $K$, the attacker would need $a = \log_g(Y_A \mod p)$ — the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**. For $p \geq 2048$ bits, this is computationally infeasible (estimated $\approx 2^{112}$ operations).

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example: Diffie-Hellman with Small Primes

This is a **frequently-asked KTU 14-mark question**. We will solve it exhaustively.

**Given:**
- Public prime $p = 23$
- Public base $g = 5$ (verify primitive root below)
- Alice's secret $a = 6$
- Bob's secret $b = 15$

### 3.2.1 Verify $g = 5$ is a primitive root mod 23

The order of the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_{23}^*$ is $\phi(23) = 22$ (since 23 is prime). Primitive roots $g$ are those with order 22. Check $5^{22/k} \mod 23$ for each prime factor $k$ of 22: $k \in \{2, 11\}$.

$$5^{22/2} = 5^{11} \mod 23$$

Compute step by step:
$$5^2 = 25 = 23 + 2 \equiv 2 \mod 23$$
$$5^4 = (5^2)^2 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \mod 23$$
$$5^8 = (5^4)^2 \equiv 4^2 = 16 \mod 23$$
$$5^{11} = 5^8 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 \equiv 16 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 160 \mod 23$$
$$160 = 6 \cdot 23 + 22 \equiv 22 \equiv -1 \mod 23$$

Since $5^{11} \not\equiv 1 \mod 23$, condition for $k=2$ is satisfied.

$$5^{22/11} = 5^2 \equiv 2 \mod 23$$

Since $5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \not\equiv 1 \mod 23$, condition for $k=11$ is satisfied.

> **Therefore $g = 5$ is a primitive root mod 23.** ✓

### 3.2.2 Compute Alice's Public Value

$$Y_A = g^a \mod p = 5^6 \mod 23$$

Compute $5^6$ step by step:
$$5^1 = 5$$
$$5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \mod 23$$
$$5^3 = 5^2 \cdot 5 = 2 \cdot 5 = 10 \mod 23$$
$$5^6 = (5^3)^2 = 10^2 = 100 \mod 23$$
$$100 = 4 \cdot 23 + 8 \equiv 8 \mod 23$$

$$\boxed{Y_A = 8}$$

### 3.2.3 Compute Bob's Public Value

$$Y_B = g^b \mod p = 5^{15} \mod 23$$

Compute $5^{15}$ using exponentiation by squaring:
$$5^1 = 5$$
$$5^2 = 2 \mod 23 \quad \text{(from above)}$$
$$5^4 = (5^2)^2 = 2^2 = 4 \mod 23$$
$$5^8 = (5^4)^2 = 4^2 = 16 \mod 23$$

Now decompose $15 = 8 + 4 + 2 + 1$:
$$5^{15} = 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 = 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 \mod 23$$
$$= (16 \cdot 4) \cdot (2 \cdot 5) = 64 \cdot 10 \mod 23$$
$$64 = 2 \cdot 23 + 18 \equiv 18 \mod 23$$
$$18 \cdot 10 = 180 \mod 23$$
$$180 = 7 \cdot 23 + 19 \equiv 19 \mod 23$$

$$\boxed{Y_B = 19}$$

### 3.2.4 Compute the Shared Secret (Alice's Way)

$$K = (Y_B)^a \mod p = 19^6 \mod 23$$

Compute $19^6$ using the fact that $19 \equiv -4 \mod 23$ (since $19 - 23 = -4$):
$$19^2 = (-4)^2 = 16 \mod 23$$
$$19^3 = 16 \cdot (-4) = -64 \mod 23$$
$$-64 = -3 \cdot 23 + 5 \equiv 5 \mod 23$$
$$19^6 = (19^3)^2 = 5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \mod 23$$

$$\boxed{K = 2}$$

### 3.2.5 Verify with Bob's Computation

$$K = (Y_A)^b \mod p = 8^{15} \mod 23$$

Express $8 = 2^3$:
$$8^{15} = 2^{45} \mod 23$$

Apply Fermat's Little Theorem: $2^{22} \equiv 1 \mod 23$.
$$2^{45} = 2^{44} \cdot 2^1 = (2^{22})^2 \cdot 2 = 1^2 \cdot 2 = 2 \mod 23$$

$$\boxed{K = 2} \quad \checkmark$$

**Verification successful:** Both Alice and Bob have independently derived the shared secret $K = 2$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: TLS 1.2 Master Secret & Key Block Computation

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Pre-Master Secret (PMS)
- For **RSA key transport:** Client generates a 48-byte random PMS, encrypts with server's public key, sends it.
- For **DH/ECDH:** The shared secret computed via DH becomes the PMS.

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Master Secret (MS) Derivation

The Master Secret is derived by hashing the Pre-Master Secret together with the two nonces (client random and server random):

$$MS = PRF(PMS, "master\ secret", ClientRandom \Vert ServerRandom)[0..47]$$

The result is a **48-byte** secret. The PRF is defined in TLS 1.2 as:

$$PRF(secret, label, seed) = P\_hash(secret, label \Vert seed)$$

where the P_hash function is constructed using HMAC:

$$P\_hash(secret, seed) = HMAC(secret, A(1) \Vert seed) \Vert HMAC(secret, A(2) \Vert seed) \Vert \ldots$$

with the iteration function:
$$A(0) = seed$$
$$A(i) = HMAC(secret, A(i-1))$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Key Block (KB) Derivation

Once the Master Secret is established, the key block is generated to produce the actual keys used in the session:

$$KeyBlock = PRF(MS, "key\ expansion", ClientRandom \Vert ServerRandom)$$

The Key Block is then partitioned as follows (for a cipher like `AES_128_CBC_SHA256`):

$$KeyBlock = \begin{cases} client\_MAC\_key & \text{32 bytes (SHA-256)} \\ server\_MAC\_key & \text{32 bytes (SHA-256)} \\ client\_encryption\_key & \text{16 bytes (AES-128)} \\ server\_encryption\_key & \text{16 bytes (AES-128)} \\ client\_IV & \text{16 bytes (explicit IV, TLS 1.2+)} \\ server\_IV & \text{16 bytes (explicit IV, TLS 1.2+)} \end{cases}$$

### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Finished Message Verification
The Finished message contains a PRF of the Master Secret and the hash of all prior handshake messages:

$$verify\_data = PRF(MS, "client\ finished", Hash(handshake\_messages))[0..11]$$

This binds the key derivation to the actual handshake transcript, preventing downgrade and MITM attacks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — IKE Phase 1 vs Phase 2

| Attribute | IKE Phase 1 (Main Mode) | IKE Phase 2 (Quick Mode) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Establish IKE SA (ISAKMP SA) | Derive IPsec SAs |
| **Number of Messages** | 6 (Main Mode) or 3 (Aggressive) | 3 |
| **Authentication** | Required (PSK / Signature / PK Enc) | Inherits from Phase 1 (IKE SA) |
| **Identity Protection** | Yes (Main Mode hides identities in encrypted msgs 5,6) | No — identities already authenticated |
| **Negotiates** | Encryption alg, hash alg, DH group, auth method, lifetime | IPsec transform (ESP/AH), mode (tunnel/transport), lifetime, optional PFS |
| **Key Output** | **SKEYID** and **SKEYID\_d** (used to derive keying material) | Two pairs of ESP/AH keys (in + out for each direction) |
| **Re-key Frequency** | Long-lived (e.g., 1 day / 8 hours) | Short-lived (e.g., 1 hour / 100 MB) |
| **Cookie Exchange** | Yes (anti-clogging, anti-DoS) | No (protected by Phase 1 SA) |
| **PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy)** | Not applicable | Optional via fresh DH exchange |
| **State** | Establishes bidirectional IKE SA | Negotiates unidirectional IPsec SA pair |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — SSL 3.0 vs TLS 1.2 vs TLS 1.3

| Feature | SSL 3.0 (1996) | TLS 1.2 (RFC 5246) | TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MAC Algorithm** | HMAC-MD5/SHA-1 (additive) | HMAC-SHA256/384 (combined with padding) | AEAD only (GCM, Poly1305) |
| **Cipher Support** | RC4, 3DES, AES | AES-CBC, AES-GCM | AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 (CBC removed) |
| **Key Exchange** | RSA, Fixed DH, Fortezza | RSA, DHE, ECDHE | DHE, ECDHE only (no static RSA) |
| **PRF** | MD5 / SHA-1 mix | SHA-256/384 PRF | HKDF (Extract + Expand) |
| **Handshake RTTs** | 2-RTT | 2-RTT | 1-RTT (with 0-RTT resumption) |
| **Forward Secrecy** | Optional (rarely used) | Optional (DHE) | Mandatory |
| **Certificate Type** | RSA, DSS | RSA, DSA, ECDSA | RSA, ECDSA, EdDSA |
| **Padding Indication** | Implicit | Explicit (used in CBC attacks) | Removed (no CBC) |
| **Status Today** | **Deprecated** (POODLE 2014) | Widely used (current default) | **Recommended** (IETF standard 2018) |

---

## 3.6 Derivation: SSL Record Protocol — Encapsulation Steps

For every outbound TLS record, the **Record Protocol** performs:

**Step 1 — Fragmentation**
$$Data \to Data_1, Data_2, \ldots, Data_n \quad \text{where each } \vert Data_i \vert \leq 2^{14} \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Optional Compression** (TLS 1.3 removes this)
$$C_i = Compress(Data_i)$$

**Step 3 —MAC Computation** (for non-AEAD ciphers)
$$MAC_i = HMAC(Hash, MAC\_key, seq\_num \Vert C\_type \Vert C\_version \Vert C\_length \Vert C_i)$$

**Step 4 — Encryption (CBC mode example)**
$$E_i = AES\_CBC\_Encrypt(Enc\_key, IV_i, C_i \Vert MAC_i \Vert Padding)$$

**Step 5 — Header Addition**
$$\text{Record} = \text{ContentType} \Vert \text{ProtocolVersion} \Vert \text{Length} \Vert E_i$$

The ContentType is one of: `change_cipher_spec (20)`, `alert (21)`, `handshake (22)`, `application_data (23)`.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example — Verifying IKE Phase 1 Authentication (PSK Method)

This is a common KTU 14-mark problem.

**Scenario:** IKE Main Mode with **Pre-Shared Key (PSK)** authentication.

**Step 1 — Both sides know PSK:** $PSK = K_{AB}$ (out of band).

**Step 2 — Exchange nonces and DH values** (messages 1–4):
- Alice sends $N_I$, $Y_A = g^a \mod p$
- Bob sends $N_R$, $Y_B = g^b \mod p$

**Step 3 — Both sides compute SKEYID:**
$$SKEYID = PRF(PSK, N_I \Vert N_R)$$

**Step 4 — Both sides compute SKEYID\_e (encryption key), SKEYID\_a (authentication key), SKEYID\_d (derivation key):**
$$SKEYID\_d = PRF(SKEYID, g^{ab} \Vert CKY\_I \Vert CKY\_R \Vert 2)$$
$$SKEYID\_a = PRF(SKEYID, SKEYID\_d \Vert g^{ab} \Vert CKY\_I \Vert CKY\_R \Vert 3)$$
$$SKEYID\_e = PRF(SKEYID, SKEYID\_a \Vert g^{ab} \Vert CKY\_I \Vert CKY\_R \Vert 4)$$

**Step 5 — Generate HASH\_I and HASH\_R for authentication:**
$$HASH\_I = PRF(SKEYID, g^i \Vert g^r \Vert CKY\_I \Vert CKY\_R \Vert SA\_I \Vert ID\_I)$$
$$HASH\_R = PRF(SKEYID, g^r \Vert g^i \Vert CKY\_R \Vert CKY\_I \Vert SA\_R \Vert ID\_R)$$

**Step 6 — Mutual Authentication (messages 5, 6, encrypted with SKEYID\_e):**
- Alice → Bob: $E_{SKEYID\_e}(ID\_I, HASH\_I)$
- Bob → Alice: $E_{SKEYID\_e}(ID\_R, HASH\_R)$

> Each side verifies the received HASH matches its own computation. If they match, **mutual authentication is established**.

**Step 7 — IKE SA Established:** The SKEYID\_e, SKEYID\_a, SKEYID\_d values now protect all subsequent Phase 2 exchanges.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

    Bob->>Mallory: Y_B = g^b mod p
