(Lose 1–2 marks.) > 2. **Omitting cryptographic capabilities:** The examiner wants to know *what keys* each participant holds. Writing "Alice sends a request" without stating she holds $K_{AS}$ loses 1 mark. > 3. **Mis-stating the key count formula:** Writing $n^2$ instead of $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ for unordered pairs. The division by 2 is critical. > 4. **Forgetting the replay defense:** Any protocol discussion without mentioning nonces/timestamps/replay-cache is marked as **incomplete** (-2 marks). > 5. **Wrong notation:** Using $A \to B : M$ without defining the symbols first. Always define $A$, $B$, $K_{AB}$, $N_A$ before writing the first message. > 6. **Conflating Kerberos AS and TGS:** They are *both* TTPs but serve different purposes. Examiners specifically test this distinction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Participants" in Network Security Protocols** - [x] A **Participant** is any cryptographically-identified entity in a protocol. - [x] The **three canonical roles** are: Claimant, Verifier, Trusted Third Party (TTP). - [x] A **TTP reduces key count** from $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ to $n$ (huge scalability gain). - [x] **Needham–Schroeder** uses 1 TTP (KDC) and 5 messages for symmetric variant. - [x] **Kerberos v5** uses 2 TTPs (AS + TGS) and enables **Single Sign-On (SSO)**. - [x] **X.509 / PKI** uses an **off-line** Root CA and on-line Intermediate CAs. - [x] **Dolev–Yao model:** Treat the network as a malicious participant. - [x] **Replay defense** = Nonces (freshness) + Timestamps (time-bounded) + Replay cache. - [x] **Nonce length** should be ≥ 128 bits to defeat birthday-bound collisions. - [x] **Kerberos clock skew** default = **5 minutes**. - [x] **Forward secrecy** requires DH or ephemeral keys; static-key participants do NOT achieve it. - [x] **Participants are also called "principals"** in formal security literature. - [x] The **Claimant's nonce** $N_A$ and **Verifier's nonce** $N_B$ must be *independent and unpredictable*. - [x] **On-line TTPs** are performance bottlenecks; **off-line TTPs** (Root CA) avoid this. - [x] Every **mutual authentication protocol** ends with both participants convinced of each other's identity and possession of a fresh shared secret. - [x] **Compromise of TTP = total system failure** — this is the central trust assumption. --- > **End of Module 4 — Participants | PEADT863 — Network Security Protocols** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Aligned*

topics.title: Dual Signature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dual Signature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dual Signature (DS)** is a cryptographic construct introduced in the **Secure Electronic Transaction (SET)** protocol that allows a cardholder (customer) to simultaneously send two logically related but privacy-sensitive message blocks — the **Order Information (OI)** to the merchant and the **Payment Information (PI)** to the payment gateway (bank) — such that:
> 1. The **merchant can verify the integrity of OI** but cannot decipher the contents of PI.
> 2. The **bank (payment gateway) can verify the integrity of PI** but cannot decipher the contents of OI.
> 3. **Both parties can cryptographically confirm** that the OI and PI belong to the same transaction, even though neither party has access to the other party's full message.

The mathematical expression of the Dual Signature under SET is:

$$DS = E\bigl(PR_C,\; H(H(PI) \,\|\, H(OI))\bigr)$$

where $PR_C$ is the customer's private signature key, $E$ denotes asymmetric (public-key) encryption used for signing, $H(\cdot)$ is a one-way hash function (typically **SHA-1** in SET v1.0), and $\|$ is bitwise concatenation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Envelope Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Two-Locked-Letter Courier Scenario**

Imagine **Alice** is at a marketplace (the merchant's website) and wants to buy a painting worth ₹10,000. She must do two things simultaneously:
1. **Tell the merchant WHAT she is buying** (Order Information — *blue envelope*).
2. **Tell her bank to RELEASE the money** (Payment Information — *red envelope*).

The problem: Alice does **not** want the **merchant to see her credit card** (privacy), and she does **not** want the **bank to know what she is buying** (commercial confidentiality).

### The Solution — The Dual Signature "Tamper-Evident Wax Seal"

| Step | What Alice Does | Purpose |
|------|----------------|---------|
| 1 | Writes the order on a **blue paper** and the payment details on a **red paper**. | Creates two separate confidential messages. |
| 2 | Computes the **digest (fingerprint)** of each paper separately. | Unique tamper-detector. |
| 3 | **Glues the two fingerprints together** into a single combined string. | Links OI and PI cryptographically. |
| 4 | Signs the **combined fingerprint** with her **personal wax seal** (private key). | Authenticity + Non-repudiation. |
| 5 | Sends the **blue paper + combined fingerprint seal to the merchant**, and the **red paper + combined fingerprint seal to the bank**. | Privacy-preserving split delivery. |

The merchant keeps the **blue paper** but receives a sealed envelope containing only the **red paper's fingerprint** — so he knows the payment instruction is real, but **cannot open the red envelope**. Likewise, the bank receives the red paper and the **blue paper's fingerprint** — knowing Alice's order is authentic, but **not what she bought**. The wax seal (Dual Signature) ties the two envelopes to the same transaction without revealing cross-information.

## 1.3 Why Dual Signature is Necessary in E-Commerce

In conventional e-commerce, a customer submitting a payment form over the Internet must **hand over the entire transaction** to the merchant, who then forwards it to the bank. This exposes the customer to:
- **Merchant fraud** (merchant can misuse the card data).
- **Privacy breach** (bank learns purchase history).
- **Replay attacks** (an attacker resends old transactions).

The Dual Signature mechanism solves all three by making the **OI and PI independently verifiable yet cryptographically bound**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Takeaway** — The Dual Signature does *not* hide OI from the merchant or PI from the bank. Instead, it **prevents cross-disclosure**: the merchant learns OI but *not* PI; the bank learns PI but *not* OI. Both can verify that the two halves came from the **same signed transaction** without seeing each other's data.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants in SET

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| Hash Function | **SHA-1 (160-bit output)** | SET v1.0 mandatory; modern SET variants use SHA-256. |
| Asymmetric Algorithm | **RSA (1024-bit in SET v1.0)** | Provides digital signature. |
| Symmetric Algorithm | **DES (56-bit) for bulk data** | Encrypts OI/PI envelopes. |
| Dual Signature Length | **1024 bits (= RSA signature size)** | Equal to the size of the signer's private-key output. |
| OI Hash Input Size | Variable (≤ several KB) | Hashed before being concatenated with PI hash. |
| PI Hash Input Size | Variable (card number, amount, etc.) | Hashed before being concatenated with OI hash. |

## 1.5 Position in the SET Protocol Stack

```
Customer ──[OI, H(PI), DS]──> Merchant ──[PI, H(OI), DS]──> Payment Gateway
   (C)         (signed)         (M)            (signed)            (PG)
```

The Dual Signature sits at the **application layer (Layer 7)** of the OSI model, working on top of the PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) certificate chain issued by the SET Certificate Authority hierarchy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Walkthrough — The Four Phases of a Dual Signature Transaction

### Phase 1 — Pre-Computation by the Customer

The customer holds the following data items:
- **OI (Order Information)** — list of items, quantities, prices, shipping address.
- **PI (Payment Information)** — card number, expiry, amount, bank ID.

Two **independent cryptographic digests** are computed:

$$H(OI) = \text{SHA-1}(\text{Order Information bytes})$$

$$H(PI) = \text{SHA-1}(\text{Payment Information bytes})$$

### Phase 2 — Concatenation and Signing

The two digests are concatenated in a specific canonical order — **PI digest first, then OI digest** — and the resulting string is hashed again and signed with the customer's private key:

$$DS = E\bigl(PR_C,\; H\bigl(H(PI) \,\|\, H(OI)\bigr)\bigr)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **order of concatenation** is protocol-fixed (PI || OI) so that both merchant and gateway can independently re-compute the expected input. Swapping the order breaks signature verification — this is a classic KTU exam trap.

### Phase 3 — Transmission to the Merchant

The customer transmits the following triple to the merchant:

$$\text{Customer} \xrightarrow{\text{OI, } H(PI), DS, \text{Cert}(C)} \text{Merchant}$$

The merchant **does not receive PI**, only its hash. The merchant can verify the Dual Signature using the **customer's public key** recovered from the customer's digital certificate.

### Phase 4 — Transmission to the Payment Gateway

The merchant forwards the following to the bank (after adding its own signature):

$$\text{Merchant} \xrightarrow{\text{PI, } H(OI), DS, \text{Cert}(C), \text{Cert}(M)} \text{Payment Gateway}$$

The bank **does not receive OI**, only its hash.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Double-Hash Construction

| Layer | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| Outer $H(\cdot)$ | Compresses the concatenated digests to a fixed length (160 bits) suitable as input to RSA signing. |
| Inner $H(OI)$, $H(PI)$ | Provides **integrity** of each individual block before linking. |
| Concatenation $H(PI) \| H(OI)$ | Binds the two messages together so they cannot be swapped or substituted. |
| $E(PR_C, \cdot)$ | Provides **authenticity** and **non-repudiation** — only the customer could have produced this signature. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Two-Hash" Trick** is the heart of the Dual Signature. If the protocol used only $E(PR_C, H(OI) \| H(PI))$ directly, then verification would require *both* OI and PI, defeating the privacy goal. By pre-hashing each part, the merchant can verify OI using just $H(PI)$ (a 160-bit value) — and the bank can verify PI using just $H(OI)$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Used By |
|---|----------------------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | $DS = E\bigl(PR_C,\; H(H(PI) \,\|\, H(OI))\bigr)$ | Master Dual Signature equation | Customer (signing) |
| 2 | $H(OI) = \text{SHA-1}(OI)$ | Order Information digest | Both parties |
| 3 | $H(PI) = \text{SHA-1}(PI)$ | Payment Information digest | Both parties |
| 4 | $H(H(PI) \| H(OI)) = D\bigl(PU_C,\; DS\bigr)$ | Verification by recipient | Merchant / Bank |
| 5 | $\text{Match} \iff D\bigl(PU_C,\; DS\bigr) \stackrel{?}{=} H(H(PI) \,\|\, H(OI))$ | Integrity check | Verifier |
| 6 | $E(K_S, PI)$ | Symmetric encryption of PI under session key | Confidentiality layer |
| 7 | $E(PU_{PG}, K_S)$ | Asymmetric encryption of session key for Payment Gateway | Key transport |
| 8 | $\{OI\}_{DS} = \{OI, H(PI), DS\}$ | Signed-order packet sent to merchant | Customer → Merchant |
| 9 | $\{PI\}_{DS} = \{PI, H(OI), DS\}$ | Signed-payment packet sent to bank | Merchant → Bank |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU exam, $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute-value bars) inside table cells can break markdown rendering. Use `mod` or text descriptions instead.

## 2.4 Verification Logic — What Each Party Checks

### Merchant's Verification (on receiving the customer's packet)

1. Recompute $H'(OI) = \text{SHA-1}(OI)$.
2. Use the customer's public key $PU_C$ to recover $X = D(PU_C, DS)$.
3. Compute $Y = H(H(PI) \| H'(OI))$.
4. Accept the transaction **if and only if** $X = Y$.

✅ Merchant now **knows**: OI is authentic and unmodified.
❌ Merchant does **not know**: the contents of PI (only $H(PI)$).

### Bank's Verification (on receiving the merchant's forwarded packet)

1. Recompute $H'(PI) = \text{SHA-1}(PI)$.
2. Use the customer's public key $PU_C$ to recover $X = D(PU_C, DS)$.
3. Compute $Y = H(H'(PI) \| H(OI))$.
4. Accept the payment **if and only if** $X = Y$.

✅ Bank now **knows**: PI is authentic and unmodified.
❌ Bank does **not know**: the contents of OI (only $H(OI)$).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Dual Signature Concept Matters |
|--------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Online Payment Gateways** | SET-style DS prevents merchant-side card-data theft (the cause of breaches like the 2013 Target breach). |
| **Electronic Voting** | Voter sends "ballot" to election authority and "voter ID" to authentication server — DS links them without cross-disclosure. |
| **Healthcare Systems** | Patient sends "prescription request" to doctor and "insurance claim" to insurer — DS preserves medical privacy. |
| **Supply-Chain Trade Finance** | Buyer issues "purchase order" to seller and "letter of credit" to bank — DS ensures consistency without leaking terms. |
| **Blockchain / Smart Contracts** | Modern zero-knowledge proofs (zk-SNARKs) generalize the DS idea: prove two statements are linked without revealing either. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **2024 Scheme** syllabus, Dual Signature is studied as a *conceptual precursor* to **zero-knowledge proofs** and **commitment schemes** in modern cryptographic protocols.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Construction of a Dual Signature

We walk through the complete generation of a Dual Signature for a transaction. Assume:

- **Order Information (OI)** = `{"item":"Laptop","qty":1,"price":75000,"ship":"Kochi"}`
- **Payment Information (PI)** = `{"card":"xxxx-1234","amt":75000,"bank":"SBI"}`
- **Hash function** $H(\cdot)$ = SHA-1, producing **160-bit (20-byte)** digests.
- **Signature algorithm** = RSA-1024.

### Step 1 — Compute the Order Information Digest

$$H_1 = H(OI) = \text{SHA-1}(\text{OI bytes})$$

> **Logic:** A one-way hash produces a fixed-length 160-bit fingerprint of the variable-length order. Any modification to OI — even a single bit — will produce a completely different digest with overwhelming probability (≈ $1 - 2^{-160}$ collision resistance).

Output (illustrative 40 hex chars):

$$H_1 = \text{a3f5b8c2d1e9f0a4b6c7d8e9f0a1b2c3d4e5f607}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Payment Information Digest

$$H_2 = H(PI) = \text{SHA-1}(\text{PI bytes})$$

> **Logic:** Identical operation performed on the payment block. The hash hides the card number while still being a unique identifier of the PI block.

$$H_2 = \text{7e2d1c4b8a9f6e3d2c1b0a9f8e7d6c5b4a3f210}$$

### Step 3 — Concatenate the Two Digests

$$C = H_2 \,\|\, H_1$$

> **Logic:** The concatenation order is **PI-digest first, then OI-digest**, as specified by the SET specification. Total length: $160 + 160 = 320$ bits.

$$C = \text{7e2d1c4b...f210 \, \| \, a3f5b8c2...f607}$$

### Step 4 — Hash the Concatenation

$$H_3 = H(C) = \text{SHA-1}(H_2 \| H_1)$$

> **Logic:** This is the critical "second hash". It compresses the 320-bit concatenation back to a 160-bit value so that it can be fed into the 1024-bit RSA signature primitive. Without this second hash, the signature would need to operate on a 320-bit input, which is non-standard for RSA.

$$H_3 = \text{9c4d2e1f8a7b6c5d4e3f2a1b0c9d8e7f6a5b4c3}$$

### Step 5 — Sign with the Customer's Private Key

$$DS = E\bigl(PR_C,\; H_3\bigr)$$

> **Logic:** The RSA private key operation $E(PR_C, m) = m^d \mod n$ produces a 1024-bit signature. Only the customer knows $d$, so non-repudiation is guaranteed. The signature is **appended** to the message envelope, not embedded.

$$DS = \bigl[1024 \text{-bit RSA-encrypted block}\bigr]$$

### Step 6 — Assemble the Outgoing Packets

**To the merchant** (sends OI, not PI):

$$M_{\text{to\_M}} = \bigl\{\, OI,\; H_2,\; DS,\; \text{Cert}(C) \,\bigr\}$$

**To the bank via merchant** (sends PI, not OI):

$$M_{\text{to\_B}} = \bigl\{\, PI,\; H_1,\; DS,\; \text{Cert}(C),\; \text{Cert}(M) \,\bigr\}$$

> **Logic:** Each recipient receives the *full* DS and the *other* party's hash, allowing it to re-construct $C$ independently and verify the signature.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Verification by the Merchant

### Step M1 — Receive and Parse

Merchant receives $\{ OI, H_2, DS, \text{Cert}(C) \}$.

### Step M2 — Validate Certificate

Extract $PU_C$ from $\text{Cert}(C)$ (after verifying the CA's signature chain).

### Step M3 — Recover the Original Hash

$$H_3' = D(PU_C,\; DS) = DS^{\,e} \mod n$$

> **Logic:** RSA verification: $D(PU, c) = c^e \mod n$. This recovers the 160-bit value that the customer signed.

### Step M4 — Recompute Local Hash

$$H_1' = H(OI)$$

$$C' = H_2 \,\|\, H_1'$$

$$H_3'' = H(C')$$

### Step M5 — Compare

$$\text{Accept} \iff H_3' = H_3''$$

✅ **Acceptance confirms**: OI is unmodified and was signed by the holder of $PR_C$.
❌ **No information gained** about PI's contents.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Verification by the Payment Gateway (Bank)

### Step B1 — Receive and Parse

Bank receives $\{ PI, H_1, DS, \text{Cert}(C), \text{Cert}(M) \}$.

### Step B2 — Validate Both Certificates

Extract $PU_C$ from $\text{Cert}(C)$; extract $PU_M$ from $\text{Cert}(M)$.

### Step B3 — Recover the Original Hash

$$H_3' = D(PU_C,\; DS)$$

### Step B4 — Recompute Local Hash

$$H_2' = H(PI)$$

$$C' = H_2' \,\|\, H_1$$

$$H_3'' = H(C')$$

### Step B5 — Compare

$$\text{Accept} \iff H_3' = H_3''$$

✅ **Acceptance confirms**: PI is unmodified and was authorized by the holder of $PR_C$.
❌ **No information gained** about OI's contents.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Properties of the Dual Signature

| Security Property | Achieved? | Mechanism |
|-------------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Confidentiality of OI from Bank** | ✅ Yes | Bank receives only $H(OI)$ — pre-image resistance of SHA-1 makes recovery infeasible. |
| **Confidentiality of PI from Merchant** | ✅ Yes | Merchant receives only $H(PI)$ — pre-image resistance. |
| **Integrity of OI** | ✅ Yes | Any modification of OI changes $H(OI)$, breaking the DS verification. |
| **Integrity of PI** | ✅ Yes | Any modification of PI changes $H(PI)$, breaking the DS verification. |
| **Authentication of Customer** | ✅ Yes | Only $PR_C$ can produce a DS that verifies under $PU_C$. |
| **Non-repudiation** | ✅ Yes | Customer cannot deny signing; the CA-issued certificate binds $PU_C$ to identity. |
| **Linkability (OI ↔ PI binding)** | ✅ Yes | The single DS commits to **both** digests simultaneously. |
| **Replay Protection** | ⚠ Partial | Requires a **transaction nonce / timestamp** layered on top (not part of DS itself). |
| **Forward Secrecy** | ❌ No | If $PR_C$ is later compromised, all past DS become forgeable. |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Simplified 4-bit Hash Analogy)

To make the math concrete, let us use a **toy 4-bit hash** that simply XORs the input with the constant `0xF`:

| Quantity | Symbolic | Toy Value |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| OI | `"Laptop"` | `(binary: 01001100 ...)` |
| $H(OI)$ | `OI XOR 0xF` | `0x3` |
| PI | `"CardData"` | `(binary: 11001010 ...)` |
| $H(PI)$ | `PI XOR 0xF` | `0x5` |
| $C$ | `H(PI) ‖ H(OI)` | `0x5 ‖ 0x3 = 0x53` |
| $H(C)$ | `C XOR 0xF` | `0x53 XOR 0xF = 0x5C` |
| $DS$ | `E(PR_C, 0x5C)` | (RSA encryption of `0x5C`) |

**Merchant verification:**

$$H_3' = D(PU_C, DS) = 0x5C$$

$$H(OI) = 0x3$$

$$H_2 ‖ H(OI) = 0x5 ‖ 0x3 = 0x53$$

$$H(0x53) = 0x5C = H_3' \quad \checkmark$$

**Bank verification (mirror):**

$$H(PI) = 0x5$$

$$H_2' ‖ H_1 = 0x5 ‖ 0x3 = 0x53$$

$$H(0x53) = 0x5C = H_3' \quad \checkmark$$

Both verifications succeed using **only the hash of the other party's block**, proving the privacy-preserving property of the Dual Signature.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Dual Signature Generation (Customer Side)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Order Information OI] --> B1[SHA-1 Hash]
    C[Payment Information PI] --> B2[SHA-1 Hash]
    B1 --> D[Concatenate H PI then H OI]
    B2 --> D
    D --> E[SHA-1 Hash of Concatenation]
    E --> F[RSA Sign with Private Key PRc]
    F --> G[Dual Signature DS]
    A --> H[Packet to Merchant OI plus H PI plus DS]
    G --> H
    C --> I[Encrypt PI with Session Key]
    I --> J[Encrypt Session Key with PU of Bank]
    J --> K[Packet to Bank PI encrypted plus H OI plus DS]
    G --> K
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — End-to-End SET Transaction with Dual Signature

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Customer
    participant M as Merchant
    participant PG as Payment Gateway
    participant CA as Certificate Authority

    C->>CA: Request Customer Certificate
    CA-->>C: Issue Cert C
    M->>CA: Request Merchant Certificate
    CA-->>M: Issue Cert M

    C->>C: Compute H OI and H PI
    C->>C: Compute DS equals Sign PRc on H of H PI concat H OI

    C->>M: Send OI, H PI, DS, Cert C
    M->>M: Verify DS using PUc and H OI
    M-->>C: Order Acknowledgement

    M->>PG: Forward PI encrypted, H OI, DS, Cert C, Cert M
    PG->>PG: Decrypt PI using private gateway key
    PG->>PG: Verify DS using PUc and H PI
    PG-->>M: Payment Authorisation
    M-->>C: Order Confirmed
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — SET Participants and Data Flows

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cardholder
        C1[Customer Browser or Wallet]
        C2[Customer Private Key PRc]
        C3[Customer Public Key Certificate Cert C]
    end

    subgraph Merchant
        M1[Merchant Server]
        M2[Merchant Private Key PRm]
        M3[Merchant Public Key Certificate Cert M]
    end

    subgraph Acquirer
        A1[Payment Gateway]
        A2[Gateway Private Key PRpg]
        A3[Gateway Public Key Certificate Cert PG]
    end

    subgraph CA_Hierarchy
        X1[Root CA]
        X2[Brand CA]
        X3[Issuer CA]
    end

    C1 -- OI and H PI and DS --> M1
    C1 -- Encrypted PI and H OI and DS --> M1
    M1 -- Forwards to Acquirer --> A1
    X1 --> X2
    X2 --> X3
    X3 --> C3
    X3 --> M3
    X3 --> A3
```

## 4.4 State Machine — Dual Signature Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Hashing: Customer initiates SET purchase
    Hashing --> Concatenating: OI and PI digests computed
    Concatenating --> Signing: H of H PI concat H OI computed
    Signing --> Dispatched: DS produced via RSA private key
    Dispatched --> MerchantVerifying: Merchant receives OI H PI DS
    MerchantVerifying --> Dispatched: Signature mismatch retry
    MerchantVerifying --> GatewayForward: Signature valid
    GatewayForward --> GatewayVerifying: Bank receives PI H OI DS
    GatewayVerifying --> Authorised: Bank signature valid
    GatewayVerifying --> Declined: Bank signature mismatch
    Authorised --> [*]
    Declined --> [*]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cross-Verification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Merchant_Verification_Engine
        MV1[Receive OI]
        MV2[Receive H PI]
        MV3[Receive DS]
        MV4[Recompute H OI]
        MV5[Form C prime equals H PI concat H OI]
        MV6[Hash C prime]
        MV7[Decrypt DS with PUc]
        MV8{Compare}
    end

    MV1 --> MV4
    MV2 --> MV5
    MV4 --> MV5
    MV5 --> MV6
    MV3 --> MV7
    MV6 --> MV8
    MV7 --> MV8
    MV8 -- Equal --> MV9[Accept OI]
    MV8 -- Not Equal --> MV10[Reject Transaction]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Dual Signature. Why is the order of concatenation H(PI) ‖ H(OI) fixed in the SET protocol?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Dual Signature is a cryptographic technique used in the SET protocol that allows a customer to sign two related messages — the Order Information (OI) and the Payment Information (PI) — in such a way that:
- The merchant can verify the integrity of OI without seeing PI.
- The bank can verify the integrity of PI without seeing OI.
- Both parties can confirm that the OI and PI belong to the **same transaction**.

The mathematical expression is $DS = E(PR_C, H(H(PI) \| H(OI)))$.

**Why order is fixed (1 mark):** The concatenation order $H(PI) \| H(OI)$ is **protocol-specified** so that both the merchant and the bank can independently reconstruct the same input string when verifying the signature. If the order were variable, the hash computed at the verifier side would not match the original, causing verification to fail. Fixing the order is a **deterministic** requirement for verification reproducibility.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List any three security properties provided by the Dual Signature in SET.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Confidentiality** — The merchant cannot view the contents of PI, and the bank cannot view the contents of OI. Only the digest of the other party's message is shared.
2. **Integrity** — Any modification to either OI or PI causes a hash mismatch, leading to verification failure and transaction rejection.
3. **Authentication and Non-Repudiation** — Since the DS is produced using the customer's private key $PR_C$, only the genuine customer could have signed the message, and the customer cannot later deny the transaction.

*(Additional valid properties: linkability/binding between OI and PI, replay protection with nonces — partial credit may be awarded.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering Dual Signature questions, students **commonly lose marks** for:
> 1. **Swapping the concatenation order** — writing $H(OI) \| H(PI)$ instead of $H(PI) \| H(OI)$. The latter is the SET standard.
> 2. **Omitting the outer hash** — writing $E(PR_C, H(PI) \| H(OI))$ instead of $E(PR_C, H(H(PI) \| H(OI)))$. The double-hash is **mandatory**.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention certificate exchange** — verification requires the customer's public key from a CA-issued certificate.
> 4. **Confusing OI and PI in the verification flow** — students often state that the merchant verifies PI; he does not.

---

### Question B (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4]`

**(a) Explain the concept of Dual Signature in the SET protocol. Illustrate the complete process of generation and verification with a neat diagram. (7 marks)**

**(b) A customer Alice is performing a SET transaction. The OI digest is $H(OI) = \text{0xA1B2}$ and the PI digest is $H(PI) = \text{0xC3D4}$. Show all the steps to compute the Dual Signature and explain how the merchant verifies it **without seeing PI**. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):** The Dual Signature (DS) is the cryptographic construct in SET that allows a customer to sign two related messages OI and PI such that the merchant and the bank can each verify the integrity of their respective message and confirm the binding between them — without cross-disclosure.

**Generation Algorithm (2 marks):**

1. Compute $H(OI) = \text{SHA-1}(OI)$.
2. Compute $H(PI) = \text{SHA-1}(PI)$.
3. Concatenate: $C = H(PI) \| H(OI)$.
4. Compute the outer hash: $H_3 = \text{SHA-1}(C)$.
5. Sign: $DS = E(PR_C, H_3)$.

**Verification by the Merchant (2 marks):**

1. Recompute $H'(OI)$ from the received OI.
2. Form $C' = H(PI) \| H'(OI)$.
3. Compute $H_3'' = \text{SHA-1}(C')$.
4. Recover $H_3' = D(PU_C, DS)$ using the customer's public key.
5. Accept iff $H_3' = H_3''$.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $H(OI) = \text{0xA1B2}$, $H(PI) = \text{0xC3D4}$.

**Step 1 — Concatenate the digests (1 mark):**

$$C = H(PI) \,\|\, H(OI) = \text{0xC3D4} \,\|\, \text{0xA1B2} = \text{0xC3D4A1B2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the outer hash (1 mark):**

$$H_3 = \text{SHA-1}(C) = \text{0xC3D4A1B2} \to \text{0xE5F6A7B8C9D0E1F2A3B4C5D6E7F80910}$$

*(Use any 160-bit deterministic value for illustration.)*

**Step 3 — Sign with customer's private key (1 mark):**

$$DS = E\bigl(PR_{\text{Alice}},\; \text{0xE5F6A7B8C9D0E1F2A3B4C5D6E7F80910}\bigr) = \text{0x[1024-bit RSA ciphertext]}$$

**Step 4 — Transmit to merchant (1 mark):**

Alice sends the merchant the packet:

$$\{\, OI,\; H(PI) = \text{0xC3D4},\; DS,\; \text{Cert(Alice)} \,\}$$

The merchant does **not** receive $PI$ (only its 16-bit digest 0xC3D4).

**Step 5 — Merchant verification (2 marks):**

1. Recompute $H'(OI) = \text{0xA1B2}$ from the received OI.
2. Concatenate: $C' = \text{0xC3D4} \| \text{0xA1B2} = \text{0xC3D4A1B2}$.
3. Compute $H_3'' = \text{SHA-1}(C') = \text{0xE5F6...0910}$.
4. Recover $H_3' = D(PU_{\text{Alice}}, DS) = \text{0xE5F6...0910}$.
5. Compare: $H_3' = H_3''$ ✅ ⇒ **OI accepted**.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The merchant verifies OI using **only the 16-bit hash of PI** (0xC3D4). He never sees Alice's card number, expiry, or amount — preserving payment confidentiality.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4]`

**(a) Discuss the role of hashing and asymmetric encryption in Dual Signature construction. Why is a double-hash required instead of a single-hash? (7 marks)**

**(b) Compare the verification steps performed by the merchant and the payment gateway in a SET transaction. How does each party gain assurance without violating the other's privacy? (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Role of Hashing (3 marks):**

1. **Fixed-length compression** — OI and PI are variable-length; SHA-1 produces a uniform 160-bit digest regardless of input size.
2. **Tamper detection** — Any modification to OI or PI changes its hash, immediately detectable at verification.
3. **Privacy preservation** — The hash is a *one-way* function: knowing $H(PI)$ does not allow recovery of $PI$ (pre-image resistance). This is what enables the merchant to verify OI while remaining ignorant of PI.
4. **Input preparation for RSA** — The outer hash reduces the 320-bit concatenation to a single 160-bit value, matching RSA's input size constraints.

**Role of Asymmetric Encryption (2 marks):**

1. **Authentication** — The private key $PR_C$ is exclusive to the customer; only she can produce a valid signature.
2. **Non-repudiation** — The signature is verifiable by anyone holding $PU_C$ (from the certificate), but unforgeable.
3. **Binding** — Signing the *combined* digest binds OI and PI into a single atomic commitment.

**Why Double-Hash (2 marks):** A single-hash construction such as $E(PR_C, H(PI) \| H(OI))$ would require the verifier to have **both** OI and PI to recompute the hash, defeating the privacy goal. The **double-hash** scheme $E(PR_C, H(H(PI) \| H(OI)))$ allows each party to verify using only **one full message + one digest of the other**, achieving the asymmetric privacy property that defines SET.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Merchant's Verification | Payment Gateway's Verification |
|--------|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Receives | $OI$, $H(PI)$, $DS$, Cert(C) | $PI$, $H(OI)$, $DS$, Cert(C), Cert(M) |
| Recomputes | $H'(OI)$ from OI | $H'(PI)$ from PI |
| Concatenates | $H(PI) \| H'(OI)$ (uses *received* $H(PI)$) | $H'(PI) \| H(OI)$ (uses *received* $H(OI)$) |
| Hashes | Outer SHA-1 of concatenation | Outer SHA-1 of concatenation |
| Decrypts DS | Using $PU_C$ from Cert(C) | Using $PU_C$ from Cert(C) |
| Compares | Decrypted value vs. computed hash | Decrypted value vs. computed hash |
| Verifies OI? | ✅ Yes (integrity + authenticity) | ❌ No (only sees $H(OI)$) |
| Verifies PI? | ❌ No (only sees $H(PI)$) | ✅ Yes (integrity + authenticity) |
| Privacy | OI visible to merchant; PI hidden | PI visible to bank; OI hidden |

**How Assurance is Gained Without Privacy Violation (3 marks):**

1. The **linkage** between OI and PI is cryptographically sealed in the DS. Each verifier, although holding only one full message, can re-derive the *same* combined hash and confirm it matches the decrypted DS. This proves the two messages are part of a single transaction.
2. **Hash pre-image resistance** ensures that holding $H(OI)$ reveals nothing about OI's content, and holding $H(PI)$ reveals nothing about PI's content.
3. **Certificate-based PKI** ensures that the $PU_C$ used in verification is genuinely bound to the customer, preventing impersonation.
4. Thus, the merchant is assured that the order is genuine and tied to a valid payment instruction, and the bank is assured that the payment is genuine and tied to a valid order — yet **neither party sees the other's sensitive data**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dual Signature (SET Protocol)**

- ✅ Dual Signature is a **SET-specific** mechanism designed for **privacy-preserving multi-party e-commerce**.
- ✅ Master equation: $DS = E\bigl(PR_C,\; H(H(PI) \| H(OI))\bigr)$.
- ✅ The concatenation order is **PI-digest FIRST, then OI-digest** — never the reverse.
- ✅ **Double hashing is mandatory** — first hash each block, then hash the concatenation again before signing.
- ✅ Merchant receives: $OI, H(PI), DS$ — does **not** see $PI$.
- ✅ Bank receives: $PI, H(OI), DS$ — does **not** see $OI$.
- ✅ Both verifiers recover $H_3 = D(PU_C, DS)$ and compare it with their locally computed outer hash.
- ✅ Verification equality $H_3' = H_3''$ guarantees **integrity + authenticity + linkage** in a single step.
- ✅ **Certificates are essential** — the verifier must obtain $PU_C$ from a CA-issued certificate before verification.
- ✅ DS provides: confidentiality, integrity, authentication, non-repudiation, and OI↔PI binding.
- ✅ DS does **not** by itself provide **replay protection** — a transaction nonce/timestamp must be added externally.
- ✅ DS does **not** provide **forward secrecy** — compromise of $PR_C$ retroactively invalidates all past DS.
- ✅ Modern analogues of DS include **zero-knowledge proofs**, **commitment schemes**, and **hash-based one-time signatures** used in blockchain protocols.
- ✅ The SHA-1 algorithm in classic SET is now considered weak; modern implementations use **SHA-256** with **RSA-2048** or **ECDSA P-256**.
- ✅ Always state the **three guarantees** in the exam — merchant-only-PI-hash, bank-only-OI-hash, and same-transaction linkage — for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Payment Processing, Firewall Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Payment Processing, Firewall Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Payment Processing — Secure Electronic Transaction (SET)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)
**Secure Electronic Transaction (SET)** is an open cryptographic protocol specification developed jointly by **Visa** and **Mastercard** (1996, with IBM, Netscape, RSA, SAIC, GTE, Terisa, and VeriSign) for securing credit/debit card transactions over open networks such as the Internet. SET provides **confidentiality of payment information, integrity of the transmitted data, authentication of all participants, and non-repudiation** of the transaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SET Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to master the **SET participants (cardholder, merchant, issuer, acquirer, payment gateway, CA), the Dual Signature mechanism, and the three transaction flows (Purchase Request, Payment Authorization, Payment Capture).**

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Triple-Sealed Courier" Intuition
Imagine you want to order a laptop from a foreign merchant. You need to send the merchant your order details (what you want) and your bank the payment details (your card info), but you don't want the merchant to see your card and you don't want the bank to know what you're buying. 

SET solves this with a **Dual Signature** — a single mathematical signature that **binds** two separate documents together. The merchant gets proof you authorized the *order*, the bank gets proof you authorized the *payment*, and the two are cryptographically linked — but neither side sees the other side's private details. Think of it as a notarized **carbon-copy contract** where the customer signs once, the merchant sees the "what," the bank sees the "how much," but the ink links both halves.

---

## 1.2 Firewall Characteristics

### Formal Definition
A **Firewall** is a hardware and/or software system that enforces an organization's security policy by acting as a controlled gateway (choke point) between two or more networks — typically an internal trusted network and an external untrusted network (e.g., the Internet). It examines and filters inbound and outbound traffic based on predefined rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rebuttable Definition (Stallings):** *"A firewall is a collection of components placed between two networks that collectively enforce a security policy between them."* The KTU 2024 module 4 outcome CO4 directly maps to this definition.

### Three Mandatory Characteristics of Any Firewall (RFC 2979 / Stallings)
1. **All traffic must pass through it** (single chokepoint enforcement).
2. **Only authorized traffic — as defined by the local security policy — is permitted to pass.**
3. **The firewall itself must be immune to penetration** (host-based hardening, "bastion" trust).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer at a Club" Intuition
Picture a fancy nightclub. The bouncer (firewall) stands at the **only** entrance/exit. He checks a guest list (security policy) and only lets in people who are on it. The bouncer is well-trained, screened, and not easily fooled (immune to penetration). Anyone trying to sneak in through a window or the back door is breaking the rules. Inside, the VIPs and regular guests are protected. That's exactly what a firewall does for a private corporate network.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the "Three Properties"** — they are a 3-mark direct question in every KTU ESE since the 2019 scheme. Any answer missing all three is half-correct.

---

## 1.3 Module 4 Outcomes Mapped (KTU 2024 CO4)

| CO | Outcome Description | Topic Coverage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CO4** | Apply the principles of cryptographic protocols to design secure payment systems and describe firewall architectures. | SET Protocol, Dual Signature, Firewall types & characteristics |
| **CO5** | Evaluate network perimeter defenses for an enterprise. | Firewall design principles, DMZ architecture |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SET Protocol — Participants (Six Entities)

| # | Participant | Role | Symbolic Notation |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | **Cardholder** | Customer who owns the card | C |
| 2 | **Merchant** | Sells goods/services to cardholder | M |
| 3 | **Issuer** | Cardholder's bank — issued the card | I |
| 4 | **Acquirer** | Merchant's bank — handles merchant's account | A |
| 5 | **Payment Gateway** | Bridge between Internet and the bankcard financial network | PG |
| 6 | **Certificate Authority (CA)** | Issues X.509 certificates binding keys ↔ identities | CA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two banks?** The *Issuer* knows the cardholder; the *Acquirer* knows the merchant. They never directly share secrets. The **Payment Gateway** acts as the trusted bridge between the Internet-side SET world and the legacy bankcard network (e.g., VisaNet).

---

## 2.2 SET — The Dual Signature (Heart of the Protocol)

The **Dual Signature (DS)** is the cryptographic invention that lets two parties each verify that the cardholder signed the *combined* transaction, while only seeing their half.

Let:
- **OI** = Order Information (sent to Merchant)
- **PI** = Payment Information (sent to Acquirer/Issuer)

The cardholder computes the Dual Signature as:

$$DS = E_{KR_C}\Big( H(H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI)) \Big)$$

Where:
- $H(\cdot)$ is a **one-way hash function** (SET uses SHA-1 historically).
- $KR_C$ is the cardholder's **private signature key**.
- The double hash ensures that *only* the **PI** owner (Acquirer) sees the full PI, and *only* the **OI** owner (Merchant) sees the full OI, but both are bound.

> [!NOTE]
> **Message Recovery Trick:** The Merchant is given $\vert H(PI) \vert$ in a "PI Message Authentication Code" (PIMAC) envelope — enough to verify, not enough to read PI. The Acquirer is given $\vert H(OI) \vert$ in OIMAC — enough to verify, not enough to read OI. This achieves **confidentiality + linked integrity**.

### Why "Dual"?
A *single* signature would let the merchant read the card and the bank read the order — violating need-to-know. A *separate* signature for each document would not bind them — the merchant could swap OIs after authorization. **Dual Signature binds both messages into one unforgeable proof, with each party seeing only its half.**

---

## 2.3 SET — Three Transaction Flows (High-Yield Steps)

### Flow 1: Purchase Request
1. C → M: `Initiate Request` (gives M cardholder's brand + transaction ID)
2. M → C: `Initiate Response` (sends merchant's certificate + transaction ID)
3. C → M: `Purchase Request` (encrypted: OI for M, PI for Acquirer, both with Dual Signature)

### Flow 2: Payment Authorization
1. M → Acquirer: `Authorization Request` (forwards PI, OI hash, DS)
2. Acquirer verifies DS, decrypts PI
3. Acquirer → Payment Gateway → Issuer: `Authorization Request` (legacy bankcard net)
4. Issuer verifies, approves/declines
5. Acquirer → M: `Authorization Response` (signed)
6. M → C: `Purchase Response` (confirmation)

### Flow 3: Payment Capture
1. M → Acquirer: `Capture Request` (with signed transaction data)
2. Acquirer → Issuer: `Capture Request`
3. Issuer → Acquirer → M: `Capture Response` (funds transferred)

---

## 2.4 Firewall — Design Principles (Stallings / Cheswick)

A firewall must embody these **6 design tenets** to be considered enterprise-grade:

| # | Principle | Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Least Privilege** | Deny by default; allow only explicitly permitted traffic. |
| 2 | **Defense in Depth** | Layered controls — firewall + IDS + antivirus + logging. |
| 3 | **Choke Point** | Single (or few) controlled point between trusted and untrusted. |
| 4 | **Fail-Safe Stance** | On failure, *deny* traffic (fail-closed), not permit. |
| 5 | **Universal Participation** | All hosts in the protected network must be covered. |
| 6 | **Diversity of Defense** | Different vendors/mechanisms at each layer (avoid monoculture). |

---

## 2.5 Firewall — Types (Four Canonical Generations)

| Type | OSI Layer | What It Inspects | Pros | Cons |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Packet-Filtering Router** | L3 / L4 | IP headers (src/dst, ports, flags) | Fast, cheap, transparent | No state, no payload inspection, susceptible to IP spoofing |
| **Stateful Inspection** | L3 / L4 | L3/L4 + state table of connections | Detects forged packets, tracks TCP state | More resource-intensive |
| **Application-Level Gateway (Proxy)** | L7 | Entire application payload (HTTP, FTP, SMTP) | Deep inspection, content filtering, anti-virus hook | Application-specific, breaks protocols, latency |
| **Circuit-Level Gateway** | L5 (Session) | TCP handshake legitimacy | Hides internal IP, simple | No payload inspection |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** *"Plain Stack State App Ckt"* — packet filter, stateful inspection, app gateway, circuit gateway. (Some vendors classify **NGFW — Next-Generation Firewall** as a 5th type, combining deep packet inspection with IDS/IPS.)

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dual Signature Generation** | $DS = E_{KR_C}\big(H(H(PI)\Vert H(OI))\big)$ | $KR_C$ = cardholder private sig key | SHA-1 used in SET v1 |
| **Dual Signature Verification (Merchant side)** | $D_{KU_C}(DS) \overset{?}{=} H(H(PI)\Vert H(OI))$ | $KU_C$ = cardholder public sig key | Merchant holds PIMAC with $H(PI)$ |
| **Hash of a Message** | $h = H(M)$ | $M$ = message | One-way, collision-resistant |
| **Digital Signature (generic)** | $S = E_{KR_A}(H(M))$ | $A$ = signer | Verified via $D_{KU_A}(S) \overset{?}{=} H(M)$ |
| **Firewall Throughput Estimate** | $T_{fw} = \dfrac{P_{avg}}{L_{pkt} \cdot 8 / C_{link}}$ | $P_{avg}$ avg packets/sec, $L_{pkt}$ avg length (bytes), $C_{link}$ link bps | Used in sizing exercises |
| **Rule Table Lookup (Packet Filter)** | $\text{action} = f(\text{src IP}, \text{dst IP}, \text{src port}, \text{dst port}, \text{protocol}, \text{flags})$ | — | First-match semantics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas above are reproducible in the KTU ESE. The Dual Signature expression is the **single most important** formula in Module 4 — at least one 7-mark sub-part of the Module 4 question will require you to write it from memory and explain the rationale.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **SET** → laid the cryptographic groundwork for **3-D Secure (3DS)**, the protocol behind "Verified by Visa" / "Mastercard SecureCode" used in trillions of dollars of online transactions annually.
- **Firewalls** → evolve into **NGFW → UTM (Unified Threat Management) → SASE (Secure Access Service Edge)** architectures, forming the spine of every enterprise **Zero-Trust** network.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Dual Signature Creation & Verification

The Dual Signature is the conceptual center of the SET protocol. Below is the complete step-by-step logic a cardholder's wallet software performs.

### Step 1 — Cardholder Composes the Two Messages
The cardholder's wallet (e.g., Microsoft Wallet, SET-compliant e-wallet) prepares:
- **OI** (Order Information) — list of items, prices, total, merchant ID, etc.
- **PI** (Payment Information) — card number (PAN), expiry, amount, order ID, etc.

### Step 2 — Cardholder Hashes Both Messages
The wallet computes two cryptographic digests using a **one-way hash function** (SET originally specified SHA-1, with MD5 in earlier drafts; modern banking prefers SHA-256 family).

$$
H_{OI} = H(OI) \qquad H_{PI} = H(PI)
$$

These digests act as compact fingerprints (160 bits for SHA-1, 256 bits for SHA-256).

### Step 3 — Cardholder Concatenates the Two Hashes
The two digests are concatenated (PI-hash first, then OI-hash — this order is protocol-fixed):

$$
D_{concat} = H_{PI} \;\Vert\; H_{OI}
$$

### Step 4 — Cardholder Hashes the Concatenation
A second hash is applied — this is the **outer hash**:

$$
H_{outer} = H(D_{concat}) = H\big(H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI)\big)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why double-hash?** Each hash is only 160 bits. Concatenation gives 320 bits which exceeds typical RSA hash encoding limits in some implementations. Double-hashing folds the pair back into a single fixed-size digest that the signature algorithm (typically **RSA-1024** in SET v1) can sign directly.

### Step 5 — Cardholder Signs with Private Key
The cardholder signs the outer hash using their **RSA private signature key** ($KR_C$), which is bound to their X.509 certificate issued by a CA:

$$
DS = E_{KR_C}\big(H_{outer}\big) = E_{KR_C}\Big(H\big(H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI)\big)\Big)
$$

This is the **Dual Signature** (DS). It binds PI and OI together.

### Step 6 — Cardholder Encrypts Each Half for the Right Recipient
- **OI** is encrypted with the Merchant's public key $KU_M$ (confidentiality) and sent to the merchant.
- **PI** is encrypted with the Acquirer's public key $KU_A$ and sent along a separate channel.

Crucially, the merchant **also receives** $H(PI)$ (PIMAC — PI Message Authentication Code) — *but not PI itself*. Similarly, the acquirer receives $H(OI)$ (OIMAC) — *but not OI itself*.

### Step 7 — Merchant Verifies DS (sees only the OI half)
The merchant:
1. Decrypts OI using $KR_M$.
2. Computes $H(OI)$ locally.
3. Uses $H(PI)$ received in the PIMAC (or extracts it from a signed envelope).
4. Computes $H\big(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)\big)$.
5. Verifies with the cardholder's public key $KU_C$:

$$
D_{KU_C}(DS) \;\overset{?}{=}\; H\big(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)\big)
$$

If yes → the cardholder signed *both* the order and the payment together, but the merchant has zero visibility into the actual card number.

### Step 8 — Acquirer Verifies DS Symmetrically
The acquirer independently runs the same verification using $H(OI)$ (received in OIMAC) and the decrypted $H(PI)$. Both sides confirm cryptographic binding without seeing each other's full data.

### Step 9 — Why It Cannot Be Forged or Replayed
- A merchant cannot substitute a different OI because $H(OI)$ in the signature would no longer match.
- An acquirer cannot substitute a different PI for the same reason.
- Replay of a captured old DS fails because each transaction carries a unique **transaction ID** + timestamp verified by the issuer.

### Complete Aligned Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & H_{OI} = H(OI), \quad H_{PI} = H(PI) \\
\text{Step 2: } & D = H_{PI} \;\Vert\; H_{OI} \\
\text{Step 3: } & H_{outer} = H(D) \\
\text{Step 4: } & DS = E_{KR_C}(H_{outer}) \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{Merchant verifies: } D_{KU_C}(DS) \;\overset{?}{=}\; H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)) \\
\text{Step 6: } & \text{Acquirer verifies: } D_{KU_C}(DS) \;\overset{?}{=}\; H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)) \\
\text{Result: } & \text{Order and payment cryptographically linked, partially hidden.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — SET vs SSL/TLS (High-Yield Comparison for KTU)

| Feature | SET | SSL/TLS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Primary Goal | Secure credit-card transactions on the Internet | Secure channel between two sockets |
| Participants | 6 (Cardholder, Merchant, Issuer, Acquirer, PG, CA) | 2 (Client, Server) |
| Dual Signature | **Yes** (core innovation) | No |
| Certificates Required | Both parties (C, M, Acquirer, PG) — multi-CA | Usually server-only (one-way) |
| Confidentiality of Card | Card number never revealed to merchant | Card number visible to merchant |
| Application Layer | Application-specific (payment wallet) | Application-transparent |
| Complexity | Very high; complex PKI | Moderate |
| Real-world adoption | Limited (replaced by 3-D Secure) | Universal (HTTPS) |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Firewall Types Capability Scoring

| Capability | Packet Filter | Stateful | App Gateway | Circuit GW |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Source/Dest IP check | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Port/protocol check | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| TCP state tracking | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Payload inspection | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| User authentication | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Logging per app command | ❌ | Limited | ✅ | ❌ |
| Performance | **High** | High | Low | Medium |
| Transparency to user | High | High | **Low** | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> A **stateful inspection firewall** is the de-facto industry default in mid-range enterprise. A **proxy (application gateway)** is mandatory where deep content filtering is needed (e.g., SQL-injection blocking, malware detection on HTTP body).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]
**List and explain the three mandatory characteristics of a firewall.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
According to the canonical definition (Stallings / RFC 2979), any firewall must satisfy the following three properties:
1. **Single Choke Point (All traffic must pass through it):** The firewall is the only path between the internal and external networks. If attackers can bypass it, the firewall is useless.
2. **Enforce Security Policy (Only authorized traffic is allowed):** It must be able to inspect traffic and admit/deny based on a defined policy. Default-deny is preferred.
3. **Immune to Penetration (Hardened):** The firewall itself must be a hardened, monitored system (bastion host principles) — operating system patches removed, only firewall software running, audited logs, since its compromise breaks the entire security perimeter.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark per characteristic. Award 0.5 extra for mentioning "bastion host hardening.")*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the Dual Signature in SET? Why is it required when both OI and PI are signed separately?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Dual Signature (DS)** is a cryptographic construct in the SET protocol that cryptographically binds two distinct messages — the **Order Information (OI)** sent to the merchant and the **Payment Information (PI)** sent to the acquirer — into a single unforgeable signature generated by the cardholder.

**Mathematical form:**

$$DS = E_{KR_C}\Big(H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI))\Big)$$

**Why it is required (vs. two separate signatures):**
- **Confidentiality:** If two separate signatures were used, both OI and PI would need to be sent to both parties, breaking the need-to-know principle. The merchant would see the card number, the acquirer would see the order. Dual signature avoids this by sharing *only the hash* of the other party's data.
- **Integrity binding:** A single DS cryptographically links OI and PI. If the merchant tries to swap the OI with a different one, $H(OI)$ will not match the one inside the signature, and the acquirer (verifying with the received $H(OI)$ in the OIMAC) will detect the swap. With two independent signatures, the merchant could alter the OI without invalidating the PI signature, and the bank would never know the order was changed.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for binding vs. confidentiality explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **SET protocol** in detail. List all six participants and describe their roles. Draw the Purchase Request transaction flow.

**(b) [7 marks]** What is the **Dual Signature** in SET? Derive the step-by-step process for the cardholder to generate the Dual Signature. Show the verification steps performed by the Merchant and the Acquirer.

---

### Model Solution A(a) — SET Protocol & Participants (7 marks)

**SET (Secure Electronic Transaction)** is a comprehensive cryptographic protocol standard (Visa + Mastercard, 1996) designed to secure credit-card transactions over open networks like the Internet. It ensures **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation** for all parties.

**Six Participants (1.5 marks):**

| # | Participant | Role |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Cardholder (C)** | Authorized holder of a payment card; initiates the purchase using a SET-compliant e-wallet. |
| 2 | **Merchant (M)** | Sells goods/services; receives Order Information, never sees the card number in clear. |
| 3 | **Issuer (I)** | Cardholder's bank — issues the card, ultimately authorizes/declines the transaction. |
| 4 | **Acquirer (A)** | Merchant's bank — processes the authorization request on behalf of the merchant. |
| 5 | **Payment Gateway (PG)** | Bridge between the Internet (SET world) and the private bankcard financial network (legacy). |
| 6 | **Certificate Authority (CA)** | Trusted third party that issues and verifies **X.509v3 digital certificates** binding public keys to identities. |

**Purchase Request Flow (3.5 marks):**

1. **Initiate Request (C → M):** The cardholder's wallet contacts the merchant server, sending the card brand (Visa/MC) and a unique transaction ID.
2. **Initiate Response (M → C):** The merchant replies with its X.509 certificate (binding its public key to its identity), the transaction ID, and a list of supported CAs.
3. **Purchase Request (C → M):** The wallet constructs **OI** and **PI**, computes the **Dual Signature**, encrypts OI with the merchant's public key and PI with the acquirer's public key, and sends the bundle. It also includes a **PIMAC** (containing $H(PI)$) so the merchant can verify the DS without seeing PI, and the wallet's own certificate.
4. **Merchant Processing:** The merchant decrypts OI, verifies the DS using the wallet's public key (extracted from the certificate), forwards PI (still encrypted for the acquirer) along with the OI hash to the acquirer for authorization.
5. **Purchase Response (M → C):** Once the merchant receives the acquirer's signed authorization, it sends a confirmation to the cardholder.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall — Exam Valuation Warning:**
> Students frequently confuse the **Issuer** with the **Acquirer**. Remember: **Issuer = Cardholder's bank, Acquirer = Merchant's bank.** If you swap them, you lose 0.5 marks per occurrence. Examiners specifically test this confusion in Module 4.

---

### Model Solution A(b) — Dual Signature (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** The Dual Signature is a cryptographic construct that binds the Order Information (OI) and Payment Information (PI) into a single unforgeable signature, allowing the merchant to verify the OI half and the acquirer to verify the PI half **without either seeing the other's full data**.

**Generation Steps (Cardholder side) — (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & H_{OI} = H(OI) \\
\text{Step 2: } & H_{PI} = H(PI) \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Concatenate: } C = H_{PI} \Vert H_{OI} \\
\text{Step 4: } & H_{outer} = H(C) \\
\text{Step 5: } & DS = E_{KR_C}(H_{outer})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification — Merchant side (1.5 marks):**

The merchant has $OI$ (decrypted), $H(PI)$ (from PIMAC), and the cardholder's public key $KU_C$. The merchant:

1. Computes $H(OI)$ locally.
2. Concatenates with the received $H(PI)$: $C' = H(PI) \Vert H(OI)$.
3. Computes $H(C')$.
4. Decrypts the DS with $KU_C$ and checks:

$$
D_{KU_C}(DS) \;\overset{?}{=}\; H(C')
$$

If equal, the DS is valid — the merchant has cryptographic proof that the cardholder signed *this* OI and that the PI is genuinely linked to it, without ever seeing the card.

**Verification — Acquirer side (1.5 marks):**

The acquirer has $PI$ (decrypted), $H(OI)$ (from OIMAC), and $KU_C$. The acquirer performs the symmetric verification:

$$
D_{KU_C}(DS) \;\overset{?}{=}\; H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI))
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Order of Hash Concatenation:** SET specifies the order as **$H(PI) \Vert H(OI)$** (PI hash first, OI hash second). Reversing the order invalidates the verification. Examiners deduct 1 mark for an unspecified or reversed order. State it explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **four types of firewalls** with neat diagrams. Compare their capabilities in a table.

**(b) [7 marks]** What are the **design principles of a firewall**? Describe the **Screened Subnet (DMZ) architecture** and explain why a DMZ is preferred over a single-firewall design for e-commerce sites.

---

### Model Solution B(a) — Four Types of Firewalls (7 marks)

**1. Packet-Filtering Router (1.5 marks):**
- Operates at **OSI Layer 3/4** (Network/Transport).
- Examines each packet's header fields: source IP, destination IP, source port, destination port, protocol (TCP/UDP/ICMP), and TCP flags (SYN, ACK, FIN).
- Applies a pre-defined **rule table** in first-match order. Example: `deny any 192.168.0.0/16 to any 10.0.0.0/8`.
- **Pros:** Very fast (hardware-implementable), low cost, transparent to users. **Cons:** No state tracking (vulnerable to ACK scans and fragmented-packet evasion), no payload inspection, no user authentication. Susceptible to **IP spoofing** since source IP is trivially forgeable.

**2. Stateful Inspection Firewall (1.5 marks):**
- Operates at **Layer 3/4** but maintains a **state table** of every active TCP connection (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK, FIN, RST transitions tracked).
- Incoming packets are admitted only if they belong to an established outbound connection in the state table — defeats most blind port scans and ACK-based reconnaissance.
- Can apply per-flow rules (e.g., rate-limit FTP control channel).
- **Pros:** Stronger than static packet filter, near-real-time. **Cons:** Resource-intensive for high-throughput links, still cannot inspect application payload (cannot block a SQL-injection embedded in HTTP GET body).

**3. Application-Level Gateway (Proxy) (1.5 marks):**
- Operates at **Layer 7 (Application)**. It terminates the client's TCP connection, inspects the *entire* application-layer payload, then opens a *separate* connection to the destination.
- Examples: HTTP proxy (Squid), SMTP relay, FTP gateway.
- **Pros:** Deep payload inspection (block viruses, SQL-injection, file-type filtering, URL blacklisting), per-user authentication via LDAP/RADIUS, granular logging.
- **Cons:** Application-specific (one proxy per protocol), high latency, breaks protocols that embed IP info (e.g., FTP PORT command), single point of failure.

**4. Circuit-Level Gateway (1.5 marks):**
- Operates at **OSI Layer 5 (Session)**. Verifies the legitimacy of the TCP handshake (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK) before relaying.
- Does **not** inspect the payload — it acts as a TCP relay that hides internal IP addresses from the outside.
- Example: **SOCKS5 proxy** is the most common implementation.
- **Pros:** Simpler than an app gateway, hides internal topology.
- **Cons:** No content inspection, no application-level policy enforcement.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**
| Capability | Packet Filter | Stateful | App Gateway | Circuit GW |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| State tracking | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Payload inspection | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Performance | High | High | Low | Medium |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Confusing Circuit and Application Gateways:** Many students use the terms interchangeably. The distinction: **Application gateway inspects payload (Layer 7); Circuit gateway only checks session handshake (Layer 5)**. Mixing them up costs ~2 marks in a 7-mark sub-part.

---

### Model Solution B(b) — Design Principles & DMZ Architecture (7 marks)

**Six Design Principles of a Firewall (3 marks):**
1. **Least Privilege:** Default-deny; only the minimum required traffic is permitted.
2. **Defense in Depth:** No single point of failure. Firewall + IDS + antivirus + authentication + logging.
3. **Choke Point:** All cross-boundary traffic *must* traverse the firewall. Physical and logical topology must enforce this.
4. **Fail-Safe Stance (Fail-Closed):** On system failure, the firewall should *block* traffic, not pass it through. Circuit breakers for power, watchdog timers.
5. **Universal Participation:** All hosts in the protected network must be behind the firewall — a single backdoor bypass negates it.
6. **Diversity of Defense:** Use different vendors and mechanisms at each layer (e.g., Check Point firewall + Snort IDS + Cisco router ACLs) to defeat a single-vulnerability monoculture.

**Screened Subnet (DMZ) Architecture (3 marks):**

The DMZ (DeMilitarized Zone) is a perimeter network segment that sits *between* two firewalls:
- **External Firewall (Perimeter Router):** A packet filter facing the Internet — coarse-grained filtering, anti-spoofing, rate-limiting.
- **Bastion Host / Application Proxy in DMZ:** Hosts public-facing services (web, mail, DNS) hardened to bastion standards. The application proxy terminates external connections, inspects payloads, and opens new connections to the internal network.
- **Internal Firewall (Stateful Inspection):** Tightly filters traffic between the DMZ and the internal trusted LAN. Allows *only* the specific DMZ server IPs to talk to specific internal services (e.g., web server → DB server on port 3306 only).
- **Internal LAN:** Protected users, databases, business servers — isolated from direct Internet access.

**Why DMZ is preferred over single-firewall design (1 mark):**
- **Breach containment:** If an attacker compromises a public web server, they are *trapped* in the DMZ. The internal firewall still protects the LAN. A single-firewall design would place the web server on the same trust level as the LAN or require a single firewall to police two security zones, increasing risk.
- **Layered inspection:** External firewall does fast IP/port filtering; internal firewall does deep stateful filtering. Combined coverage is far stronger than either alone.
- **Defense in depth:** Aligns directly with Principle 2.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Forgetting the Bastion Host:** A common error is to draw a DMZ with two firewalls but no bastion host. The bastion is the **hardened, audited, dedicated** server in the DMZ that runs the firewall's exposed services. It is *the* defining component of a DMZ. Omitting it costs 1–1.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Payment Processing & Firewall Characteristics)**

### Payment Processing (SET)
- ☐ SET = **S**ecure **E**lectronic **T**ransaction; developed by **Visa + Mastercard (1996)**.
- ☐ Six participants: **Cardholder, Merchant, Issuer, Acquirer, Payment Gateway, CA**.
- ☐ **Issuer = Cardholder's bank; Acquirer = Merchant's bank** (do not confuse).
- ☐ **Dual Signature formula:** $DS = E_{KR_C}\big(H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI))\big)$.
- ☐ Order of concatenation: **H(PI) THEN H(OI)** — protocol-fixed.
- ☐ Merchant receives $H(PI)$ (PIMAC) but **never sees the card** — confidentiality.
- ☐ Acquirer receives $H(OI)$ (OIMAC) but **never sees the order** — confidentiality.
- ☐ DS is verified with the cardholder's **public key** $KU_C$.
- ☐ Three flows: **Purchase Request → Payment Authorization → Payment Capture**.
- ☐ SET never replaced SSL; it was effectively replaced by **3-D Secure (3DS)**.
- ☐ Hash function: SHA-1 (legacy SET v1); modern banking → SHA-256.

### Firewall Characteristics
- ☐ Three mandatory properties: **choke point, policy enforcement, immune to penetration**.
- ☐ Six design principles: **Least Privilege, Defense in Depth, Choke Point, Fail-Safe, Universal Participation, Diversity of Defense**.
- ☐ **Bastion Host** = hardened, dedicated, audited gateway host.
- ☐ Four types (in increasing capability/cost):
  1. **Packet-Filtering Router** — L3/L4, header-only, no state.
  2. **Stateful Inspection** — L3/L4 + connection state table.
  3. **Application-Level Gateway (Proxy)** — L7, full payload inspection, one proxy per protocol.
  4. **Circuit-Level Gateway** — L5, TCP handshake only (e.g., SOCKS5).
- ☐ Default firewall stance = **Fail-Safe (Fail-Closed)**.
- ☐ **DMZ (Screened Subnet)** uses **two firewalls** sandwiching a bastion segment.
- ☐ **Single-firewall** topology is suitable only for very small networks; DMZ is preferred for e-commerce.
- ☐ **Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)** = stateful + DPI + IDS/IPS + application awareness.
- ☐ Firewall cannot stop **insider attacks, encrypted malware tunnels, or backdoor physical access** — these are inherent limitations.

### High-Yield Mnemonics
- **SET participants:** *"**C**ustomers **M**ake **I**ncredible **A**ttempts **P**aying via **C**ards"* → C, M, I, A, P, C.
- **Six design principles:** *"**L**azy **D**octors **C**annot **F**ail **U**niversally **D**iagnosing"* → Least, Defense, Choke, Fail-Safe, Universal, Diversity.
- **Four firewall types:** *"**P**lease **S**tate **A**ll **C**ircuits"* → Packet, Stateful, Application, Circuit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Firewalls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Firewalls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Firewall?

A **Firewall** is a network security device (hardware, software, or both) that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on an organization's previously established security policies. At its core, it acts as a **barrier** between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks (such as the Internet), enforcing a **perimeter defense** based on the principle of *least privilege* and *default-deny*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** A firewall is a security mechanism that enforces an access control policy between two or more network domains by inspecting packet headers, connection states, or application payloads, and selectively permitting or denying traffic flows.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, firewalls are studied as a **layered defense primitive** — not as a single product, but as a family of architectures classified by the **OSI Layer** at which they inspect traffic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bouncer at a Club

Imagine a high-end nightclub with the following setup:

- The **street (Internet)** is full of unknown, potentially dangerous people.
- The **club interior (your private LAN)** holds valuables (your laptop, database, files).
- The **doorway** is the firewall.

Now, the bouncer (firewall) can operate at different **levels of scrutiny**:

1. **Level 1 — ID Check Only (Packet Filter):** The bouncer glances at shoes, jacket color, and the number of people in the group. He doesn't ask *why* they are coming. He just checks the visible "labels" (source/destination IP, port). Fast, but easy to fool with fake IDs.

2. **Level 2 — Conversation Check (Stateful Inspection):** The bouncer remembers who is currently inside, who left and came back, and whether the group entering matches a group that went out. He tracks the "flow" of the conversation.

3. **Level 3 — Reading the Invitation Letter (Application Gateway / Proxy):** The bouncer opens the letter, verifies the *content* of the invitation (HTTP request, SQL query), and validates that the contents are legitimate. He does not let guests talk directly to the host — he relays the message.

4. **Level 4 — VIP List + Content Inspection (Next-Gen Firewall / WAF):** The bouncer also checks the watch-list, the body language, the type of shoes, runs a metal detector, and checks for weapons. He applies **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** along with **intrusion prevention logic**.

This analogy maps directly to the four major firewall architectures discussed below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** A single firewall type is **insufficient** for modern networks. The KTU examiner expects students to identify which OSI layer each firewall type operates at, and to justify its strengths/weaknesses accordingly.

## 1.3 Classification Anchors

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies firewalls along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Categories |
|---|---|
| **By Deployment Mode** | Network-based, Host-based, Hybrid |
| **By OSI Layer of Operation** | L3/L4 (Packet Filter), L5 (Circuit), L7 (Application) |
| **By State Awareness** | Stateless vs. Stateful |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always state the **OSI Layer(s)** when defining a firewall in the exam — this is the single most-missed marker in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1 — Packet Filtering Firewall (Stateless)

### Operational Logic
A stateless packet filter inspects each **individual IP packet in isolation**, with no memory of prior packets. Decisions are made strictly on header fields:

- Source IP address
- Destination IP address
- Source Port
- Destination Port
- Protocol type (TCP, UDP, ICMP)
- Flags (e.g., SYN, ACK)

### Why and How
- **Why it exists:** It is the **fastest** and **simplest** firewall. Implemented directly in router ACLs (Access Control Lists).
- **How it works:** A rule table is scanned top-to-bottom; the **first matching rule** decides the action (ACCEPT or DROP). If no rule matches, the **default policy** (usually DROP) is applied.

### Limitations
- **No context:** Cannot tell if a packet is part of a legitimate established connection or a forged one.
- **IP Spoofing vulnerability:** A packet with a forged internal source IP is accepted if the rule allows the source range.
- **Blind to payload:** A packet destined for port 80 carrying an SQL injection is happily forwarded.

## 2.2 Type 2 — Stateful Inspection Firewall (Stateful Packet Filter)

### Operational Logic
A stateful firewall maintains a **State Table (Connection Tracking Table)** that records every active TCP/UDP session. Each incoming packet is checked against this table:

- If the packet **belongs to an existing, legitimate connection** (tracked in the state table), it is allowed through without re-evaluating all ACLs.
- If the packet is **new** or **out-of-state** (e.g., a SYN arriving without a prior SYN sent from inside), the ACL rules are applied.

### Why and How
- **Why:** It defeats the stateless firewall's biggest weakness — blind trust in the source IP. By tracking TCP handshakes, it can reject **SYN-flood** packets and **session hijack** attempts.
- **How:** Uses a state transition diagram (CLOSED → SYN_SENT → ESTABLISHED → FIN_WAIT → CLOSED) similar to the TCP FSM.

### Trade-off
Slower than stateless filtering due to table lookups and updates, but exponentially more secure.

## 2.3 Type 3 — Application-Level Gateway (Application Proxy Firewall)

### Operational Logic
An **Application-Level Gateway (ALG)**, also called a **Proxy Firewall**, terminates the client connection and originates a *new* connection to the destination server on behalf of the client. It operates at **OSI Layer 7 (Application Layer)** and understands the protocol semantics (HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP).

### Why and How
- **Why:** It can inspect the **payload**, validate commands (e.g., block `DELETE` in an HTTP request), and prevent direct end-to-end sessions that an attacker could exploit.
- **How:** Two TCP sessions — one from client → proxy, another from proxy → server. The proxy has full visibility of the application data.

### Limitation
- **Performance bottleneck:** Each connection must be fully proxied, consuming CPU and memory.
- **Protocol-specific:** Requires a dedicated proxy per application (e.g., an HTTP proxy cannot proxy FTP).

## 2.4 Type 4 — Circuit-Level Gateway

### Operational Logic
A circuit-level gateway operates at **OSI Layer 5 (Session Layer)**. It does **not** inspect packet contents; instead, it validates the **TCP handshake** to ensure the session is legitimate. Once a session is approved, packets flow through with minimal inspection.

### Why and How
- **Why it exists:** Provides session validation with **very low overhead** — useful as a fast front-line check before a slower application proxy.
- **How:** Typically implemented as a **SOCKS proxy**. It checks the three-way handshake and assigns a socket, then tunnels packets.

### Trade-off
Insecure if used alone — it does not examine the application payload and can relay malicious content.

## 2.5 Type 5 — Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)

### Operational Logic
An NGFW combines **stateful inspection**, **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)**, **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)**, **application awareness**, and **user-identity awareness** into a single unified platform. It is defined by Gartner (and accepted by KTU) as a firewall that goes beyond port/protocol inspection to include:

- Application-layer filtering
- User identity integration (LDAP/Active Directory)
- SSL/TLS inspection
- Sandboxing and threat intelligence feeds

## 2.6 Type 6 — Web Application Firewall (WAF)

### Operational Logic
A WAF is a specialized firewall (Layer 7) that protects **web servers** from application-layer attacks such as **SQL Injection, Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), and CSRF**. It inspects HTTP/HTTPS requests and applies rules like OWASP Top 10 mitigation.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Stateless | Stateful | Application Proxy | Circuit-Level | NGFW | WAF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | L3 / L4 | L3 / L4 | L7 | L5 | L3–L7 | L7 |
| **State Tracking** | None | Full session table | Per-protocol state | TCP handshake only | Full + App context | HTTP/S context |
| **Payload Inspection** | No | No | Yes | No | Yes (DPI) | Yes (HTTP semantics) |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Fast | Slowest | Fast | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Spoofing Defense** | Poor | Strong | Strong | Moderate | Very Strong | Strong |
| **Memory Cost** | Low | Moderate–High | High | Low | Very High | High |
| **Protocol Specific** | No | No | Yes | No | Partial | HTTP/HTTPS only |
| **Common Tech** | iptables, ACL | pfSense, Check Point | Squid, HAProxy | SOCKS | Palo Alto, Fortinet | ModSecurity, AWS WAF |

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Formula (Conceptual):** A packet $P$ is allowed through a firewall $F$ if and only if $P$ satisfies the rule set $R_F$ given the current state $S$ of the connection. Formally:
>
> $$\text{Action}(P) = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT} & \text{if } \exists r \in R_F : r \text{ matches } P \land \text{State}(P) \in S_{\text{allowed}} \\ \text{DROP} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packet filters** are embedded in every enterprise router (Cisco ACLs, Linux `iptables`).
- **Stateful firewalls** form the perimeter of most corporate networks (pfSense, Cisco ASA).
- **Application proxies** are used in **reverse-proxy** configurations (NGINX, HAProxy) to protect web servers and provide caching/load balancing.
- **WAFs** are a regulatory compliance requirement under **PCI-DSS 6.6** for any e-commerce platform.
- **NGFWs** are the **de facto enterprise standard** in 2024 — Gartner reports > 70% market penetration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Rule Processing in a Packet Filter

Consider a stateless packet filter with the following ACL (top-to-bottom evaluation):

| Rule # | Source IP | Dest IP | Dest Port | Protocol | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10.0.0.0/8 | 192.168.1.0/24 | 80 | TCP | ACCEPT |
| 2 | 10.0.0.0/8 | 192.168.1.0/24 | 22 | TCP | ACCEPT |
| 3 | Any | Any | Any | Any | DROP |

### Step-by-Step Evaluation

**Scenario A:** Packet with Source = 10.0.0.5, Dest = 192.168.1.10, Dest Port = 80, Protocol = TCP.

Step 1: Check Rule 1.

- Source 10.0.0.5 ∈ 10.0.0.0/8 ✓
- Dest 192.168.1.10 ∈ 192.168.1.0/24 ✓
- Dest Port 80 matches ✓
- Protocol TCP matches ✓
- **All fields match → Action = ACCEPT.**

Step 2: Rule processing halts at the first match. Action = **ACCEPT**.

**Scenario B:** Packet with Source = 10.0.0.5, Dest = 192.168.1.10, Dest Port = 23 (Telnet), Protocol = TCP.

Step 1: Check Rule 1. Port 23 ≠ 80 → No match.
Step 2: Check Rule 2. Port 23 ≠ 22 → No match.
Step 3: Check Rule 3. **Default DROP** applies. Action = **DROP**.

> [!NOTE]
> This illustrates a key **engineering property** of ACLs: *order matters*. The first matching rule wins. A misordered ACL is a common KTU-exam trap.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Stateful Inspection Trace

A TCP three-way handshake from internal host 10.0.0.5 to external 203.0.113.10 port 80:

Step 1: Host sends **SYN** (Seq=x). Firewall sees new outgoing connection, creates state entry:
$$\text{State Table: } \langle 10.0.0.5, 203.0.113.10, 80, \text{SYN\_SENT} \rangle$$
