When would you prefer one over the other?** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Comprehensive Comparison Matrix:** | Parameter | IPsec | SSL/TLS | |---|---|---| | OSI Layer | Layer 3 (Network) | Layer 4–7 (Transport/Application) | | Encryption scope | Entire IP packet (Tunnel Mode) | Application payload only | | Authentication | Certificates, PSK, EAP | X.509 certificates mainly | | Key exchange | IKEv2 / DH / ECDH | ECDHE in TLS 1.3 | | NAT traversal | Requires NAT-T (UDP 4500) | NAT-friendly (TCP 443) | | Firewall traversal | Often problematic (ESP protocol 51) | Easy (HTTPS port 443) | | Configuration complexity | High (IKE policies, SAs, SPD) | Low (browser-managed) | | User mobility support | Limited | Excellent | | End-to-end coverage | Host-to-host or gateway-to-gateway | Client-to-server | | Typical use | Site-to-site VPN, network segmentation | Web security, remote access | **When to Prefer IPsec:** - Site-to-site connectivity between branch offices and HQ. - Securing all IP traffic, not just web. - Environments with mature PKI and dedicated VPN gateways. - High-throughput bulk data protection with hardware acceleration. **When to Prefer SSL/TLS:** - Remote access for individual users behind NAT. - Web applications, SaaS, cloud APIs. - BYOD environments where installing VPN clients is impractical. - When firewall friendliness and zero client configuration are critical. **Modern Trend:** The industry is shifting toward **ZTNA (Zero-Trust Network Access)** which uses mutual TLS (mTLS) over encrypted tunnels, blurring the line between IPsec and SSL/TLS deployments. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. Do **NOT** claim SSL/TLS provides "end-to-end encryption" unconditionally — it is only as end-to-end as the certificate trust chain allows. > 2. Do **NOT** state that IPsec is "obsolete" — it remains the gold standard for site-to-site enterprise VPNs in 2025. > 3. Do **NOT** confuse SSL with TLS — SSL 3.0 has been deprecated since 2015; always specify TLS 1.2 or 1.3. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — General Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Module 4 Encrypted Tunnel Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing AH and ESP scope:** Students often claim ESP authenticates the outer IP header. **ESP authenticates from the ESP header onward, NOT the outer IP header.** AH is the protocol that authenticates the outer IP header. > > 2. **Forgetting SA directionality:** Writing "an SA protects the connection" instead of "an SA protects traffic in one direction". A bidirectional IPsec tunnel requires **two SAs**. > > 3. **Omitting Anti-Replay Mechanism:** ESP/AH anti-replay via sequence numbers and sliding windows is a **mandatory topic** in KTU Module 4. Skipping it costs 2–3 marks. > > 4. **Wrong ICV computation:** The ICV is computed **before** encryption in some textbooks and **after** in others. In IPsec ESP, the ICV is computed over the **ciphertext + ESP header** (encrypt-then-MAC) — students writing encrypt-after-MAC lose marks. > > 5. **Ignoring ESP Trailer:** The ESP trailer (Pad Length + Next Header + Padding) is **part of the encrypted region**, not plaintext. This is a classic trap question. > > 6. **Missing NAT-T mention:** For any modern IPsec deployment, NAT-Traversal (encapsulating ESP inside UDP port 4500) is essential. Failing to mention it indicates outdated knowledge. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Encrypted Tunnels (Module 4):** - ✅ An **encrypted tunnel** = secure logical channel + outer IP header encapsulation + cryptographic protection of inner packet. - ✅ **Three tunnel families:** Network-layer (IPsec), Data-link-layer (L2TP), Application-layer (SSL/TLS, SSH). - ✅ **IPsec core protocols:** AH (integrity only, IP protocol 50) and ESP (confidentiality + integrity, IP protocol 51). - ✅ **Two modes:** Transport Mode (host-to-host, no new IP header) and Tunnel Mode (gateway-to-gateway, new outer IP header). - ✅ **Security Association (SA)** is **unidirectional**, identified by $\langle SPI, DestIP, Protocol \rangle$ triple. Two SAs needed for bidirectional communication. - ✅ **Three databases per gateway:** SAD (active SAs), SPD (policies), PAD (peer credentials). - ✅ **IKEv2 has 4 exchanges:** IKE_SA_INIT, IKE_AUTH, CREATE_CHILD_SA, INFORMATIONAL. TLS 1.2 needed 4–6 messages; IKEv2 needs only 4. - ✅ **SKEYSEED** = $PRF(N_i \Vert N_r,\; g^{ir})$ — derived from DH shared secret. - ✅ **KEYMAT** = $PRF^+(SK\_d,\; N_i \Vert N_r)$ — used to derive Child SA encryption and integrity keys. - ✅ **ESP packet structure order:** SPI → SeqNum → Encrypted Payload → Padding → PadLen → NextHeader → ICV. - ✅ **ICV computation** is over `[ESP Header ∥ Ciphertext ∥ ESP Trailer]` (encrypt-then-MAC pattern). - ✅ **Anti-replay** uses sequence numbers + receiver sliding window of size **W = 32 or 64**. - ✅ **TLS 1.3** uses mandatory **ECDHE** for forward secrecy; static RSA key exchange is **removed**. - ✅ **TLS 1.3 handshake** = 1-RTT (vs 2-RTT in TLS 1.2); 0-RTT supported for resumption. - ✅ **NAT-Traversal** wraps ESP in **UDP port 4500** to traverse NAT devices. - ✅ **MTU consideration:** ESP Tunnel Mode adds ~50–70 bytes; inner packets should be ≤ 1432 bytes for Ethernet. - ✅ **IPsec vs TLS preference:** IPsec for site-to-site, TLS for remote-access / web / cloud. - ✅ **Real-world deployments:** Cisco ASA, Palo Alto GlobalProtect, OpenVPN, WireGuard, AWS Site-to-Site VPN — citing at least one elevates your answer. - ✅ **PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy):** Compromise of long-term keys cannot decrypt past sessions — guaranteed by ephemeral DH/ECDH. - ✅ **HMAC-SHA-256 truncated to 128 bits** is the most common ESP ICV in modern deployments. - ✅ **AES-GCM** is now preferred over **AES-CBC** because it provides authenticated encryption (AEAD) in a single primitive. - ✅ **Anti-replay window formula:** $W_{low} \leq SeqNum_{incoming} \leq W_{high}$, else DROP. > [!NOTE] > **Final Examiner Note:** Module 4 of PEADT863 is heavily weighted toward **IPsec architecture and ESP/AH packet construction**. Approximately **60% of Module 4 marks** come from correctly drawing packet formats and explaining the IKEv2 key derivation. Practice drawing ESP packets in both Transport and Tunnel Mode at least 10 times before the exam for top-band performance.

topics.title: Trusted Systems: Data Access Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trusted Systems: Data Access Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Trusted Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trusted System (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *Trusted System* is a computer system in which a specific set of internal controls (hardware, software, firmware, and procedural mechanisms) can be **assured** to enforce a given set of security policies, in such a way that the system can be relied upon to protect sensitive data from unauthorized disclosure, modification, or destruction. The degree of trust is established through rigorous verification, formal specification, and evaluation criteria (e.g., **Trusted Computer System Evaluation Criteria — TCSEC / Orange Book**).

A *trusted* system is not the same as a *secure* system:
- A **secure** system is one that is *claimed* to be safe.
- A **trusted** system is one that has been *verified* (often via formal proofs, certification, or evaluation) to behave as specified — even under attack.

### 1.2 Data Access Control — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Access Control (DAC Subsystem of a Trusted System):**
> Data Access Control is the *selective restriction of access to data*. It defines **WHO** (subject — user, process) can do **WHAT** (action — read/write/execute/append) on **WHICH** object (file, record, memory segment, resource) and **WHEN** (time, session, context), enforced by a **Reference Monitor** that mediates every access attempt.

The classic KTU formulation is the **triple `(S, O, A)`** — Subjects, Objects, and Actions — governed by an access policy **P ⊆ S × O × A**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Corporate Vault Building

Imagine a **Fortress-Corporate-Office** (a Trusted System):

| Real-World Element | Trusted System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| 🏢 The building with restricted floors | The operating system / database |
| 🛂 The single security gate (one main entrance) | The **Reference Monitor** |
| 🪪 The photo-ID badge of every employee | The *Subject identity* (UID, role, clearance) |
| 🚪 Locked office doors | *Objects* (files, records) |
| 🔑 Key card permission list on each door | **Access Control List (ACL)** |
| 🪪 Permission list stamped on the badge | **Capability List** |
| 📋 A "need-to-know" classification stamped on every document | **Security Label / Classification Level** |
| 🚨 The audit log at the gate | **Audit Trail / Security Kernel Logs** |
| 🏗️ The walls, locks, doors — the entire physical security apparatus | The **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** |

A new intern walks in. The guard checks the badge → looks up access list for the requested floor → grants or denies. **Every** access goes through this single, tamper-proof gate. That gate is your **Reference Monitor** — the heart of data access control.

> [!NOTE]
> **TCSEC Key Insight:** The Orange Book (DoD 5200.28-STD) requires that a trusted system satisfy three absolute properties: **(1) Complete Mediation**, **(2) Tamper-Proofness**, and **(3) Verifiability** of the reference monitor.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Access Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authentication** → "Who are you?" (Identity verification via password, biometrics, tokens)
> **Authorization** → "What are you allowed to do?" (Permission check via access policy)
> **Accountability** → "What did you do?" (Audit logging & non-repudiation)

All three must coexist in a *trusted* system.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU 2024 Highlights)

- **Security Level (L):** A pair `(λ, C)` where `λ` is the *classification level* and `C` is the *category set* (compartments).
- **Clearance:** The maximum security level a subject is trusted to access.
- **Classification:** The security level assigned to an object.
- **Dominance Relation:** `L1 ≥ L2` iff `λ1 ≥ λ2` AND `C1 ⊇ C2`.
- **Bell-LaPadula Confidentiality Lattice (BSL):** Partial order `(SC, ≤)` over security levels.
- **Reference Monitor:** `3` mandatory properties — *always invoked, tamper-proof, small enough to verify*.
- **TCSEC Trust Classes:** A1 (highest) → B3 → B2 → B1 → C2 → C1 → D (lowest).

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Access Control Matrix (ACM) — Lampson's Foundation

The **Access Control Matrix** (Butler Lampson, 1971) is the canonical mathematical model. It is a sparse two-dimensional table where rows = subjects, columns = objects, and the cell `(s, o)` contains the set of allowed actions `A(s, o) ⊆ {read, write, execute, append, delete, own}`.

**Structure of the ACM:**

- **Row of subject s_i** → the *capability list* of `s_i` (what `s_i` can do on every object).
- **Column of object o_j** → the *Access Control List (ACL)* of `o_j` (who can do what to `o_j`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Point:** The ACM is conceptually complete but **physically inefficient** for large systems. Real operating systems implement either the **row view (capabilities)** or the **column view (ACLs)**, never the full matrix.

### 2.2 Three Primary Access Control Policies

#### 2.2.1 Discretionary Access Control (DAC)
- The **owner** of an object decides who can access it (`chmod` / `chown` in UNIX).
- Subject to *Trojan Horse attacks* and *confused-deputy problems*.
- Used in civilian / commercial systems (Windows NT, UNIX).

#### 2.2.2 Mandatory Access Control (MAC)
- A **central security policy** (system-wide) overrides any owner's discretion.
- Based on **security labels** and a **lattice of classifications**.
- Implemented via **Bell-LaPadula (confidentiality)** and **Biba (integrity)** models.
- Used in military / government systems (SELinux, Trusted Solaris).

#### 2.2.3 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
- Permissions are assigned to **roles** (e.g., Doctor, Cashier, Admin), and users are assigned to roles.
- Follows the **principle of least privilege** and **separation of duties**.
- Standardized as **ANSI INCITS 359**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Separation of Duty (RSD):** A subject must not hold enough roles to unilaterally complete a sensitive task. Enforced via *static* (role definition) and *dynamic* (session) constraints.

### 2.3 The Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model — Confidentiality Lattice

Developed by **David Bell and Leonard LaPadula (1973)** for the U.S. DoD as a formal confidentiality model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bell-LaPadula Two Rules (memorize verbatim for KTU):**
> 1. **No Read Up (Simple Security Property / ss-property):** A subject at level `L_s` may **only read** objects at level `L_o` if `L_o ≤ L_s`. (Reading higher = "leaking" a secret.)
> 2. **No Write Down (*-property / Star Property):** A subject at level `L_s` may **only write** to objects at level `L_o` if `L_o ≥ L_s`. (Writing lower = "dumping" a secret to a less-trusted level.)

**Tranquility:**
- **Strong Tranquility:** Security labels of subjects/objects **never change** while the system runs.
- **Weak Tranquility:** Labels may change, but never in a way that violates an established security property.

### 2.4 The Biba Model — Integrity Lattice

> [!NOTE]
> **Biba is the *integrity* mirror of Bell-LaPadula.** It uses the same lattice but with opposite rules.

- **No Read Down (Simple Integrity):** A subject at integrity level `I_s` may only read objects at `I_o ≤ I_s` (avoid being corrupted by low-integrity data).
- **No Write Up (*-property):** A subject may only write to objects at `I_o ≥ I_s` (do not pollute higher-integrity data).

### 2.5 Reference Monitor & Trusted Computing Base (TCB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Monitor (RM) — the *mediator*:**
> An abstract machine that intercepts **every** access of a subject to an object, validates it against the access policy, and either permits or denies. Mandatory properties: **(1) Complete mediation**, **(2) Tamper-proof**, **(3) Verifiable (small enough to analyze).**

> **Trusted Computing Base (TCB) — the *enforcement apparatus*:**
> The totality of protection mechanisms (hardware + software + firmware) responsible for enforcing the security policy. TCB components include: the **Security Kernel**, the **Reference Monitor**, the **Trusted Computing Software**, the **Audit Subsystem**, and **Authentication Mechanisms.**

### 2.6 The Take-Grant Protection Model

A graph-theoretic model where:
- **Vertices:** Subjects (S) and Objects (O).
- **Directed edges:** Rights — `take`, `grant`, `read`, `write`, `create`, `remove`.
- **tg-connectivity:** Two subjects are connected if rights can be transferred between them.

It is used to formally answer: *"Can subject A obtain right R on object B?"* via **closure rules**.

### 2.7 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Definition / Meaning | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| `S` | Set of subjects | Finite set of users/processes |
| `O` | Set of objects | Finite set of resources |
| `A` | Set of actions / access modes | `{r, w, x, a, d}` |
| `M[s, o]` | Access matrix entry | `⊆ A` |
| `L = (λ, C)` | Security level — classification λ, category-set C | `λ ∈ {Unclass, Conf, Secret, TS}` |
| `L1 ≤ L2` | Dominance: λ1 ≤ λ2 ∧ C1 ⊆ C2 | Boolean predicate |
| `ss-property` | No Read Up: `λ_o ≤ λ_s` | BLP |
| `*-property` | No Write Down: `λ_o ≥ λ_s` | BLP |
| `secure_state` | State where all rules + tranquility hold | Boolean |
| `BSC` | Basic Security Theorem: initial state secure + every transition preserves security ⇒ system secure | Theorem |
| `TCSEC class` | Trust rating: `A1 \vert B3 \vert B2 \vert B1 \vert C2 \vert C1 \vert D` | Ordinal scale |
| `CC EAL` | Common Criteria Evaluation Assurance Level: `EAL1 \vert EAL2 \vert ... \vert EAL7` | Ordinal scale |
| `R(s, o)` | Read access: `R ∈ M[s, o]` | Boolean |
| `W(s, o)` | Write access: `W ∈ M[s, o]` | Boolean |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Systems (Oracle Label Security, Microsoft SQL Row-Level Security):** MAC via security labels.
- **Cloud (AWS IAM, Azure RBAC):** RBAC with fine-grained policies.
- **Operating Systems (SELinux, AppArmor, Trusted Solaris):** MAC enforcement via LSM (Linux Security Modules).
- **Government & Defense:** Common Criteria certification is mandatory (EAL4+).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bell-LaPadula "No Write Down" Leads to the Tranquility Principle

**Given:**
- A system with subjects S, objects O.
- Each subject has a *current security level* `λ_s(t)`.
- Each object has a *fixed security level* `λ_o`.
- A *state* `v = (b, M, λ)` where `b` = current access set, `M` = access matrix, `λ` = level function.

**Step 1: Define the Simple Security Condition (ss-condition).**

For a state `v = (b, M, λ)`, the ss-condition holds iff, for every access `(s, o, r) ∈ b`:

$$\lambda_o \le \lambda_s$$

*Logic:* Reading is permitted only when the object's classification is dominated by (i.e., is at or below) the subject's clearance.

**Step 2: Define the *-property (Star Property).**

For a state `v = (b, M, λ)`, the *-property holds iff, for every write access `(s, o, w) ∈ b`:

$$\lambda_o \ge \lambda_s$$

*Logic:* Writing is permitted only when the object's classification dominates the subject's current level — preventing a subject at Secret level from writing into a Confidential file (which could be read by Confidential-cleared subjects, leaking the Secret data).

**Step 3: Combine the two properties — "secure state" definition.**

A state `v = (b, M, λ)` is *secure* iff the ss-condition AND the *-property both hold simultaneously:

$$secure(v) \iff \left(\forall (s, o, r) \in b : \lambda_o \le \lambda_s \right) \;\land\; \left(\forall (s, o, w) \in b : \lambda_o \ge \lambda_s \right)$$

**Step 4: State Transition Rule — Basic Security Theorem.**

A *transition* `T` from state `v` to state `v*` is *security-preserving* iff:

$$secure(v) \;\land\; T \text{ is a valid BLP rule} \;\Rightarrow\; secure(v*)$$

The **Basic Security Theorem (BSC)** states:

$$secure(v_0) \;\land\; \forall i \ge 0 : T_i \text{ preserves security} \;\Longrightarrow\; \forall i \ge 0 : secure(v_i)$$

*Intuition:* If the system *starts* in a secure state and *every* legal state transition is security-preserving, the system remains secure forever. This is the formal backbone of BLP.

**Step 5: Tranquility — Why subject levels must not be downgraded dynamically.**

Consider subject `s` at level Top Secret, writing into an object `o` at level Unclassified. The *-property forbids this. But if `s` could dynamically lower its level to Unclassified *just before* the write, the *-property is technically satisfied, yet the data was known at Top Secret.

**Conclusion:** Without tranquility, the *-property is *trivially* subvertible. Hence BLP mandates **tranquility**:

$$\forall s, \forall t_1, t_2 : \lambda_s(t_1) = \lambda_s(t_2) \quad \text{(strong tranquility)}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Properties of BLP and Biba

| Property | Bell-LaPadula (Confidentiality) | Biba (Integrity) |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Prevent unauthorized **disclosure** | Prevent unauthorized **modification** |
| **Lattice Order** | `≤` (low → high) | `≤` (low → high integrity) |
| **Read rule (ss)** | `λ_o ≤ λ_s` (No Read Up) | `λ_s ≤ λ_o` (No Read Down) |
| **Write rule (*)** | `λ_o ≥ λ_s` (No Write Down) | `λ_s ≥ λ_o` (No Write Up) |
| **Strict version** | Strict BLP (no read-up, no write-down) | Strict Biba (no read-down, no write-up) |
| **Used in** | Military, classified systems | Financial, safety-critical systems |
| **Mandatory?** | Yes (MAC) | Yes (MAC) |
| **Trojan-horse mitigation** | Partial (via *-property) | No (Biba does not address disclosure) |

### 3.3 Derivation: RBAC Role-Conflict Detection

**Given:**
- `U` = set of users, `R` = set of roles, `P` = set of permissions.
- `UA ⊆ U × R` (user-role assignment).
- `PA ⊆ P × R` (permission-role assignment).
- `SSD ⊆ R × R` (Static Separation of Duty — mutually exclusive role pairs).

**Objective:** Detect whether a user `u` violates SSD by holding two mutually-exclusive roles.

**Step 1:** Compute the set of roles assigned to user `u`:

$$roles(u) = \left\{ r \in R \;:\; (u, r) \in UA \right\}$$

**Step 2:** Detect a violation:

$$violates\_SSD(u) = \exists (r_1, r_2) \in SSD : r_1 \in roles(u) \;\land\; r_2 \in roles(u)$$

**Step 3:** Enforce via constraint on UA construction:

$$\forall u \in U, \forall (r_1, r_2) \in SSD : (u, r_1) \in UA \;\land\; (u, r_2) \in UA \;\Rightarrow\; \bot \;(\text{reject})$$

*Logic:* No user may be assigned a role pair declared as statically separated. This is the formal statement of **Static Separation of Duty (SSD)** in RBAC.

**Step 4: Dynamic Separation of Duty (DSD)** — A user may *separately* hold two conflicting roles, but cannot *simultaneously activate* both in a single session:

$$\forall s \in sessions(u), \forall (r_1, r_2) \in DSD : r_1 \in active(s) \;\land\; r_2 \in active(s) \;\Rightarrow\; \bot$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem:** A system has classification levels `Unclass < Conf < Secret < TopSecret`. A subject `Alice` is cleared at `Secret` and currently operates at `Secret`. Object `FileX` is at `TopSecret`. Object `FileY` is at `Conf`.
> **(i)** Can Alice read FileX? **(ii)** Can Alice read FileY? **(iii)** Can Alice write to FileY?

**Solution:**

**Step (i):** Reading requires `λ_o ≤ λ_s` (ss-property).

$$\lambda_{FileX} = \text{TopSecret}, \quad \lambda_{Alice} = \text{Secret}$$

$$\text{TopSecret} \le \text{Secret} \;\;? \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{FALSE}$$

∴ **No Read Up violated → Alice CANNOT read FileX.**

**Step (ii):** 

$$\lambda_{FileY} = \text{Conf}, \quad \lambda_{Alice} = \text{Secret}$$

$$\text{Conf} \le \text{Secret} \;\;? \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{TRUE}$$

∴ **Alice CAN read FileY.**

**Step (iii):** Writing requires `λ_o ≥ λ_s` (*-property).

$$\lambda_{FileY} = \text{Conf}, \quad \lambda_{Alice} = \text{Secret}$$

$$\text{Conf} \ge \text{Secret} \;\;? \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{FALSE}$$

∴ **No Write Down violated → Alice CANNOT write to FileY.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-intuitive insight:** Even though Alice is *cleared* to read Secret data, the *-property prevents her from *writing* that same data *down* to a lower classification. This blocks the **"write-down Trojan"** attack.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Reference Monitor Placement in the TCB Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app1["USER APPLICATION A"]
    app2["USER APPLICATION B"]
    app3["USER APPLICATION C"]
    rm["REFERENCE MONITOR"]
    secdb[("SECURITY POLICY DATABASE ACL CAPABILITIES LABELS")]
    aud[("AUDIT LOG SUBSYSTEM")]
    obj1["FILE OBJECT 1"]
    obj2["FILE OBJECT 2"]
    obj3["MEMORY SEGMENT"]
    obj4["DEVICE RESOURCE"]
    hw["HARDWARE TCB LAYER CPU MMU FIRMWARE"]

    app1 -- "ACCESS REQUEST S O A" --> rm
    app2 -- "ACCESS REQUEST S O A" --> rm
    app3 -- "ACCESS REQUEST S O A" --> rm
    rm -- "QUERY POLICY" --> secdb
    rm -- "LOG EVENT" --> aud
    rm -- "PERMIT OR DENY" --> app1
    rm -- "PERMIT OR DENY" --> app2
    rm -- "PERMIT OR DENY" --> app3
    rm -. "ENFORCES" .-> obj1
    rm -. "ENFORCES" .-> obj2
    rm -. "ENFORCES" .-> obj3
    rm -. "ENFORCES" .-> obj4
    hw --- rm
    hw --- secdb
    hw --- aud
```

*Interpretation:* Every subject's access request must traverse the **Reference Monitor (RM)**, which queries the **Security Policy Database** and logs every decision to the **Audit Subsystem**. The hardware TCB layer (CPU, MMU, firmware) provides the **tamper-proof** foundation. This is the **Complete Mediation** property of the Orange Book.

### 4.2 Diagram B — The Bell-LaPadula Information-Flow Lattice

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ts["TOP SECRET NUCLEAR WEAPONS"]
    s["SECRET CRYPTO KEYS"]
    c["CONFIDENTIAL PAYROLL DATA"]
    uc["UNCLASSIFIED PUBLIC NOTICE"]
    R1["RULE SS PROPERTY NO READ UP"]
    R2["RULE STAR PROPERTY NO WRITE DOWN"]
    U1["USER CLEARED TS"]
    U2["USER CLEARED SECRET"]
    U3["USER CLEARED CONFIDENTIAL"]
    U4["USER CLEARED UNCLASSIFIED"]

    U1 -- "MAY READ" --> ts
    U1 -- "MAY READ" --> s
    U1 -- "MAY READ" --> c
    U1 -- "MAY READ" --> uc
    U2 -- "MAY READ" --> s
    U2 -- "MAY READ" --> c
    U2 -- "MAY READ" --> uc
    U3 -- "MAY READ" --> c
    U3 -- "MAY READ" --> uc
    U4 -- "MAY READ" --> uc

    U1 -. "DENIED WRITE DOWN" .-> c
    U1 -. "DENIED WRITE DOWN" .-> uc
    U2 -. "DENIED WRITE DOWN" .-> uc
    R1 -.- U1
    R1 -.- U2
    R2 -.- U1
    R2 -.- U2
```

*Interpretation:* Arrows = allowed reads (downward + horizontal); dashed lines = denied writes (downward). A Secret-cleared user can read Confidential and Unclassified, but **cannot write to Unclassified** — preventing the leakage of a Secret into the public domain.

### 4.3 Diagram C — Trusted Computing Base (TCB) Layered Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1["LAYER 4 APPLICATION LAYER USER PROGRAMS UTILITIES"]
    layer2["LAYER 3 TRUSTED SOFTWARE OS SECURITY KERNEL DAC MAC RBAC ENGINES"]
    layer3["LAYER 2 SECURITY SUBSYSTEMS REFERENCE MONITOR AUDIT LOG AUTHENTICATION MODULE"]
    layer4["LAYER 1 HARDWARE ROOT OF TRUST CPU MMU TPM FIRMWARE"]
    layer5["LAYER 0 PHYSICAL SECURITY VAULT BIOMETRIC ACCESS PHYSICAL BARRIERS"]

    layer1 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer5
    layer4 --- layer3
    layer3 --- layer2
```

*Interpretation:* The TCB is a *nested onion*. A compromise at any upper layer may not bypass the integrity of the lower layers. The **Hardware Root of Trust** is the *smallest, most verifiable* element — the ultimate anchor of trust.
