## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a "Trusted System". List and explain the three mandatory properties of a Reference Monitor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Trusted System** is a computer system whose internal controls have been *verified* (formally or via certification) to enforce a specified security policy. Trust is a *demonstrated* property, not an *assumed* one.

The three mandatory properties of a **Reference Monitor** (Anderson's Report, 1972 — adopted in the Orange Book) are:

1. **Complete Mediation:** The Reference Monitor must intercept *every* access of a subject to an object — no path can bypass it.
2. **Tamper-Proofness:** The Reference Monitor and its database must be protected from unauthorized modification, even by subjects with administrative privilege.
3. **Verifiability:** The Reference Monitor must be small, modular, and simple enough to be subjected to *complete analysis and testing* (formal verification).

> *(1 mark definition + ½ mark each property + ½ mark for the explanation of tamper-proofness.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Compare DAC and MAC. Which is more suitable for military systems and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DAC (Discretionary) | MAC (Mandatory) |
|---|---|---|
| Policy authority | Owner of object | Central system policy |
| Flexibility | High | Low |
| Vulnerability | Trojan horse, confused deputy | Covert channels |
| Best for | Civilian / commercial | Military / government |

**MAC is more suitable for military systems** because the policy is *centrally enforced* and *cannot be overridden* by individual users, ensuring consistent enforcement of *confidentiality* and *integrity* across all subjects — essential for classified data.

> *(1 mark comparison + 1 mark military suitability + 1 mark for justification.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks] Explain the Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model in detail. State and justify its two fundamental security properties. What is the *Basic Security Theorem*?**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Bell-LaPadula (BLP) model, developed by David Bell and Leonard LaPadula in 1973, is a *formal state-transition model* of mandatory access control. It is the foundational model for **confidentiality** in trusted systems and was adopted in the U.S. Orange Book (TCSEC).

**Security Levels and Dominance (1 mark):**
- Each subject and object is assigned a *security level* `L = (λ, C)`, where `λ` is a classification (`Unclass < Conf < Secret < TS`) and `C` is a set of categories (compartments).
- **Dominance:** `L1 ≥ L2` iff `λ1 ≥ λ2` AND `C1 ⊇ C2`. The set of all security levels forms a *lattice*.

**The Two Properties (3 marks):**

1. **Simple Security Property (ss-property) — "No Read Up":**
   $$\forall (s, o, \text{read}) \in b : \lambda_o \le \lambda_s$$
   *Justification:* A subject may only read objects whose classification is dominated by its own clearance. This prevents a low-cleared subject from learning higher-classified data.

2. ***-Property (Star Property) — "No Write Down":**
   $$\forall (s, o, \text{write}) \in b : \lambda_o \ge \lambda_s$$
   *Justification:* A subject may only write to objects whose classification dominates its own current level. This prevents a high-cleared subject from placing classified data into a lower-classified object that could be read by less-cleared subjects (a *write-down leakage*).

**Tranquility (1 mark):**
Security labels must not be changed dynamically in a way that violates the above. *Strong tranquility* forbids any change; *weak tranquility* allows safe changes.

**Basic Security Theorem (1 mark):**
If a system starts in a *secure state* and every legal state transition is *security-preserving* (i.e., it leaves the system in a secure state), then the system is *secure for all time*. Formally:
$$secure(v_0) \;\land\; \forall i \ge 0 : T_i(secure(v_i)) \Rightarrow secure(v_{i+1}) \;\Rightarrow\; \forall i \ge 0 : secure(v_i)$$

---

**(b) [7 marks] With a neat diagram, describe the architecture of a Trusted Computing Base (TCB). Explain the role of the Reference Monitor within it.**

**Model Solution:**

**TCB Definition (1 mark):**
The **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** is the totality of hardware, software, and firmware that collectively enforce the security policy of a trusted system. A failure or compromise of any TCB element is a compromise of the entire system.

**Layered TCB Architecture (3 marks) — diagram required (use the diagram from Section 4.3 of these notes):**

- **Hardware Root of Trust (Layer 1):** CPU protection rings, Memory Management Unit (MMU), TPM chip, secure boot firmware. Provides tamper-proof primitives.
- **Security Subsystems (Layer 2):** Reference Monitor, Audit Log, Authentication module, crypto engines.
- **Trusted OS Software (Layer 3):** Security kernel, MAC/DAC/RBAC engines, file system permission enforcers.
- **Application Layer (Layer 4):** User programs that *trust* and *rely* on the lower layers.

**Role of the Reference Monitor (3 marks):**
The Reference Monitor is the **central mediation engine** of the TCB:

1. It is the *only path* through which a subject can access any object (Complete Mediation).
2. It is *isolated* from user-level tampering (Tamper-Proofness), typically residing in the OS kernel or a hardware-protected enclave.
3. It is *small and verifiable* — it is the most rigorously audited component (Verifiability).

**Conclusion:** The Reference Monitor is the *active enforcement core* of the TCB; the TCB is the *passive structural foundation*. Together they convert policy into practice.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks] Explain the Access Control Matrix model. Distinguish between Access Control Lists (ACLs) and Capability Lists, with examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Access Control Matrix — Definition (2 marks):**
Proposed by Butler Lampson (1971), the Access Control Matrix (ACM) is a 2D table where:
- Rows represent *subjects* (S).
- Columns represent *objects* (O).
- The cell `M[s, o]` contains the set of access rights `A ⊆ {read, write, execute, append, delete, own}` granted to subject `s` on object `o`.

The ACM is *conceptually complete* but rarely implemented as a physical table in large systems because it is sparse and memory-wasteful.

**Access Control Lists (ACLs) (2.5 marks):**
- An **ACL is the column view** of the ACM.
- It is a list stored *with each object* `o`, enumerating subjects and their rights: `ACL(o) = { (s, A) : A ⊆ M[s, o] }`.
- **Example (UNIX):** `rwx r-- --- owner=alice group=staff` — stored as the inode permission bits + ACL extension.
- **Advantage:** Easy to *revoke* access (modify the object's ACL).
- **Disadvantage:** Hard to *enumerate* all permissions of a specific subject (must scan every object's ACL).

**Capability Lists (2.5 marks):**
- A **capability list is the row view** of the ACM.
- It is a list stored *with each subject* `s`, enumerating objects and their rights: `Cap(s) = { (o, A) : A ⊆ M[s, o] }`.
- **Example (UNIX file descriptors):** A process's FD table — each FD is a capability pointing to a file with specific rights.
- **Advantage:** Efficient access check — subject's capability is *carried* with the request.
- **Disadvantage:** Revocation is hard — must invalidate the capability centrally or use a tagged architecture.

---

**(b) [7 marks] Describe RBAC. Explain with an example how Role Hierarchy and Separation of Duty are implemented.**

**Model Solution:**

**RBAC Definition (1.5 marks):**
**Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** is a policy-neutral access control model in which:
- **Users (U)** are assigned to **Roles (R)** via `UA ⊆ U × R`.
- **Permissions (P)** are assigned to **Roles** via `PA ⊆ P × R`.
- **Sessions (S):** A user may activate a *subset* of their roles per session.

RBAC is standardized as **ANSI INCITS 359** and underpins modern systems (AWS IAM, Windows Active Directory).

**Role Hierarchy (2.5 marks):**
- A *partial order* `≥` on roles such that `r1 ≥ r2` means `r1` inherits all permissions of `r2`.
- **Example:** `Senior_Engineer ≥ Junior_Engineer`. If `Junior_Engineer` has permission `view_logs`, then `Senior_Engineer` automatically inherits it.
- Reduces administrative overhead: permissions are defined *once* at the base role and inherited upward.

**Separation of Duty (SoD) (2.5 marks):**
- **Static SoD (SSD):** Declared at role assignment time — no user may hold two *mutually exclusive* roles. *Example:* A user cannot be both `Cashier` and `Auditor` simultaneously (the auditor verifies the cashier's work).
- **Dynamic SoD (DSD):** Two conflicting roles may be assigned to the same user, but not *both activated* in a single session. *Example:* A user may hold `Initiator` and `Approver` roles overall, but cannot approve a request they themselves initiated.
- Enforces the **principle of least privilege** and prevents fraud.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
RBAC provides scalability, administrative simplicity, and natural alignment with organizational structure — making it the de facto standard for enterprise and cloud security.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 1–3 marks in KTU ESE papers:**
> 1. **Confusing "trusted" with "secure"** — a trusted system is *verified*; a secure system is merely *claimed*.
> 2. **Mixing up BLP and Biba rules** — BLP is *confidentiality* (No Read Up, No Write Down); Biba is *integrity* (No Read Down, No Write Up). Reversing them is a *direct 7-mark loss*.
> 3. **Forgetting the third Reference Monitor property** — students often list only "Complete Mediation" and "Tamper-Proofness", missing the crucial **Verifiability** (1 mark lost).
> 4. **Writing "MAC = Multi-level Access Control"** — wrong. MAC stands for **Mandatory Access Control**.
> 5. **Not stating "Tranquility"** in BLP answers — a complete BLP answer must mention tranquility; without it, the *-property is subvertible.
> 6. **Conflating ACL and Capability** — ACL is *per-object* (column view); Capability is *per-subject* (row view). Reversing these in the long-answer diagram loses 2 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting categories (C) in security levels** — the security level is a *pair* `(λ, C)`, not just the classification. KTU specifically tests this.
> 8. **In RBAC, forgetting "Sessions"** — a complete RBAC definition must include user-role assignment (`UA`), permission-role assignment (`PA`), and *session activation* of role subsets.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Quick-Fire Recall — KTU Module 4: Trusted Systems & Data Access Control**

- **Trusted System** = *verified* (not just claimed) enforcement of a security policy.
- **Reference Monitor** = 3 properties: *Complete Mediation + Tamper-Proof + Verifiable*.
- **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** = totality of protection mechanisms; includes RM, security kernel, audit subsystem, authentication.
- **Access Control Matrix (ACM)** = Lampson 1971; rows = subjects, columns = objects.
- **ACL** = per-object view; **Capability** = per-subject view.
- **DAC** = owner's discretion; **MAC** = system-enforced; **RBAC** = role-based.
- **Bell-LaPadula (BLP)**: **No Read Up** (ss-property) + **No Write Down** (*-property) → **confidentiality**.
- **Biba Model**: **No Read Down** + **No Write Up** → **integrity**.
- **Basic Security Theorem (BSC)**: secure initial state + secure-preserving transitions ⇒ system always secure.
- **Tranquility**: labels must not change to subvert the *-property.
- **RBAC components**: `U, R, P, S, UA, PA`; standard = **ANSI INCITS 359**.
- **Role Hierarchy**: `r1 ≥ r2` ⇒ `r1` inherits `r2`'s permissions.
- **Separation of Duty**: *Static* (no user can hold both roles) vs *Dynamic* (cannot activate both in same session).
- **TCSEC (Orange Book) trust classes**: `A1 > B3 > B2 > B1 > C2 > C1 > D` (D = no security).
- **Common Criteria EAL**: `EAL1 → EAL7` (EAL7 = highest assurance, formal verification).
- **Take-Grant Model**: graph-based, uses *tg-connectivity* to reason about right propagation.
- **TCB Layered Architecture**: Hardware Root → Security Subsystems → Trusted OS → Applications.
- **Trojan Horse Attack**: bypasses DAC; mitigated (not eliminated) by BLP's *-property.
- **Covert Channels**: timing, storage — side-channel leaks not addressed by BLP.
- **Principle of Least Privilege**: assign minimum permissions required for a task.
- **Authentication ≠ Authorization ≠ Accountability** — all three are mandatory in a trusted system.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Concept of Trusted Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Concept of Trusted Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trusted System (KTU Board-Standard Definition):**
> A *Trusted System* is a computer system whose hardware, software, and operational procedures collectively enforce a specified **security policy** such that the system can be relied upon to process, store, and protect sensitive information against unauthorized disclosure, modification, or destruction.

The word **"trusted"** here does **not** mean "trustworthy in absolute terms." In KTU/NCSC formal terminology, a system is *trusted* when it has been **subjected to rigorous analysis and testing**, and its enforcement mechanisms have been **formally verified** to satisfy a defined set of security requirements. The *trust* is placed in the **integrity of the system itself**, not in its components individually.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault

Imagine a bank vault. The vault is called "trusted" not because it is *guaranteed* impenetrable, but because:
1. It was designed by certified engineers using mathematical proofs of strength.
2. It has been **independently inspected and rated**.
3. Every interaction (open/close, deposit/withdraw) is **logged and audited**.
4. There is one **central choke point** (the vault door with a single lock) through which every access must pass.

A *Trusted System* in computing is exactly this — a system where **every access to sensitive data** must pass through a single, verifiable, auditable choke point (the **Reference Monitor**), and the entire protective envelope (the **Trusted Computing Base**) has been certified.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction KTU Examiners Love:**
> - **Trusted** = Subjected to evaluation & proven to enforce a policy.
> - **Trustworthy** = Believed to be reliable (subjective).
> - **Secure** = Free from danger (an *outcome*, not a *property* of the system itself).

### 1.3 Core Constituent Sub-Systems

A Trusted System is built from three layered concepts, each of which is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**:

| # | Sub-System | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Security Policy** | The formal statement of *what* is being protected and *from whom*. |
| 2 | **Security Model** | The mathematical/abstract representation that proves the policy is enforceable. |
| 3 | **Security Mechanism** | The actual hardware/software that implements the model (firewalls, ACLs, encryption). |

### 1.4 Trust Levels at a Glance (TCSEC Orange Book)

The U.S. **Trusted Computer System Evaluation Criteria (TCSEC)**, popularly called the **"Orange Book"** (DoD 5200.28-STD, **1983** — *not 1985*), classifies systems into four major divisions: **A, B, C, D**, with **A** being the most trusted and **D** the least.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic — "Dogs Can Bite Bad Animals"**
> Order from **least → most trusted**: **D → C1 → C2 → B1 → B2 → B3 → A1**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of a Trusted System

A trusted system is decomposed logically into the following structural elements:

**Pillar 1 — Trusted Computing Base (TCB)**

> [!NOTE]
> **TCB Definition (NCSC / KTU Standard):**
> The **Trusted Computing Base** of a system is the totality of protection mechanisms within a computer system responsible for enforcing security policy. It is the *only* part of the system we need to trust.

The TCB typically consists of:
- **Hardware** (CPU privilege levels, MMU, TPM chip).
- **Firmware** (BIOS/UEFI, microcode).
- **Operating System Kernel** (specifically the security kernel portion).
- **Trusted Software** (authentication modules, audit daemons).
- **Reference Monitor** (the heart of the TCB).

**Pillar 2 — Reference Monitor**

The Reference Monitor is the **controlling element of the TCB**. It mediates every access between a *subject* (active entity: user, process) and an *object* (passive entity: file, memory segment, I/O device).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Mandatory Properties of a Reference Monitor (KTU Board-Favourite):**
> 1. **Complete Mediation (Tamper-Proof)** — *Every* access is checked; **no bypass path** exists.
> 2. **Isolation (Verifiable)** — The monitor itself is **small enough to be subjected to complete analysis and testing**.
> 3. **Tamper-Proof (Always Invoked)** — The monitor **cannot be modified or circumvented** by any subject, including those with root privileges.

**Pillar 3 — Security Kernel**

The Security Kernel is the **hardware + firmware + software implementation of the Reference Monitor**. It is the actual running code. If the Reference Monitor is the *blueprint*, the Security Kernel is the *building*.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why do we need complete mediation?** Because a single unchecked path becomes the *Achilles' heel* — an attacker who finds it bypasses everything.
- **Why must the monitor be small?** So that exhaustive testing, code review, and formal verification are feasible. A 10-million-line OS cannot be fully audited.
- **Why is tamper-proofing mandatory?** Because if an attacker can disable the monitor, the entire security posture collapses regardless of how well it was designed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All notation below follows KTU / NCSC-Rainbow-Series convention.

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | KTU Board Format |
|---|---|---|
| $S$ | Set of subjects (active entities) | Capital letter, italic |
| $O$ | Set of objects (passive entities) | Capital letter, italic |
| $L$ | Lattice of security levels | Partially ordered set $(L, \leq)$ |
| $\lambda(x)$ | Security level (clearance for subject / classification for object) | Greek lambda |
| $\rho(s, o)$ | Access decision function: *grant* $\vert$ *deny* | Boolean / Tri-state |
| $M = (S, O, L, \rho)$ | Security Model (4-tuple) | — |
| $\text{TCB}$ | Trusted Computing Base | — |
| $\text{RM}$ | Reference Monitor | — |
| $\text{SK}$ | Security Kernel | — |

**The Dominance Relation in a Lattice:**

Given two security levels $L_i, L_j \in L$, the dominance relation $\geq$ is:

$$L_i \geq L_j \iff \text{every component of } L_i \text{ is} \geq \text{the corresponding component of } L_j$$

The **least upper bound (join / lub)** and **greatest lower bound (meet / glb)** are:

$$L_{\text{lub}} = L_i \sqcup L_j, \qquad L_{\text{glb}} = L_i \sqcap L_j$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Lattice?** A lattice guarantees that for *any two* security levels, a unique least upper bound and a unique greatest lower bound exist. This makes the security model mathematically complete — every access decision has a definite answer.

### 2.4 TCSEC (Orange Book) Trust Levels — Exam-Ready Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vertical separator symbol is rendered as `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Class | Trust Level | Key Properties | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **D** | Minimal Protection | No security enforcement; failed evaluation | Early personal computers, MS-DOS |
| **C1** | Discretionary Security Protection | DAC via owner-controlled ACLs; user identification | Workstations, departmental LANs |
| **C2** | Controlled Access Protection | + Object reuse, full auditing, granular ACLs | Commercial OS (Windows NT, modern Linux with SELinux) |
| **B1** | Labeled Security Protection | + MAC begins, security labels on subjects/objects, informal policy model | Government offices |
| **B2** | Structured Protection | + Formal security policy model, TCB structuring, covert-channel analysis | Military message systems |
| **B3** | Security Domains | + Trusted recovery, minimal TCB, full reference-validation mechanism | High-value military DBs |
| **A1** | Verified Design | + Formal verification of TCB, configuration management | Ultra-secure intelligence systems |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Sector**: C2-level controls underpin ATM switching networks.
- **Defense & Intelligence**: B2 and above used in command-and-control systems.
- **Cloud Computing**: Modern Trusted Platform Module (TPM) chips implement B1-equivalent hardware roots of trust.
- **Smart Cards / HSMs**: Hardware Security Modules are essentially **A1-equivalent** security kernels in compact form.
- **Operating Systems**: SELinux, Trusted Solaris, and Apple iOS sandboxing implement **B1–B3** style MAC.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Foundations of a Trusted System

The standard KTU treatment of trusted systems rests on the **Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model (1973, MIT)**. We now derive the access decision rules from first principles.

#### 3.1.1 The Four-Tuple Security Model

A security model is formally defined as:

$$M = (S, O, L, \rho)$$

where:
- $S$ is the set of subjects (e.g., user processes).
- $O$ is the set of objects (e.g., files, sockets).
- $L$ is a **lattice** of security levels with partial order $\leq$.
- $\rho : S \times O \rightarrow \{\text{grant}, \text{deny}\}$ is the access decision function.

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the *No-Read-Up (NRU) / Simple Security Property*

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation traces the reasoning that leads to the *Simple Security Condition* — a guaranteed KTU 14-mark sub-question.

**Step 1 — State the Assumption.**

Every subject $s \in S$ has a clearance $\lambda(s) \in L$.
Every object $o \in O$ has a classification $\lambda(o) \in L$.

**Step 2 — Define the Threat.**

If a subject $s$ with clearance $\lambda(s)$ is permitted to *read* an object $o$ with classification $\lambda(o)$, then information flows from $o$ to $s$. For confidentiality to hold, the receiving subject must be at least as trusted as the source object:

$$\text{Read-Allowed} \implies \lambda(s) \geq \lambda(o)$$

**Step 3 — Negate the Condition (the Policy Rule).**

A subject $s$ is **denied read access** to object $o$ if and only if:

$$\lambda(s) < \lambda(o)$$

Equivalently, the **Simple Security Property** (No-Read-Up) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho(s, o, \text{read}) = \text{grant} &\iff \lambda(s) \geq \lambda(o) \\
\rho(s, o, \text{read}) = \text{deny} &\iff \lambda(s) < \lambda(o)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Validate against the Lattice Axioms.**

Because $(L, \leq)$ is a partial order, the relation $\geq$ is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive. Hence for any given $(s, o)$, the comparison yields exactly one of three outcomes:
- $\lambda(s) > \lambda(o)$ — *grant*.
- $\lambda(s) = \lambda(o)$ — *grant*.
- $\lambda(s) < \lambda(o)$ — *deny*.

The decision is therefore **deterministic**, satisfying the *complete mediation* requirement of the Reference Monitor.

#### 3.1.3 Derivation of the *No-Write-Down (NWD) / *-Property*

**Step 1 — Prevent Trojan Leaks.**

Even if a subject legitimately reads high-classified data, allowing it to write that information into a low-classified object would constitute an *illegal downward information flow*. We therefore impose:

$$\text{Write-Allowed} \implies \lambda(o) \geq \lambda(s)$$

**Step 2 — Formal *-Property.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho(s, o, \text{write}) = \text{grant} &\iff \lambda(o) \geq \lambda(s) \\
\rho(s, o, \text{write}) = \text{deny} &\iff \lambda(o) < \lambda(s)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Effect (NRU + NWD):** A subject may only read at its own level (or above) and write at its own level (or below). Combined, this means a subject can **only read-and-write at its own clearance level** unless *tranquility* is invoked.

#### 3.1.4 The Tranquility Principle

The Tranquility Principle states that **security levels of subjects and objects do not change during normal operation**.

We model this as a discrete-time sequence. Let $t \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ index system states. The state at time $t$ is:

$$\Sigma(t) = (\lambda_t(s), \lambda_t(o))_{s \in S,\, o \in O}$$

Tranquility requires that there exists a function $\tau(t)$ such that:

$$\tau(t) = \begin{cases} \text{stable} & \text{if } \lambda_t(x) = \lambda_{t-1}(x), \; \forall x \in S \cup O \\ \text{transition} & \text{otherwise (subject to "no compromise of security")} \end{cases}$$

The *Strong Tranquility* mandates that **no change can ever violate the security policy**:

$$\Sigma(t+1) \models \text{Policy} \quad \Longleftarrow \quad \Sigma(t) \models \text{Policy}$$

The *Weak Tranquility* permits change as long as **no subject reads or writes data at the new classification that it could not have at the old classification**.

#### 3.1.5 Covert Channel Capacity (Important B2-Level Concept)

A covert channel is an **unintended communication path** that bypasses the Reference Monitor. Its capacity is bounded by the **maximum rate of information transfer** $C_{\max}$:

$$C_{\max} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\log_2 \mid \mathcal{M}_T \mid}{T}$$

where $\mathcal{M}_T$ is the set of distinguishable messages transmittable in time $T$.

For a B2-rated system, the KTU/Orange Book requires:

$$C_{\max} \leq C_{\text{tolerance}}$$

where $C_{\text{tolerance}}$ is typically **1 bit per second** for system-high classifications.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Access Requested | $\lambda(s)$ vs $\lambda(o)$ | BLP Verdict | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Read | $\lambda(s) \geq \lambda(o)$ | **GRANT** (Simple Security / NRU) | Subject is cleared to know the object's content. |
| Read | $\lambda(s) < \lambda(o)$ | **DENY** (NRU violation) | Would leak classified data upward. |
| Write | $\lambda(o) \geq \lambda(s)$ | **GRANT** (*-Property / NWD) | Information stays at or above subject's clearance. |
| Write | $\lambda(o) < \lambda(s)$ | **DENY** (NWD violation) | Trojan horse could dump high data into low object. |
| Append | $\lambda(o) > \lambda(s)$ | **GRANT** (special case) | Limited upward write — no read-back possible. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Append-Only Exception (KTU Favourite Trick Question):** Some trusted systems relax the *-Property to allow *append* when $\lambda(o) > \lambda(s)$. This is used in **trusted downgrader** subsystems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Trusted System

The diagram below shows how the **Reference Monitor** sits at the choke point between **subjects** and **objects**, supported by the **TCB**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ExternalWorld["External World"]
        U1[User A - Clearance SECRET]
        U2[User B - Clearance CONFIDENTIAL]
    end

    subgraph TCBzone["TRUSTED COMPUTING BASE TCB"]
        direction TB
        RM[Reference Monitor]
        SK[Security Kernel Implementation]
        AUDIT[Audit Log Subsystem]
        AUTH[Authentication Subsystem]
        DAC[Mandatory Access Control Engine]
    end

    subgraph ObjectStore["Protected Object Store"]
        O1[File1 - Classification TOP SECRET]
        O2[File2 - Classification SECRET]
        O3[File3 - Classification CONFIDENTIAL]
    end

    U1 -->|access request| RM
    U2 -->|access request| RM
    RM --> AUTH
    AUTH --> RM
    RM --> DAC
    DAC --> SK
    RM -->|grant or deny| ObjectStore
    RM --> AUDIT
    SK --> ObjectStore
    AUDIT --> AUDIT
```

### 4.2 Reference Monitor Placement — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SubjectSide["Subject Side"]
        S1[Process P1]
        S2[Process P2]
        S3[Process P3]
    end

    subgraph RM_Block["Reference Monitor Boundary"]
        direction TB
        RM1[Validate Subject Identity]
        RM2[Check Clearance vs Classification]
        RM3[Apply MAC and DAC Rules]
        RM4[Audit Decision]
    end

    subgraph ObjectSide["Object Side"]
        Obj1[Memory Segment]
        Obj2[File Descriptor]
        Obj3[I O Device]
    end

    S1 --> RM1
    S2 --> RM1
    S3 --> RM1
    RM1 --> RM2 --> RM3 --> RM4
    RM4 -->|GRANT| Obj1
    RM4 -->|GRANT| Obj2
    RM4 -->|GRANT| Obj3
    RM4 -->|DENY path closed| SubjectSide
```

### 4.3 TCSEC Trust-Class Hierarchy (Diamond View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D[Class D - Minimal Protection]
    C1[Class C1 - Discretionary]
    C2[Class C2 - Controlled Access]
    B1[Class B1 - Labeled Security]
    B2[Class B2 - Structured Protection]
    B3[Class B3 - Security Domains]
    A1[Class A1 - Verified Design]

    D --> C1 --> C2 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each upward arrow represents **strictly increasing** mandatory controls: every higher class must **include all requirements of every lower class plus its own additions**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define a Trusted System. How is it different from a Secure System?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Trusted System** is a system in which sufficient hardware and software assurance has been achieved such that the system can be relied upon to enforce a stated security policy (NCSC Rainbow Series definition).

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Trusted System | Secure System |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Has been **evaluated & verified** to enforce a policy. | **Outcome state** — free from risk. |
| Dependency | Depends on the *enforcement mechanism*. | Depends on *environment and threats*. |
| Attainability | Achievable via certification (TCSEC, Common Criteria). | Often only **theoretical ideal**. |

A system can be **trusted but not secure** (certified under a weak policy) and **secure but not trusted** (using unverified but strong controls). **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **What is a Reference Monitor? List its three mandatory properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Reference Monitor** is the abstract machine that mediates all access between subjects and objects, enforcing the system's security policy. It is implemented by the **Security Kernel**.

**Three Mandatory Properties:**
1. **Complete Mediation** — Every access must pass through the monitor; no bypass path.
2. **Tamper-Proof** — The monitor cannot be modified by any subject.
3. **Verifiable** — The monitor is small enough to be fully analysed and tested. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **IMPORTANT:** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that within each module, students answer **one full 14-mark question** with an internal choice between **Q(A)** and **Q(B)**. Both choices carry equal marks and have sub-parts **(a)** for 7 marks and **(b)** for 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** with a neat diagram. List its components and explain why TCB minimization is critical for a trusted system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** is the totality of protection mechanisms — hardware, firmware, and software — within a computer system that are responsible for enforcing the security policy. It is the **only** part of the system that needs to be trusted because a compromise of any element inside the TCB compromises the entire system's security.

**Components of TCB (3 marks):**
- **Hardware** — CPU with at least two privilege states (user/supervisor), Memory Management Unit (MMU), TPM.
- **Firmware** — BIOS/UEFI initialization and low-level drivers.
- **Operating System Security Kernel** — implements Reference Monitor.
- **Trusted Utilities** — login authenticators, audit daemons.
- **Reference Monitor** — abstract policy enforcer.
- **Audit Trails** — non-modifiable logs.

**Why TCB Minimization is Critical (2 marks):**
The smaller the TCB, the easier it is to:
- Perform **exhaustive code review**,
- Conduct **formal verification**,
- Reduce the **attack surface**.

A large TCB (like a full Linux kernel ~30M LOC) is impractical to verify fully, hence modern approaches use **microkernels** or **security-focused separation kernels** to shrink the TCB.

**(b)** Describe the **Reference Monitor Concept** and its three mandatory properties. How does a Security Kernel implement it? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):**
The **Reference Monitor** is an access-control concept in which every access by a subject to an object is mediated by a single, central, policy-enforcing mechanism. It is the *abstract design*; the *Security Kernel* is its *concrete implementation* in software and hardware.

**Three Mandatory Properties (3 marks):**
1. **Complete Mediation** — Every reference by every subject to every object is validated.
2. **Tamper-Proof** — The Reference Monitor itself is protected from unauthorized modification.
3. **Verifiable** — The monitor is small enough to be subjected to complete analysis and testing.

**Security Kernel Implementation (2 marks):**
The Security Kernel is the OS code that runs at the highest privilege level and contains:
- The **access-control decision module**,
- The **audit generator**,
- The **authentication gateway**,
- The **trusted path** interface (secure terminal).

It is the only code that runs in supervisor mode; everything else operates in user mode and is mediated by the kernel.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TCSEC (Orange Book) classification of trusted systems**. Compare **C2** and **B2** levels in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(Use the diamond hierarchy from Section 4.3.)*

**Explanation (2 marks):**
The **Trusted Computer System Evaluation Criteria (TCSEC)**, known as the **Orange Book** (DoD 5200.28-STD, 1983), evaluates systems across four divisions: **A, B, C, D**, with sub-classes **C1, C2, B1, B2, B3, A1**, in increasing order of trust.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | C2 — Controlled Access | B2 — Structured Protection |
|---|---|---|
| **Access Control** | DAC with granular ACLs | DAC + MAC with formal policy model |
| **Labeling** | Not required | Mandatory security labels on all subjects/objects |
| **Audit** | Full auditing of security-relevant events | + Audit trail protection |
| **Covert Channels** | Not addressed | Must be analysed and bounded |
| **TCB Structure** | May be monolithic | Must be modular with well-defined interfaces |
| **Trusted Path** | Not required | Required for user-to-TCB communication |
| **Example Use** | Commercial OS (Windows NT base) | Military-grade message switches |

**(b)** Explain the **Bell-LaPadula Model** with its three core properties: *Simple Security Property (ss-property)*, ***-Property*, and *Tranquility Principle*. Provide one limitation of the model. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Model Overview (1 mark):**
The **Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model**, formulated by David Bell and Leonard LaPadula in **1973** for MIT, is the foundational formal security model for enforcing **confidentiality** in trusted systems.

**Property 1 — Simple Security Property (No-Read-Up) (2 marks):**
A subject $s$ may read object $o$ only if the clearance of $s$ dominates the classification of $o$:

$$\rho(s, o, \text{read}) = \text{grant} \iff \lambda(s) \geq \lambda(o)$$

**Property 2 — *-Property (No-Write-Down) (2 marks):**
A subject $s$ may write to object $o$ only if the classification of $o$ dominates the clearance of $s$:

$$\rho(s, o, \text{write}) = \text{grant} \iff \lambda(o) \geq \lambda(s)$$

This prevents a Trojan horse from leaking high-classification data to a low-classification object.

**Property 3 — Tranquility Principle (1 mark):**
Security labels of subjects and objects do not change during normal operation. The **Strong Tranquility** forbids any change that would violate the security policy.

**Limitation (1 mark):**
BLP addresses only **confidentiality**, not **integrity**. It does not prevent unauthorized modification. The **Biba Model** was later proposed as its integrity counterpart.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Trusted with Trustworthy** — Costing 1–2 marks in definition questions.
> 2. **Listing only 2 Reference Monitor properties** instead of all 3 — Examiner awards only partial marks (1.5/3).
> 3. **Forgetting the diagram** in Part-B sub-questions that explicitly ask for one — Up to **2 marks deducted**.
> 4. **Mixing up Biba and Bell-LaPadula** — BLP = confidentiality; Biba = integrity. Confusing them signals weak understanding.
> 5. **Skipping the Tranquility Principle** in BLP explanations — frequently a compulsory 1–2 mark component.
> 6. **Writing "Orange Book, 1985"** — The actual publication year is **1983** (DoD 5200.28-STD). Examiners do check.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Trusted System** = a system that has been **evaluated and certified** to enforce a security policy.
- **Trusted ≠ Secure ≠ Trustworthy** — these are distinct concepts.
- **Three Pillars:** Security Policy → Security Model → Security Mechanism.
- **TCB** = entire envelope of trusted hardware + software.
- **Reference Monitor** = abstract choke point for all subject-to-object access.
- **Three Reference Monitor Properties** = **Complete Mediation**, **Tamper-Proof**, **Verifiable**.
- **Security Kernel** = concrete implementation of Reference Monitor.
- **Bell-LaPadula (1973)** = confidentiality model with **ss-property (NRU)** + ***-property (NWD)** + **Tranquility**.
- **NRU**: $\lambda(s) \geq \lambda(o)$ for read; **NWD**: $\lambda(o) \geq \lambda(s)$ for write.
- **TCSEC Orange Book (1983)** = 4 divisions **A, B, C, D** with 7 classes total.
- **Mnemonic**: "**Dogs Can Bite Bad Animals**" = D → C1 → C2 → B1 → B2 → B3 → A1 (least → most trusted).
- **Biba Model** = integrity counterpart to BLP (do **not** confuse).
- **Covert Channels** must be analysed at **B2 and above**.
- **Microkernel architectures** minimize TCB for higher assurance.
- **TPM chip** = modern hardware root-of-trust equivalent of B1-level trust.
- **Strong Tranquility** > **Weak Tranquility** in strictness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trojan Horse Defense
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Trojan Horse Defense ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Trojan Horse** (or simply *Trojan*) is a class of malicious software (malware) that masquerades as a legitimate, benign, or useful program in order to deceive the end-user into installing, executing, or propagating it. Unlike viruses or worms, a Trojan **cannot self-replicate**; it relies entirely on social engineering and user interaction for its propagation vector. In the context of **Network Security Protocols**, Trojan Horse Defense refers to the comprehensive, multi-layered set of preventive, detective, and corrective security controls designed to protect host systems, network endpoints, and user data confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the **CIA Triad**) from Trojan-based intrusion vectors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEADT863 - Module 4):** > Trojan Horse Defense constitutes a critical sub-topic under *Program Threats and System Security*. Examiners frequently test: > - The differentiation between Trojans, Viruses, and Worms > - The taxonomy of Trojan types > - The layered defense architecture (prevention → detection → response) > - The role of Host-Based Intrusion Detection Systems (HIDS) and Network-Based Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Wooden Horse of Troy" Analogy** > > Imagine a heavily fortified city (your computer system) protected by massive walls (your firewall and OS security). The enemy cannot break in by force. Instead, they gift the city a beautiful, large wooden horse (the Trojan program). The citizens, believing it is a peace offering (a "free" game, a "useful" utility, or a "mandatory" update), drag the horse inside the city gates. At night, hidden enemy soldiers (the malicious payload — backdoors, keyloggers, rootkits) emerge from inside the horse and open the gates for the invading army. > > **Key insight for students:** The Trojan exploits the *human* (the user) rather than the *software* (the code). This is why technical defenses alone are insufficient; **user awareness** is the most critical defense layer. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Engineering Constants In the engineering analysis of Trojan Defense Systems, the following quantitative metrics are used: - **Mean Time To Detection (MTTD):** Average time between infection and detection. Industry benchmark: **< 24 hours** for enterprise-grade EDR solutions. - **Mean Time To Response (MTTR):** Average time to isolate, contain, and remediate. - **True Positive Rate (TPR) / Recall:** $\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — proportion of actual Trojans correctly identified. - **False Positive Rate (FPR):** $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ — proportion of legitimate files incorrectly flagged. - **Detection Latency:** Typically measured in **seconds** for signature-based, **hours** for heuristic, **days** for behavior-based. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Taxonomy of Trojan Horses Understanding the *types* of Trojans is essential for designing targeted defenses. Each category exploits a different attack surface. | **Trojan Type** | **Primary Payload** | **Target Asset** | **Defense Counter-Mechanism** | |-----------------|---------------------|------------------|-------------------------------| | **Backdoor Trojan** | Opens covert remote access (port) | Confidentiality, Control | Firewall egress filtering, HIDS | | **Keylogger Trojan** | Captures keystrokes | Confidentiality (credentials) | Virtual keyboards, OTP, HIDS | | **Rootkit Trojan** | Hides malicious processes at kernel level | Integrity, Stealth | Kernel-level integrity checkers | | **Downloader/Dropper** | Fetches additional malware | Multi-stage infection | Sandboxing, URL filtering | | **Banking Trojan** | Intercepts browser/session data | Financial assets | MFA, transaction signing | | **RAT (Remote Access Trojan)** | Full GUI control of victim host | Total system compromise | Network segmentation, EDR | | **DDoS Trojan** | Recruits host into botnet | Availability | Rate limiting, anomaly detection | | **FakeAV / Scareware** | Tricks user into paying for fake fix | Financial fraud | User education, allow-listing | ### 2.2 The Defense-in-Depth Architecture Trojan defense is *not* a single control but a **multi-layered onion model**. The underlying principle is: *If one layer fails, the next layer must still prevent compromise.* The five canonical layers (mapped to the **OSI Security Architecture — X.805**): 1. **Layer 1 — User Awareness & Policy** - Principle of least privilege - Security awareness training - Application allow-listing policies 2. **Layer 2 — Perimeter / Network Defenses** - Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFW) - Network Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (NIDS/NIPS) - Email and web gateways with sandboxing 3. **Layer 3 — Host-Based Defenses** - Host Intrusion Detection System (HIDS) - Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) - Application control / Software Restriction Policies 4. **Layer 4 — Integrity Verification** - File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) - Code signing and digital signature verification - Trusted Platform Module (TPM) and Secure Boot 5. **Layer 5 — Recovery & Forensics** - Regular offline backups - Incident response playbooks - Memory forensics and rootkit scanners ### 2.3 Detection Methodologies (Core Theory) **A. Signature-Based Detection** - Operates on a database of known Trojan hashes/patterns. - Fast ($O(n)$ lookup), but **zero-day** ineffective. - Relies on regularly updated **AV signature databases**. **B. Heuristic / Rule-Based Detection** - Inspects file *structure* (entropy of packed sections, suspicious API call sequences, unusual section names). - Detects **unknown** Trojans via rules like: *If a `.exe` performs `CreateRemoteThread` and `WriteProcessMemory` together, flag as suspicious.* **C. Behavioral / Anomaly-Based Detection** - Establishes a baseline of normal system behavior (CPU, network calls, registry writes). - Flags deviations using statistical models. - Effective against **polymorphic** and **metamorphic** Trojans. **D. Sandbox-Based Detection** - Executes suspect file in an isolated virtual environment. - Monitors side-effects (file creation, registry changes, network traffic). - Resource-intensive; vulnerable to **sandbox-evasion** Trojans. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table contains the verifiable mathematical formulations for KTU numerical/analytical problems on Trojan Defense.** | **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Variables & Units** | **Engineering Application** | |-------------|--------------------------|------------------------|-----------------------------| | True Positive Rate (Sensitivity) | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $TP$ = True Positives, $FN$ = False Negatives (count) | Measures detector coverage of real Trojans | | False Positive Rate (Fall-out) | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $FP$ = False Positives, $TN$ = True Negatives (count) | Measures false alarm burden on SOC analysts | | Precision (Positive Predictive Value) | $\text{PPV} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Same as above | Trustworthiness of an alert | | F1-Score (Harmonic Mean) | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{PPV} \cdot \text{TPR}}{\text{PPV} + \text{TPR}}$ | Dimensionless ratio $\in [0, 1]$ | Single-metric balance of precision vs recall | | Detection Confidence (Bayes) | $P(M \vert A) = \dfrac{P(A \vert M) \cdot P(M)}{P(A)}$ | $M$ = Malware, $A$ = Alert | Posterior probability of infection given alert | | Signature Lookup Time | $T_{\text{lookup}} = k \cdot n$ | $n$ = signatures, $k$ = per-hash time (s) | Performance sizing for AV engine | | Risk Severity Score | $R = P \times I$ | $P$ = Probability $\in [0,1]$, $I$ = Impact $\in [0,10]$ | NIST risk assessment methodology | | Information Entropy (Packed Files) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = byte probability | Detects encrypted/packed Trojans ($H \to 8$ for encrypted) | | Mean Time To Detection | $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{detect},i} - t_{\text{infect},i})$ | Time in **seconds** | SOC operational KPI | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Convention for Markdown:** In all subsequent derivations, the absolute value notation $\vert x \vert$ uses the LaTeX `\vert` command to prevent markdown table-breaking issues. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Trojan defense principles are not abstract — they underpin: - **Banking security:** Why your bank uses hardware tokens (defeats keyloggers). - **Critical Infrastructure (SCADA/ICS):** Where a Trojan like *Stuxnet* destroyed centrifuges. - **Cloud Computing:** Where EDR replaces traditional AV in virtualized environments. - **IoT Security:** Where resource constraints demand lightweight heuristic engines. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Bayesian Trojan Detection Confidence A network security analyst receives an alert from a Host-Based Intrusion Detection System. We wish to compute the **posterior probability** that a flagged file is truly a Trojan, given the alert was raised. This is a direct application of **Bayes' Theorem**, a classic KTU analytical question. **Given Data (Industrial-Realistic Scenario):** - Prior probability of a randomly downloaded executable being a Trojan: $P(M) = 0.05$ - Probability the AV engine raises an alert *given* the file IS a Trojan (Sensitivity): $P(A \vert M) = 0.98$ - Probability the AV engine raises an alert *given* the file is CLEAN (False Positive Rate): $P(A \vert \neg M) = 0.02$ **Goal:** Find $P(M \vert A)$ — the probability the file is a Trojan *given* that an alert was raised. **Step 1: State Bayes' Theorem.** $$ \begin{aligned} P(M \vert A) &= \frac{P(A \vert M) \cdot P(M)}{P(A)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Apply the Law of Total Probability to expand the denominator $P(A)$.** The event "alert raised" $A$ can occur in two mutually exclusive ways: the file is malicious (and detected), or the file is clean (and falsely flagged). Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} P(A) &= P(A \vert M) \cdot P(M) + P(A \vert \neg M) \cdot P(\neg M) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute the complement probability $P(\neg M)$.** Since the file is either a Trojan or clean, the probabilities must sum to unity: $$ \begin{aligned} P(\neg M) &= 1 - P(M) \\ P(\neg M) &= 1 - 0.05 \\ P(\neg M) &= 0.95 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Substitute numerical values into the denominator.** $$ \begin{aligned} P(A) &= (0.98)(0.05) + (0.02)(0.95) \\ P(A) &= 0.0490 + 0.0190 \\ P(A) &= 0.0680 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Substitute into the numerator of Bayes' Theorem.** $$ \begin{aligned} P(A \vert M) \cdot P(M) &= (0.98)(0.05) \\ &= 0.0490 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Compute the final posterior probability.** $$ \begin{aligned} P(M \vert A) &= \frac{0.0490}{0.0680} \\ P(M \vert A) &= 0.720588\ldots \\ P(M \vert A) &\approx 0.7206 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7: Engineering Interpretation.** Despite the AV engine having a 98% sensitivity, the **posterior probability** that an alert represents a real Trojan is only **≈ 72.06%**. This counterintuitive result arises from the **low base rate** ($P(M) = 0.05$) — a phenomenon known as the **Base Rate Fallacy**. This is precisely why SOC analysts must not blindly trust single alerts and must corroborate with secondary indicators (process behavior, network IOCs). ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Information Entropy of a Packed Trojan A common KTU question: *Show that a packed/encrypted Trojan has near-maximum entropy, distinguishing it from a normal executable.* **Definition:** Shannon Entropy for a byte sequence of length $N$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} H(X) &= -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p_i \log_2 p_i \end{aligned} $$ where $p_i$ is the empirical probability of byte value $i$ in the file. **Theoretical Maximum:** When all 256 byte values are **equiprobable** ($p_i = 1/256$ for all $i$): $$ \begin{aligned} H_{\max}(X) &= -\sum_{i=0}^{255} \left(\frac{1}{256}\right) \log_2\left(\frac{1}{256}\right) \\ &= -\sum_{i=0}^{255} \left(\frac{1}{256}\right) \cdot (-8) \\ &= 256 \cdot \left(\frac{8}{256}\right) \\ H_{\max}(X) &= 8 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Rule of Thumb (used by YARA rules and EDR heuristics):** - $H(X) \in [0, 5]$: Likely plain-text or structured binary (normal `.exe`, `.dll`). - $H(X) \in [5, 6]$: Possibly compressed (legitimate installers). - $H(X) \in [6, 7]$: Highly compressed or partially encrypted. - $H(X) \in [7, 8]$: **Encrypted or packed** — strong Trojan indicator. Threshold of **H > 7.2** is a common YARA rule for flagging. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Defense Layer Failure Cascade A frequently tested KTU analytical problem: *Given a multi-layer defense, what is the probability the system is compromised if each layer has an independent failure rate?* **Given:** $n$ independent defense layers, each with breach probability $p_i$. **Single-Layer Survival Probability:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_i &= 1 - p_i \end{aligned} $$ **Total System Survival Probability (Layers must ALL succeed):** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{\text{total}} &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} S_i = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i) \end{aligned} $$ **Total System Compromise Probability:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{compromise}} &= 1 - S_{\text{total}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i) \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical Example (3-Layer Defense):** - User Awareness: $p_1 = 0.30$ (30% click rate) - Email Gateway AV: $p_2 = 0.05$ - Host-Based EDR: $p_3 = 0.02$ $$ \begin{aligned} S_{\text{total}} &= (1 - 0.30)(1 - 0.05)(1 - 0.02) \\ &= (0.70)(0.95)(0.98) \\ &= 0.6517 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{compromise}} &= 1 - 0.6517 = 0.3483 \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Even with three layers, the compromise probability is ≈ 34.83%. This mathematically demonstrates *why* the human layer (awareness) is the weakest link and must be hardened via training and policy. ### 3.4 Risk Severity Scoring (NIST Methodology) A standard KTU problem format: *Calculate the risk score for a Banking Trojan targeting customer data.* **Formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= P \times I \end{aligned} $$ where $P$ is the Threat Probability (0.0 to 1.0) and $I$ is the Impact Score (0 to 10). **Mapping Table for $I$:** | **Impact Level** | **Score Range** | **Description** | |------------------|-----------------|-----------------| | Negligible | 0–1 | No business disruption | | Minor | 2–3 | Localized, recoverable | | Moderate | 4–5 | Significant, requires response | | Major | 6–7 | Severe, regulatory implications | | Catastrophic | 8–10 | Business-ending, mass PII leak | **Worked Example:** Banking Trojan with $P = 0.4$, $I = 8$: $$ \begin{aligned} R &= 0.4 \times 8 = 3.2 \end{aligned} $$ A score of 3.2 falls in the **Major Risk** band, mandating immediate mitigation (e.g., mandatory MFA rollout, network segmentation). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Trojan Attack Lifecycle (Kill Chain) The following Mermaid block depicts the standard **Cyber Kill Chain** as applied to a Trojan Horse intrusion, mapped to the **MITRE ATT&CK Framework** for adversarial tactics. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Reconnaissance
TA0043] --> B[Weaponization
TA0001] B --> C[Delivery
TA0002 Email Web USB] C --> D[Exploitation
TA0002 User Execution] D --> E[Installation
TA0003 Persistence] E --> F[Command and Control
TA0011 C2 Channel] F --> G[Actions on Objectives
TA0040 Exfiltration Damage] G --> H[Defense Evasion
TA0005 Cleanup] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style D fill:#DC143C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style E fill:#8B0000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style F fill:#4B0082,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style G fill:#00008B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style H fill:#2F4F4F,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Defense-in-Depth Architecture (Block Diagram) The following block diagram maps each Trojan defense layer to its corresponding security control and the threat it mitigates. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L1["Layer 1 Human Perimeter"] L1A[Security Awareness Training] L1B[Principle of Least Privilege] L1C[Email Phishing Drills] end subgraph L2["Layer 2 Network Perimeter"] L2A[Next Generation Firewall NGFW] L2B[Email Security Gateway with Sandbox] L2C[Network IDS NIDS] end subgraph L3["Layer 3 Host Endpoint"] L3A[Endpoint Detection Response EDR] L3B[Application Allow Listing] L3C[Host Based Firewall] end subgraph L4["Layer 4 System Integrity"] L4A[TPM and Secure Boot] L4B[Code Signing Verification] L4C[File Integrity Monitoring FIM] end subgraph L5["Layer 5 Recovery and Forensics"] L5A[Offline Encrypted Backups] L5B[Incident Response Playbook] L5C[Memory Forensics Toolkit] end L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 style L1 fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC style L2 fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF6600 style L3 fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009900 style L4 fill:#FFE6F3,stroke:#CC0066 style L5 fill:#F3E6FF,stroke:#6600CC ``` ### 4.3 Detection Decision Flowchart (Signature + Heuristic + Behavioral) The following diagram models the **decision tree** used by a modern AV/EDR engine when processing a suspect executable. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[New File Arrives on Host] --> B{Has Valid Code Signature?} B -->|Yes| C{Signature from Trusted CA?} B -->|No| D[Run Heuristic Engine] C -->|Yes| E[ALLOW Execution] C -->|No| D D --> F{Entropy H greater than 7.2?} F -->|Yes| G[Flag as Packed Suspect] F -->|No| H{Checks Suspicious API Sequence?} H -->|Yes| G H -->|No| I[Monitor Behavior in Sandbox] G --> J{Quarantine and Analyze} I --> K{Behavior Matches Trojan TTP?} K -->|Yes| L[BLOCK and Alert SOC] K -->|No| E J --> M{Manual Reversing Confirms?} M -->|Yes| L M -->|No| N[Add Heuristic and Release] style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style L fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff style G fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 style J fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > Students **commonly lose marks** in the following ways on Trojan Defense questions: > 1. **Conflating Trojans with Viruses/Worms.** A Trojan *does not self-replicate* — this distinction is worth full marks. > 2. **Listing controls without justification.** Merely naming "antivirus" earns partial credit; explaining *which Trojan type* it counters earns full marks. > 3. **Confusing signature-based with behavior-based detection.** Examiners test the operational mechanism, not just the name. > 4. **Forgetting the human layer.** Many students omit user awareness, which is the *primary* defense (the Base Rate Fallacy problem in 3.1 proves this). > 5. **Skipping the "Recovery" stage.** Defense is incomplete without backup and incident response — this is a common 2-mark loss. --- ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between a Trojan Horse, a Virus, and a Worm. Why is a Trojan considered the most "social-engineered" of the three?** **Model Answer:** | **Property** | **Trojan Horse** | **Virus** | **Worm** | |--------------|------------------|-----------|----------| | Self-replication | **No** | Yes (attaches to host file) | Yes (network spread) | | Propagation trigger | **User action required** | User opens infected file | Autonomous via network | | Primary attack vector | **Social engineering / deception** | File infection | Network vulnerabilities | | Carrier | Disguised as legitimate software | Host executable | Standalone network packet | A Trojan is the most social-engineered because **the entire infection chain depends on tricking the human user** to willingly execute the disguised payload, whereas viruses and worms exploit technical/code-level vulnerabilities. *(3 marks: 1 mark for replication distinction, 1 mark for propagation, 1 mark for social engineering justification)* #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Explain the concept of a "Backdoor Trojan." How does it violate the Confidentiality-Integrity-Availability (CIA) Triad?** **Model Answer:** A **Backdoor Trojan** is a malicious program that covertly installs a hidden remote-access channel (typically by opening a TCP/UDP port on a non-standard high number) on the victim's host, allowing the attacker to bypass normal authentication and gain unauthorized remote control. **Violations of the CIA Triad:** - **Confidentiality (C):** The attacker can read, copy, or exfiltrate sensitive files, passwords, and encryption keys without the user's knowledge. - **Integrity (I):** The attacker can modify system files, inject additional malware, alter database records, or tamper with logs. - **Availability (A):** The backdoor can be used to install ransomware, wipe disks, or recruit the host into a botnet for DDoS attacks, denying service to legitimate users. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for CIA mapping)* --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Cyber Kill Chain** as applied to a Trojan Horse attack. Identify at which stage each **defense-in-depth layer** is most effective. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **Signature-based**, **Heuristic**, and **Behavior-based** Trojan detection methods. State two advantages and two limitations of each. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** The **Cyber Kill Chain** (Lockheed Martin model) decomposes a Trojan attack into 7 stages: 1. **Reconnaissance:** Attacker gathers victim info (emails, OS version). 2. **Weaponization:** Pairs the Trojan payload with a legitimate-looking carrier (e.g., PDF, .exe). 3. **Delivery:** Sends via email, drive-by download, or USB. 4. **Exploitation:** User opens the file; payload executes. 5. **Installation:** Trojan establishes persistence (registry run-keys, scheduled tasks). 6. **Command & Control (C2):** Trojan beacons out to attacker infrastructure. 7. **Actions on Objectives:** Data theft, lateral movement, ransomware deployment. **Defense-in-Depth Mapping (Critical — most students skip this):** | **Kill Chain Stage** | **Most Effective Defense Layer** | |----------------------|----------------------------------| | Reconnaissance | Threat intelligence feeds | | Weaponization | Code signing, YARA rules on email gateway | | Delivery | Email security gateway with sandboxing (Layer 2) | | Exploitation | User awareness + application allow-listing (Layer 1) | | Installation | EDR with process telemetry (Layer 3) | | C2 | Egress firewall + NIDS (Layer 2) | | Actions on Objectives | Network segmentation + FIM (Layer 4) | **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block — examiners award full marks for a clear 7-stage labeled diagram with arrows.) *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for stage descriptions, 2 for defense mapping)* --- **Model Solution for (b):** | **Method** | **Mechanism** | **Advantages** | **Limitations** | |------------|---------------|----------------|-----------------| | **Signature-Based** | Hash / byte-pattern matching against known malware database | (i) Very fast O(n) lookup, (ii) Very low FPR on known samples | (i) Zero-day ineffective, (ii) Easily bypassed by polymorphism | | **Heuristic** | Rule-based static analysis (entropy, API imports, section names) | (i) Detects unknown variants, (ii) No execution required (safe) | (i) Higher FPR, (ii) Bypassed by code obfuscation | | **Behavior-Based** | Dynamic monitoring of syscalls, network, registry in sandbox/runtime | (i) Catches zero-days and fileless malware, (ii) Resilient to packing | (i) Resource intensive, (ii) Sandbox-evasion via sleep timers | *(7 marks: 1 mechanism + 2 advantages + 2 limitations per method = 5, plus 2 for the comparison summary statement)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(a)** What is the **Base Rate Fallacy** in the context of Trojan detection? An AV engine has a True Positive Rate of 99% and a False Positive Rate of 1%. If only 1 in 1000 downloaded executables is actually a Trojan, calculate the probability that a flagged file is truly malicious. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Defense-in-Depth** model for Trojan defense. Explain why each layer is necessary, using the principle of "weakest link in the chain." **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** The **Base Rate Fallacy** is the cognitive bias where humans (and analysts) over-rely on the *accuracy* of a single test while ignoring the *low prior probability* of the event being tested. In cybersecurity, even a highly accurate detector will produce mostly false positives when deployed against a large population where the actual infection rate is low. **Given:** - $P(M) = 1/1000 = 0.001$ (prior probability of Trojan) - $P(A \vert M) = 0.99$ (True Positive Rate / Sensitivity) - $P(A \vert \neg M) = 0.01$ (False Positive Rate) **Step 1: Compute the complement.** $$ \begin{aligned} P(\neg M) &= 1 - 0.001 = 0.999 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute $P(A)$ using the law of total probability.** $$ \begin{aligned} P(A) &= P(A \vert M) \cdot P(M) + P(A \vert \neg M) \cdot P(\neg M) \\ P(A) &= (0.99)(0.001) + (0.01)(0.999) \\ P(A) &= 0.00099 + 0.00999 \\ P(A) &= 0.01098 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Apply Bayes' Theorem.** $$ \begin{aligned} P(M \vert A) &= \frac{P(A \vert M) \cdot P(M)}{P(A)} \\ P(M \vert A) &= \frac{(0.99)(0.001)}{0.01098} \\ P(M \vert A) &= \frac{0.00099}{0.01098} \\ P(M \vert A) &\approx 0.0902 \end{aligned} $$ **Comment:** Despite a 99% accurate detector, the probability that an alert corresponds to a *real* Trojan is only **≈ 9.02%**. This demonstrates the **Base Rate Fallacy** and explains why SOCs are flooded with low-quality alerts. Mitigation requires **multi-indicator correlation** (combining AV, EDR, network IOCs) to raise the effective base rate. *(7 marks: 1 definition, 4 calculation, 2 comment)* --- **Model Solution for (b):** The **Defense-in-Depth** (DiD) model, formalized in NIST SP 800-53 and ISO 27001, is a security strategy that layers multiple controls so that the failure of any single control does not result in system compromise. **Why Each Layer is Necessary (Weakest Link Argument):** A system is only as secure as its **weakest** component. A single antivirus cannot stop: - A zero-day that bypasses signatures - A user who clicks a malicious link - A insider with stolen credentials - A network-borne lateral movement attack Therefore, *n* independent layers are required, each addressing a different attack vector. **The Five Layers with Necessity Justification:** 1. **Human Layer (Awareness + Training):** Defeats *social engineering* — addresses the fact that **95% of breaches involve human error** (Verizon DBIR). 2. **Network Layer (Firewall, NIDS, Email Gateway):** Filters *known malicious traffic* before it reaches the endpoint — reduces attack surface by **≈ 70%**. 3. **Host Layer (EDR, HIPS):** Provides *last-line behavioral defense* when perimeter is bypassed — uses ML to catch novel TTPs. 4. **Integrity Layer (TPM, Code Signing, FIM):** Ensures *boot-time and runtime trust* — defeats bootkits and rootkits that operate below the OS. 5. **Recovery Layer (Backups, IR Plan):** Provides *business continuity* when all prevention fails — reduces MTTR and data loss. **Mathematical Justification (from §3.3):** The total compromise probability is $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{compromise}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i) \end{aligned} $$ For $n=5$ layers each with $p_i = 0.10$: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{compromise}} &= 1 - (0.9)^5 = 1 - 0.59049 = 0.40951 \end{aligned} $$ A single layer ($n=1$) gives $P = 0.10$, but five independent layers give **$P \approx 40.95\%$ — still significant**, demonstrating the **asymptotic nature of defense** (it is impossible to reach $P=0$, only reduce risk). This is why **risk acceptance** is the final stage of any security framework. *(7 marks: 1.5 per layer × 5 = 7.5, rounded to 7 with 0.5 bonus for the mathematical justification)* --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Trojan Horse Defense** - **Definition:** A Trojan is a *non-self-replicating* malicious program disguised as legitimate software; it depends on **user deception** for execution. - **Key Distinction:** Trojan ≠ Virus (no replication, no host file attachment) ≠ Worm (no autonomous network spread). - **Top Trojan Types to Memorize:** Backdoor, Keylogger, Rootkit, RAT, Banking Trojan, Downloader/Dropper, FakeAV. - **Defense-in-Depth = 5 Layers:** Human → Network → Host → Integrity → Recovery. - **Detection Methods:** Signature (fast, known-only) / Heuristic (static rules, detects variants) / Behavioral (dynamic, catches zero-days) / Sandbox (isolated execution). - **Critical Formulas:** - $P(M \vert A) = \dfrac{P(A \vert M) \cdot P(M)}{P(A)}$ — Bayes' Theorem - $P_{\text{compromise}} = 1 - \prod (1 - p_i)$ — Defense-in-Depth - $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ — Entropy (packed Trojans have $H > 7.2$) - $R = P \times I$ — Risk score - **Base Rate Fallacy:** A 99% accurate detector against a 1-in-1000 base rate yields only ≈9% true positive alerts. **Always use multi-indicator correlation.** - **OSI Security Architecture:** DiD is formalized under **ITU-T X.805** (end-to-end network security). - **Forensic Tools to Know:** `chkrootkit`, `rkhunter`, `Volatility` (memory), `YARA` (signature), `Sysinternals Suite`, `Wireshark` (network). - **Modern Term:** "Trojan" in the 2024 KTU syllabus maps to the broader **MITRE ATT&CK** tactic `TA0002 — Initial Access` and `TA0003 — Persistence`. - **Exam Keyword Triggers:** "Defense-in-Depth," "Least Privilege," "Base Rate," "Kill Chain," "CIA Triad," "Heuristic vs. Signature."
